422.name: Long-chain 3-hydroxyacyl-CoA dehydrogenase deficiency
422.description: LCHADD deficiency, or long-chain 3-hydroxyacyl CoA dehydrogenase deficiency, is a disease that affects the metabolic process through which our body obtains energy from certain fats, especially during periods of fasting.
422.overview:
Long-chain 3-hydroxyacyl-CoA dehydrogenase deficiency (LCHADD) is an autosomal recessive inherited disorder of metabolism included in the group of monogenic mitochondrial disorders of long-chain fatty acid oxidation. LCHADD is a rare disease with a prevalence of approximately one case per 250,000 population.

In LCHADD, there is an impairment of mitochondrial beta-oxidation, which is a metabolic pathway involving several enzymes that generates acetyl-CoA and energy from fatty acids. The acetyl-CoA produced can enter the Krebs cycle and produce energy. When beta-oxidation is impaired, the accumulation of potentially toxic unoxidized acyl-CoA esters occurs and the acyl-CoA groups are transferred to other molecules forming acyl-conjugates that are unable to enter the usual biochemical pathways. Some of the acyl-conjugates, such as acylcarnitine, pass into the blood and are excreted in the urine. Screening that analyzes the acylcarnitine profile using a dried blood spot (Guthrie card) is a well-known and commonly used method that allows neonatal screening.
422.symptoms: Symptoms appear immediately in early infancy, during periods of fasting, and include lethargy, hypoglycemia or low blood sugar levels, weak muscle tone (hypotonia), febrile infections and gastroenteritis, liver problems and retinal abnormalities. Known irreversible long-term complications are peripheral neuropathy, retinopathy and heart disease.
422.prevention: The treatment of LCHADD is lifelong and is based on the avoidance of long periods of fasting and a diet free of long-chain fatty acids. In addition, L-carnitine supplementation and anaplerotic therapy, which is based on providing a substrate for the citric acid cycle that prevents deficient fatty acid oxidation, are used. One example is triheptanoin, a synthetic medium-chain triglyceride, which is being studied for long-chain fatty acid oxidation disorders.
422.technical_description:
The HADHA (hydroxyacyl-CoA dehydrogenase trifunctional multienzyme complex subunit alpha) gene produces the mitochondrial trifunctional enzyme involved in the last three steps of the mitochondrial beta-oxidation pathway. Mutations in HADHA result in LCHADD.

The c.1528G>C mutation (p.Glu510Gln; E510Q; rs137852769) has been observed in patients with LCHADD in homozygous state and in compound heterozygosity (one copy of the c.1528G>C variant with another copy of a different pathogenic variant in the same HADHA gene). c.1528 G>C is considered the main disease-causing mutation and significantly reduces the activity of the trifunctional enzyme (IJlst et al., 1996; Lotz-Havla et al., 2018). The frequency of the mutated allele in non-Finnish Europeans is 0.0016 and accounts for 91% of the defective alleles of the HADHA gene.

The c.180+3A>G variant (p.Thr37SerfsX6; rs781222705) has also been found in some LCHADD patients, mostly in compound heterozygosity (Liewluck et al., 2013). c.180+3A>G affects a HADHA mRNA cleavage and splice site and producing exon 3 deletion and a premature stop codon. The frequency of the mutated allele of c.180+3A>G in non-Finnish Europeans is 0.00023.

423.name: Pyruvate kinase deficiency
423.description: The enzyme pyruvate kinase or PK is an enzyme involved in glycolysis, a metabolic pathway in which energy is obtained from glucose, and whose deficiency can lead to alterations in red blood cells causing hemolytic anemia.
423.overview:
Pyruvate kinase deficiency (PKD) is a genetic blood disorder that causes chronic nonspherocytic hemolytic anemia and is the most common enzymatic defect of the glycolytic pathway. The glycolytic pathway is a metabolic process of generating energy from glucose.In red blood cells, glycolysis plays an important role in the maintenance of their structure and functions and it has been observed that deficient PK activity leads to premature destruction of red blood cells in the spleen and liver.

The prevalence of PKD is estimated to be between 1:20,000 and 1:300,000 in Caucasian populations and increases in malaria endemic areas, where it may have an advantage because it confers some protection against malaria.
423.symptoms:
The clinical presentation of PKD is quite variable and the severity of symptoms varies widely, from patients with few or no symptoms to patients who develop severe hemolytic anemia requiring treatment with regular red blood cell transfusions.

It usually appears in the neonatal period; however, it is not uncommon for some patients to go undiagnosed until adulthood. Symptoms include mild to severe hemolytic anemia, jaundice, enlargement of the spleen (splenomegaly) and increased numbers of reticulocytes or immature red blood cells (reticulocytosis).
423.prevention:
Neonatal jaundice is usually treated with phototherapy and hydration, which can help avoid exchange transfusion. However, neonates without evidence of jaundice and/or with mild anemia are sometimes not diagnosed until hemolysis or more profound symptoms occur.

Patients with mild PKD usually do not require treatment. Patients with severe PKD are usually treated with blood transfusions, surgical removal of the spleen (splenectomy) and medications to remove excess iron from the blood (chelation therapy). Promising new treatments are emerging such as the use of a small molecule known as AG-348 allosteric activator of PK that is under development, with safety and efficacy demonstrated in phase I and II trials.
423.technical_description:
PKD is caused by mutations in the gene that controls PK expression, known as PKLR which is located on chromosome 1q21. PKD follows an autosomal recessive inheritance pattern, individuals with two copies of the same PKLR mutation or who carry two or more different pathogenic PKLR alleles (compound heterozygotes) may develop PKD.

Mature red blood cells depend on ATP production from glycolysis. Homozygous or compound heterozygous loss-of-function mutations in the PKLR gene result in altered erythrocyte ATP production.

The molecules involved in erythrocyte ATP synthesis are ATPases, located in the membrane, and regulate the exchange of sodium for potassium, which is essential for maintaining membrane integrity and function. ATP deficiency appears to reduce the membrane maintenance capacity of erythrocytes and decrease their deformability, resulting in shortened lifespan and destruction of erythrocytes in the spleen (Al-Samkari et al., 2020).

The consequence of PKD in hemolytic anemia due to pyruvate and ATP deficiency in erythrocytes simultaneously causes an increase of 2,3-diphosphoglycerate (2,3-DPG) in erythrocytes. 2,3-DPG is produced upstream in the glycolytic pathway and regulates the amount of oxygen the body receives, facilitates the release of oxygen from hemoglobin to the tissues.

Here we discuss some of the most common PKLR variants, which are listed below:

The nonsense variant c.1529G>A (p.Arg510Gln, R510Q or rs113403872) is considered the most common PKD variant in northern Europe and the United States. It has been found in homozygosis and compound heterozygosis. The Arg510Gln substitution results in PK enzyme instability and increases susceptibility to ATP inhibition (Zanella et al., 2007).

The c.1456C>T (p.Arg486Trp, R486W or rs116100695) is also frequent in southern European Caucasians. The R486W variant does not affect protein stability, but does affect protein function (Bianchi and Fermo, 2020).

The nonsense variant c.1436G>A (p.Arg479His, R479H or rs118204085) is common in Amish communities, but can also be found in other populations. It has been observed that c.1436G>A is one of the most frequent variants in Indian patients where it has been found in both homozygosis and compound heterozygosis (Warang et al., 2013).

The c.721G>T (p.Glu241Ter, E241* or rs201953584) is a nonsense variant that introduces a premature stop codon. The variant is frequently found in patients from Spain usually combined with another PKLR gene variant (Montllor et al., 2017).

424.name: von Willebrand disease
424.description: von Willebrand`s disease is the most common inherited bleeding disorder and affects one in every 1,000 people. It is characterized by an alteration in the adhesion and aggregation of blood platelets that increases the risk of bleeding.
424.overview:
Von Willebrand factor (vWF) is an essential glycoprotein during blood coagulation that occurs when there is an injury, e.g. injury to a blood capillary. VWF is responsible for mediating platelet adhesion and aggregation in damaged tissue and is also involved in the transport of clotting factor VIII (FVIII) in plasma.

Von Willebrand disease (VWD) may be caused by a defect in the gene that produces VWF, called the VWF gene. Alterations in the VWF gene can lead to decreased levels of VWF and clotting factor VIII in the blood and trigger the disease.

VWD is classified into different types depending on how it affects VWF levels and function. VWD type 1 produces a partial deficiency of vWF, generally follows an autosomal dominant mode of inheritance with incomplete penetrance, although some mutations follow an autosomal recessive mode of inheritance. A disease-causing mutation is considered to have incomplete penetrance when not all individuals with the mutation show symptoms.

VWD type 2 has different subtypes with autosomal dominant and recessive inheritance depending on the subtype and produces hemorrhages ranging from mild to moderate severity. VWD type 3 is the most severe form, is inherited recessively and patients have complete VWF deficiency.
424.symptoms:
The severity of bleeding and its frequency in VWD is highly variable and depends on the patient`s VWF levels and clotting factor VIII activity. The most common symptoms in patients with mild VWD include: mucosal bleeding such as epistaxis or nosebleeds, gingival bleeding, menorrhagia (excessive and prolonged menstrual blood loss) and prolonged bleeding after surgery and dental extractions.

More severe symptoms such as hemarthrosis or gastrointestinal bleeding and soft tissue bruising often occur only in individuals with the more severe and less frequent form of the disease.
424.prevention: Treatment focuses on controlling acute bleeding and preventing the risk of hemorrhage. Hemostatic drugs such as desmopressin are available to prevent and control bleeding, and injectable VWF/FVIII concentrates are used for substitution therapy.
424.technical_description:
VWD is mainly caused by mutations in the VWF gene that affect protein levels or function. Hundreds of mutations have been identified in VWF that can produce various effects on the protein.

The most frequent VWF gene mutation in Europe is rs41276738 (p.Arg854Gln, R854Q, or c.2561G>A), where 1% of the population carries one copy of c.2561G>A. This variant is associated with VWD type 2N and follows an autosomal recessive mode of inheritance. The mutation affects the binding of vWF to coagulation factor VIII making it sometimes difficult to distinguish between 2N VWD and mild hemophilia A (Goodeve, 2016).

Another variant associated with type 2N VWD is rs121964894 (c.2446C>T, p.Arg816Trp, or R816W), which has been observed in homozygosis and compound heterozygosis (one copy of the mutation combined with a different mutation in the same gene) (Johansson et al., 2011).

The nonsense mutation rs61751286 (c.7390C>T, p.Arg2464Cys, or R2464C) is characteristic of patients with the VWD type 1 form and follows autosomal recessive inheritance pattern. It has also been observed in compound heterozygosis. It is important to note that approximately half of the families with VWD1 have incomplete penetrance (only some individuals with a mutation show symptoms).

The rs61750074 variant (c.4135C>T, p.Arg1379Cys, or R1379C) has been found in patients with VWD type 2B. This variant can produce circulating aggregates of VWF and platelets that are removed from the circulation, resulting in a reduction in the amount of platelets in the blood (thrombocytopenia) (Casonato et al., 2015; Pagliari et al., 2016). Another mutation associated with type 2B VWD is rs61749397 (c.3946G>A, p.Val1316Met, or V1316M), patients with this variant show, among other things, increased bleeding, reduced platelet aggregation and macrothrombocytopenia (reduced platelet count and significantly increased platelet size or "giant platelets") (Kauskot et al., 2016).

The rs61751296 is a loss-of-function variant in the VWF gene (also known as p.Arg2535Ter, R2535*, or c.7603C>T) that produces a truncated protein. It is associated with the severe form of VWD type 3 (James and Goodeve, 2011; Manderstedt et al., 2018; Zhang et al., 1992).

425.name: Neurofibromatosis type I
425.description: Neurofibromatosis type 1 (NF1) or von Recklinghausen`s disease is one of the most common autosomal dominant diseases and has an incidence of approximately one case per 3000 births.  This pathology is characterized by the formation of neurofibromas in the skin.
425.overview: NF1 is caused by alterations in the NF1 gene that codes for the neurofibromin protein which is thought to have a tumor suppressor function. It is estimated that in 50% of NF1 patients the mutations are spontaneous or sporadic and are not inherited from the parents, while in the other 50% of cases they are germline mutations that are inherited from one parent in an autosomal dominant manner.
425.symptoms:
The severity and clinical manifestations of NF1 can vary markedly among patients, even among affected members of the same family. It is a disease that can result in the development of multiple nerve and skin tumors, called neurofibromas, which are usually benign. Another characteristic clinical manifestation is the formation of small nodules in the iris known as Lisch nodules.

Other skin changes may also occur, such as abnormal colored spots (pale tan or light brown), which usually appear in the first year of life and increase in number and size during childhood; and freckles in atypical locations (e.g. in the armpits or groin).

Occasionally, tumors may involve the brain or spinal cord nerves. In patients with NF1, the risk of developing gliomas is increased and in women, the predisposition to breast cancer is increased.

Other symptoms include macrocephaly and short stature. Seizures, learning disabilities, speech difficulties, hyperactivity and skeletal malformations such as scoliosis may occasionally occur.
425.prevention: Medical follow-up by various specialists should be performed since it is a multisystemic disorder. Neurofibromas are usually monitored. If treatment is required, neurofibromas can be surgically removed. Smaller cutaneous neurofibromas can be treated with laser or electrocautery.
425.technical_description:
Neurofibromatosis (NF) is a disease that causes tumors in nervous tissue, including the brain, spinal cord, and peripheral nerves. It is classified into three subtypes NF1, NF2, and schwannomatosis (SWN), NF1 is the most frequent subtype and accounts for 96% of NF cases.

NF1 is caused by the presence of pathogenic mutations in the NF1 gene that produces neurofibromin, a tumor suppressor GTPase protein that regulates the RAS/MAPK signaling pathway. For this reason, NF1 is classified within the group of rasopathies and has in common certain clinical manifestations with other diseases that are part of this group, such as Noonan syndrome.

To date, more than 2,000 pathogenic variants in the NF1 gene have been identified in patients with the disease. Most pathogenic mutations in the NF1 gene reduce the total expression of the neurofibromin protein by premature truncation or microdeletion. It has been shown that there is a variety of phenotypes and that these could be influenced by the mutation, for example, patients with p.Met992del (rs267606606) have a mild phenotype, while those with substitutions such as p.Arg1276 (rs137854556) and p.Arg1809Cys (rs797045139) have Noonan syndrome-like features, including cardiovascular abnormalities. Patients carrying microdeletions in the NF1 gene have a more severe phenotype.

429.name: Methylmalonic Aciduria with Homocystinuria, cblD type
429.description: Methylmalonic aciduria and homocystinuria, cblD type (cblD), is one of the less common genetic disorders of vitamin B12 metabolism, caused by mutations in the MMADHC gene. The protein encoded by this gene participates in the metabolism and trafficking of vitamin B12 within the cell.
429.overview:
cblD disorder is a very rare autosomal recessive inborn error of intracellular vitamin B12 (cobalamin) metabolism, with few cases described in the literature, resulting from pathogenic variants in the MMADHC gene. The protein produced from this gene is responsible for directing processed cobalamin to its correct location within the cell. It sends cobalamin to the mitochondria to be converted into adenosylcobalamin (AdoCbl), or to the cytoplasm to be converted into methylcobalamin (MeCbl). These two active forms of B12 are essential for the function of two separate enzymes.

When the MMADHC protein is defective, this critical trafficking system fails. Depending on the specific mutation, this can lead to a deficiency of AdoCbl, MeCbl, or both. This results in the accumulation of methylmalonic acid, homocysteine, or both, altering normal development and cellular function. The cblD type is significantly rarer than other cobalamin defects such as cblC, and its precise incidence is not well established. The existence of different clinical subtypes makes its diagnosis and characterization complex.
429.symptoms:
The clinical presentation of cblD is highly heterogeneous, both in onset and severity, and is classified into three main subtypes according to the biochemical defect.

cblD-MMA/HC combined or classic form: combined elevation of methylmalonic acid and homocysteine. Symptoms include developmental delay, encephalopathy, seizures, intellectual developmental deterioration, nystagmus, and megaloblastic anemia. Some cases of symptom onset in adolescence have been described.cblD-MMA isolated: patients only present with methylmalonic aciduria. They manifest with typical symptoms of isolated MMA, such as vomiting, lethargy, and metabolic acidosis.cblD-HC isolated: Patients only present with homocystinuria. Symptoms include developmental delay, learning difficulties, and thromboembolic events (blood clots).
429.prevention: Treatment for cblD is tailored to the patient's specific biochemical profile and aims to correct underlying metabolic imbalances. The main lifelong therapy involves administering high doses of hydroxocobalamin (OHCbl), though responsiveness can vary. For patients with elevated homocysteine, oral betaine is given to provide an alternative processing route. Folinic acid and dextrose may also be employed. L-carnitine supplementation is used to help manage toxic methylmalonic acid accumulation. A protein-restricted diet is often necessary, particularly in patients with a significant methylmalonic aciduria component.
429.technical_description: CblD deficiency can produce the three described phenotypes according to the type of alteration produced in the MMADHC protein: isolated homocystinuria due to nonsense mutations located in the C-terminal part of the protein (e.g. rs118204044, c.776T>C or p.Leu259Pro), isolated methylmalonic aciduria due to mutations in the N-terminal part of the protein (e.g. rs118204047, c.160C>T or p.Arg54Ter), or combined methylmalonic aciduria and homocystinuria due to nonsense, splice site, or truncating variants towards the C-terminal part of the protein (e.g. rs118204048, c.748C>T or p.Arg250Ter) (Atkinson et al., 2014).

Abu-El-Haija A, Mendelsohn BA, Duncan JL, et al. Cobalamin D Deficiency Identified Through Newborn Screening. JIMD Rep. 2019;44:73-77.

 Coelho D, Suormala T, Stucki M, et al. Gene identification for the cblD defect of vitamin B12 metabolism. N Engl J Med. 2008 Apr 3;358(14):1454-64.

 Stucki M, Coelho D, Suormala T, et al. Molecular mechanisms leading to three different phenotypes in the cblD defect of intracellular cobalamin metabolism. Hum Mol Genet. 2012 Mar 15;21(6):1410-8.

430.name: Methylmalonic Aciduria with Homocystinuria, cblC type
430.description: Methylmalonic aciduria with homocystinuria cblC type is the most common inborn error of vitamin B12 metabolism, caused by mutations in the MMACHC gene. This defect leads to the accumulation of toxic substances that cause a wide range of severe neurological, developmental, and hematological problems.
430.overview:
Methylmalonic aciduria and homocystinuria, cblC type (cblC), is a complex inherited disorder of vitamin B12 (cobalamin) metabolism. It is caused by pathogenic variants in the MMACHC gene. This gene provides instructions for a protein that is essential for converting vitamin B12 into its two active forms within the cell: adenosylcobalamin (AdoCbl) and methylcobalamin (MeCbl). These two molecules act as vital cofactors for two different enzymes. AdoCbl is needed for the function of methylmalonyl-CoA mutase, while MeCbl is needed for methionine synthase.

A defect in the MMACHC protein affects both pathways simultaneously. This results in elevated methylmalonic acid in the body (due to the blockage of the first pathway) and elevated homocysteine (due to the blockage of the second pathway). The combined toxic effects of these substances disrupt many cellular processes, leading to multisystem organ damage. This defect in intracellular cobalamin metabolism has an estimated incidence of 1 in 200,000 births, affecting all populations.
430.symptoms: The severe form of early onset, between the first and fourth year of life, produces symptoms such as lethargy, low muscle tone, electroencephalographic abnormalities, nystagmus, strabismus, and developmental delay. Patients may develop severe neurological damage, eye disease (retinopathy), and blood abnormalities. The late onset form, less frequent than the former, can manifest in adolescence or adulthood with a different set of symptoms, including cognitive impairment, psychiatric problems (psychosis, dementia), ataxia (loss of coordination), kidney disease, and pulmonary hypertension.
430.prevention: Long-term management focuses on reducing homocysteine levels, treating with high-dose hydroxocobalamin (a specific form of vitamin B12) to provide a surplus of the vitamin and maximize any residual enzyme function. Oral betaine supplementation is administered to provide an alternative route for reducing homocysteine levels. L-carnitine is also used to help excrete toxic organic acids. In some severe cases, a protein-restricted diet may be employed.
430.technical_description:
Methylmalonic acidemia or methylmalonic aciduria, or MMA, which is the most frequent inborn error of organic acid metabolism, follows an autosomal recessive inheritance pattern. MMA is classified as isolated or combined with homocystinuria. The latter is divided into 5 subtypes depending on the biochemical characteristics of the patients (cblC, cblD, cblF, cblJ, and cblX). Of the 5 subtypes, cblC is the most common, accounting for 80% of MMA cases.

More than 100 pathogenic variants have been described in the literature in MMACHC, including missense mutations, nonsense mutations, small insertions and deletions, and splicing mutations. Three of the most common variants identified in patients with this inborn error are c.271dup (p.Arg91fs, rs398124292), c.394C>T (p.Arg132Ter, rs121918241), and c.609G>A (p.Trp203Ter, rs587776889) which can present in homozygosity or in compound heterozygosity with other pathogenic variants of MMACHC.
430.technical_citations:
Arhip L, Brox-Torrecilla N, Romero I, et al. Late-onset methylmalonic acidemia and homocysteinemia (cblC disease): systematic review. Orphanet J Rare Dis. 2024 Jan 20.19(1):20.

 Hu S, Mei S, Liu N, et al. Molecular genetic characterization of cblC defects in 126 pedigrees and prenatal genetic diagnosis of pedigrees with combined methylmalonic aciduria and homocystinuria. BMC Med Genet. 2018 Aug 29.19(1):154.

431.name: Methylmalonic Aciduria with Homocystinuria, cblJ type
431.description: Methylmalonic acidemia with homocystinuria, cblJ type (cblJ), is an extremely rare genetic disorder caused by mutations in the ABCD4 gene. The ABCD4 protein together with the LMBRD1 protein transports vitamin B12 out of the lysosome; a defect in it traps the vitamin, leading to a severe infantile-onset disease that is clinically indistinguishable from cblF type.
431.overview:
CblJ disorder is a childhood-onset, severe inborn error of vitamin B12 (cobalamin) metabolism that is caused by pathogenic variants in the ABCD4 gene. This gene encodes a protein of the ATP-binding cassette (ABC) transporter family, which forms a functional complex with the LMBRD1 protein (the protein defective in cblF disorder) in the lysosomal membrane. The ABCD4/LMBRD1 complex is essential for the transport of vitamin B12 from the lysosome to the cellular cytoplasm.

In cblJ, a defective ABCD4 protein disrupts the function of this entire transport complex. As in cblF, the result is that vitamin B12 becomes trapped and accumulates within this compartment. The cell is therefore deprived of B12, preventing its conversion to the active forms, AdoCbl and MeCbl. This causes the characteristic combined elevation of methylmalonic acid and homocysteine.
431.symptoms: The clinical signs and symptoms of cblJ are considered identical to those observed in cblF disorder. Affected infants present with feeding problems, growth delay, and marked hypotonia. Developmental delay is common, and some infants may lose previously acquired skills. Hematological problems such as megaloblastic anemia, as well as inflammation of the mouth and tongue (stomatitis and glossitis), can also occur.
431.prevention: Because the underlying functional problem in cblJ (lysosomal trapping of B12) is the same as in cblF, the management strategy is identical. The aim is to bypass the transport defect and correct the severe biochemical consequences. Treatment involves lifelong, high-dose intramuscular injections of hydroxycobalamin in combination with oral supplements: betaine to reduce homocysteine levels and L-carnitine to help excrete toxic organic acids.
431.technical_description:
Currently, five types of methylmalonic aciduria with homocystinuria have been identified: cblC (the most frequent), cblD, cblF, cblJ, and cblX. Each of these types is caused by specific genetic alterations:

cblC: Mutations in the MMACHC genecblD: Mutations in the MMADHC genecblF: Mutations in the LMBRD1 genecblJ: Mutations in the ABCD4 genecblX: Mutations in the HCFC1 geneIt is relevant to highlight that, in the cblJ type, a specific mutation, c.423C>G (p.Asn141Lys) of the ABCD4 gene, has been observed in homozygosis. This mutation has been reported in three cases from Taiwan and China, these being some of the few documented cases of cblJ to date.
431.technical_citations:
Coelho D, Kim JC, Miousse IR, et al. Mutations in ABCD4 cause a new inborn error of vitamin B12 metabolism. Nat Genet. 2012 Oct.44(10):1152-5.

 Kawaguchi K, Morita M. ABC Transporter Subfamily D: Distinct Differences in Behavior between ABCD1-3 and ABCD4 in Subcellular Localization, Function, and Human Disease. Biomed Res Int. 2016.2016:6786245.

 Liu Y, Kang L, Li D, Jin Y, et al. Patients with cobalamin G or J defect missed by the current newborn screening program: diagnosis and novel mutations. J Hum Genet. 2019 Apr.64(4):305-312.

432.name: Methylmalonic Aciduria with Homocystinuria, cblF type
432.description: Methylmalonic aciduria and homocystinuria, cblF type (cblF), is an extremely rare genetic disorder caused by mutations in the LMBRD1 gene. This defect alters the transport of vitamin B12 from the lysosome to the cellular cytoplasm.
432.overview:
cblF disorder is an autosomal recessive inborn error of vitamin B12 (cobalamin) metabolism, of infantile and severe onset. It is caused by pathogenic variants in the LMBRD1 gene. 

In cblF, the defective LMBRD1 protein cannot transport B12 out of the cell's lysosome. The vitamin becomes trapped and accumulates within this compartment, so it cannot be transformed into its active metabolites methylcobalamin or MeCbl and adenosylcobalamin or AdoCbl. This leads to a functional deficiency of both AdoCbl and MeCbl, causing the simultaneous elevation of methylmalonic acid and homocysteine. cblF is an exceptionally rare condition, with a prevalence of less than 1 case per 1,000,000 people.
432.symptoms: CblF typically presents in early childhood with severe symptoms such as growth retardation, significant feeding difficulties, hypotonia, or low muscle tone. Patients may present with developmental delay or loss of previously acquired skills. Other common symptoms include stomatitis (mouth inflammation), glossitis (tongue inflammation), and hematological problems such as macrocytic anemia with neutropenia and thrombocytopenia. Congenital heart defects or atypical facial features have also been observed in some cases.
432.prevention: The main treatment consists of lifelong intramuscular injections and high doses of hydroxocobalamin. In addition, oral supplements are crucial: betaine and folic acid are administered to help reduce toxic homocysteine levels, and L-carnitine is used to help eliminate harmful organic acids from the body. In these patients, it is important to avoid prolonged fasting and maintain adequate hydration. Early diagnosis and treatment are fundamental to obtain greater benefit from the treatment.
432.technical_description:
The protein encoded by LMBRD1 (lipocalin membrane receptor domain containing 1) is located in the lysosomal membrane and is responsible for exporting cobalamin to the cytoplasm. When LMBRD1 activity is affected, free cobalamin accumulates inside the lysosome, so that cobalamin is not converted to its active forms, leading to an accumulation of homocysteine and methylmalonic acid.

Around 50 pathogenic or possibly pathogenic variants have been linked to cblF deficiency. However, there is no clear genotype-phenotype correlation. One of the most common variants in patients with cblF deficiency of European ancestry is c.1056del or p.Asn353fs (rs749272546), which causes the deletion of a guanine in the LMBRD1 gene sequence and has been observed in homozygosity and compound heterozygosity.
432.technical_citations:
Armour CM, Brebner A, Watkins D, et al. A patient with an inborn error of vitamin B12 metabolism (cblF) detected by newborn screening. Pediatrics. 2013 Jul.132(1):e257-61.

 Huizing M, Gahl WA. Inherited disorders of lysosomal membrane transporters. Biochim Biophys Acta Biomembr. 2020 Dec 1.1862(12):183336.

 Rutsch F, Gailus S, Suormala T, Fowler B. LMBRD1: the gene for the cblF defect of vitamin B12 metabolism. J Inherit Metab Dis. 2011 Feb.34(1):121-6.

 Tanner SM, Sturm AC, Baack EC, et al. Inherited cobalamin malabsorption. Mutations in three genes reveal functional and ethnic patterns. Orphanet J Rare Dis. 2012 Aug 28.7:56.

435.name: Oculocutaneous Albinism (TYRP1 gene)
435.description: Oculocutaneous albinism type 3 (OCA3) is caused by mutations in the TYRP1 gene, which hinders eumelanin production. More common in individuals of Southern African ancestry, this condition results in a characteristic reddish-brown skin and hair phenotype.
435.overview:
Oculocutaneous albinism type 3 (OCA3), also known as rufous albinism, is a distinct form of inherited albinism characterized by reduced, rather than absent, pigmentation. It is caused by pathogenic variants in the TYRP1 gene, which encodes for tyrosinase-related protein 1, or TYRP1. OCA3 follows an autosomal recessive inheritance pattern. 

OCA3 affects 1 in 8,500 people in Africa, whereas it is very rare in Caucasian and Asian populations. Cases of OCA3 have been described in Germany, Japan, China, Indo-Pakistani populations, and some regions of Europe.
435.symptoms:
The clinical presentation of OCA3 is quite different from other common types of albinism and is apparent from birth. Instead of white skin and hair, individuals with OCA3 typically have reddish-copper or reddish-brown skin, red or reddish hair, and hazel or green irises combined with brown. Their skin may have some ability to tan. In OCA3 individuals of Asian origin, the hair is blond or golden, with light brown eyebrows, and the skin pigmentation is lighter compared to the skin color of the parents.

Ocular symptoms are often milder than in OCA1 or OCA2. Nystagmus (involuntary eye movements) and photophobia (light sensitivity) may be subtle or even absent. Visual acuity, while often reduced, is usually better preserved than in other forms of OCA.
435.prevention: As with all forms of albinism, there is no cure for OCA3. Although individuals with OCA3 have some pigment, they still have a significantly increased risk of sun damage and skin cancer. Therefore, dermatological care involving consistent use of broad-spectrum, high-SPF sunscreen, protective clothing, and regular skin surveillance is essential. Ophthalmic care is also important; this includes regular eye exams to monitor vision and the prescription of corrective lenses if needed.
435.technical_description:
The enzyme TYRP1 plays an essential role in the melanin synthesis pathway, specifically in the production of eumelanin (black-brown pigment). Its functions include stabilizing the main enzyme, tyrosinase, and catalyzing the oxidation of certain melanic precursors. A defect in TYRP1 significantly disrupts eumelanin synthesis, although its effect on pheomelanin (red-yellow pigment) is lesser.

Among the first mutations of the TYRP1 gene described in South Africa are the variants p.Ser166Ter (rs104894130) and p.Lys368fs (rs387906560). In Asian patients with oculocutaneous albinism type 3 (OCA3), pathogenic variants such as p.N353Vfs (rs387906562), which causes a frameshift, have been identified.

Alterations in the TYRP1 gene have also been observed in Solomon Islands populations associated with the recessive trait of "Melanesian blond," among which the variant rs387907171 (p.Arg93Cys or R93C) stands out. This variant is found in 26% of the island's inhabitants, but does not exist outside of Oceania. Current evidence is insufficient to classify R93C as pathogenic, so it is not reported here.
435.technical_citations:
Grønskov K, Ek J, Brondum-Nielsen K. Oculocutaneous albinism. Orphanet J Rare Dis. 2007 Nov 2.2:43.

 Kenny EE, Timpson NJ, Sikora M, et al. Melanesian blond hair is caused by an amino acid change in TYRP1. Science. 2012 May 4.336(6081):554.

 Manga P, Kromberg JG, Box NF, et al. Rufous oculocutaneous albinism in southern African Blacks is caused by mutations in the TYRP1 gene. Am J Hum Genet. 1997 Nov.61(5):1095-101.

 Ullah MI. Clinical and Mutation Spectrum of Autosomal Recessive Non-Syndromic Oculocutaneous Albinism (nsOCA) in Pakistan: A Review. Genes (Basel). 2022 Jun 16.13(6):1072.

437.name: Oculocutaneous Albinism (SLC45A2 gene)
437.description: It is a form of albinism caused by mutations in the SLC45A2 gene, particularly prevalent in the Japanese population. It produces a very variable reduction of melanin and significant visual impairment from birth.
437.overview:
Oculocutaneous albinism type 4 (OCA4) is an autosomal recessive hereditary disorder characterized by reduced melanin pigmentation in the skin, hair, and eyes. It is caused by pathogenic variants in the SLC45A2 gene, also known as the membrane-associated transporter protein (MATP) gene. The SLC45A2 protein is localized to the membrane of melanosomes (the cellular compartments where melanin is produced) and is thought to function as a transporter. Its precise role is still being investigated, but it is considered crucial for the proper trafficking, folding, or environmental regulation of tyrosinase, the key enzyme that initiates melanin production.

When the SLC45A2 gene is mutated, the resulting dysfunctional protein disrupts normal tyrosinase processing, severely impairing its ability to produce melanin. This leads to the characteristic hypopigmentation observed in OCA4. OCA4 is a frequent cause of albinism in Japan, accounting for approximately 24% of all OCA cases, while in Europeans, variants in SLC45A2 account for approximately 11% of OCs. Globally, the prevalence of OCA4 is estimated to be 1:100,000.
437.symptoms:
The clinical presentation of OCA4 is highly variable, with a spectrum ranging from mild to severe and a phenotype that can overlap with that of OCA1 (TYR gene) and OCA2 (OCA2 gene). At birth, hair may be white, blond, or light brown and, although the skin is fair, some individuals gain some pigment with age.

However, ocular involvement is the most constant feature and is present from birth. This includes nystagmus (involuntary eye movements), strabismus, foveal hypoplasia (underdevelopment of the retina), photophobia (light sensitivity), and reduced visual acuity that, although of variable degree, always implies a visual deficit.
437.prevention:
OCA4 has no cure, so its management is supportive and continuous, focusing on ophthalmological and dermatological complications.

Ophthalmological care is key and includes regular check-ups, corrective lenses (glasses or contact lenses), and tinted or photochromic filters to control photophobia. Low vision aids are usually required in educational and work settings. Strabismus can be corrected with surgery.

Likewise, strict sun protection is crucial to minimize the high risk of skin cancer. This involves the use of broad-spectrum sunscreens with high sun protection factor, appropriate clothing, and regular dermatological check-ups.
437.technical_description:
The SLC45A2 gene, a gene located on chromosome 5, encodes a 530-amino acid membrane protein that interacts, through a mechanism that is not yet fully understood. Variants in this gene have been related to skin and hair color, melanoma risk, and mutations in this gene cause OCA4. Currently, more than 150 variants considered pathogenic or likely pathogenic as a cause of OCA4 have been identified in the SLC45A2 gene.

Among them, the most common variant in the Japanese population is c.469G>A (p.Asp157Asn, rs121912621). This is associated with a phenotype of yellow hair, blue eyes, and nystagmus, and has also been observed, albeit with lower frequency, in other Asian populations such as Korean, Chinese, and Indian. In patients of Chinese origin, the variant c.478G>C (p.Asp160His, rs760780597) stands out, representing 55.6% of the mutated alleles in the SLC45A2 gene within this population.
437.technical_citations:
Hayashi M, Suzuki T.  Oculocutaneous Albinism Type 4. 2005 Nov 17 [updated 2017 Sep 7]. In: Adam MP, Feldman J, Mirzaa GM, Pagon RA, Wallace SE, Amemiya A, editors. GeneReviews .

 Mauri L, Barone L, Al Oum M, et al. SLC45A2 mutation frequency in Oculocutaneous Albinism Italian patients doesn't differ from other European studies. Gene. 2014 Jan 1.533(1):398-402.

 Moreno-Artero E, Morice-Picard F, Lasseaux E, et al. Oculo-Cutaneous Albinism Type 4 (OCA4): Phenotype-Genotype Correlation. Genes (Basel). 2022 Nov 23.13(12):2198.

444.name: Amyloidosis, Finnish-type (GSN gene)
444.description: Gelsolin amyloidosis, also known as Finnish type Familial Amyloidosis, is a rare hereditary systemic disorder caused by mutations in the gelsolin gene or GSN.
444.overview:
Variants in the GSN gene are the cause of Gelsolin Amyloidosis (AGel), or Meretoja syndrome, a slow-progressing systemic disease that causes progressive corneal dystrophy, cutis laxa, and polyneuropathy. The GSN gene provides instructions for making gelsolin, a protein that helps control the assembly and disassembly of actin, an essential component of the cell cytoskeleton.

A specific mutation in the GSN gene (most commonly p.Asp214Asn) results in an altered gelsolin protein that is susceptible to being incorrectly cleaved by other enzymes within the cell. This process releases a small, insoluble protein fragment that misfolds and aggregates. The damaged protein is deposited in various tissues, causing a characteristic triad of symptoms affecting the eyes, nerves, and skin.

AGel is inherited in an autosomal dominant pattern, meaning that inheriting a single mutated copy of the GSN gene is sufficient to cause the disorder. Although the condition has a high prevalence in Finland, due to a founder effect, cases have been described in Africa, Asia, Europe, and South America.
444.symptoms: The onset of symptoms typically occurs in adulthood, usually between 30 and 50 years of age. The disease is defined by a classic triad of clinical findings: bilateral lattice corneal dystrophy (causing visual impairment and eye irritation), progressive cranial and peripheral neuropathy (leading to facial weakness or paralysis and numbness), and cutis laxa (flaccid, loose, and inelastic skin). Slow progression means symptoms worsen over several decades.
444.prevention: There is no cure for gelsolin amyloidosis, so management is supportive and focuses on treating specific symptoms to improve quality of life. This requires a multidisciplinary team of specialists. Ophthalmic care includes lubricating eye drops and, in cases, corneal transplantation to restore vision. Neurological symptoms can be managed with physical therapy, while plastic and reconstructive surgery (such as blepharoplasty for drooping eyelids) can address problems caused by cutis laxa.
444.technical_description: The most prominent and best-characterized variant of gelsolin-associated familial amyloidosis is the Finnish variant c.487G>T (p.Asp163Tyr, D187Y, rs121909715). At the same position in the gene, the change c.487G>A (p.Asp163Asn, D187N, rs121909715) can occur, which is also pathogenic. The D187N/Y substitution occurs at a calcium-binding site in the second domain of the protein, preventing it from binding properly and making gelsolin activation less efficient. Other mutations have been observed in patients affecting other domains of the gelsolin protein, and their effect on the protein is still being studied.
444.technical_citations:
Mustonen T, Schmidt EK, Valori M, et al. Common origin of the gelsolin gene variant in 62 Finnish AGel amyloidosis families. Eur J Hum Genet. 2018 Jan.26(1):117-123.

 Solomon JP, Page LJ, Balch WE, et al. Gelsolin amyloidosis: genetics, biochemistry, pathology and possible strategies for therapeutic intervention. Crit Rev Biochem Mol Biol. 2012 May-Jun.47(3):282-96.

 Zorgati H, Larsson M, Ren W, et al. The role of gelsolin domain 3 in familial amyloidosis (Finnish type). Proc Natl Acad Sci U S A. 2019 Jul 9.116(28):13958-13963.

445.name: Systemic Amyloidosis (FGA gene)
445.description: It is a rare hereditary systemic pathology caused by pathogenic variants in the FGA gene that lead to the accumulation of amyloid deposits of the alpha chain of fibrinogen A, which progressively damage organs and mainly affect the kidneys.
445.overview:
Systemic amyloidosis due to mutations in the FGA gene is a rare genetic disorder. The FGA gene encodes for the alpha A chain of fibrinogen, an essential protein produced in the liver that plays a key role in blood clotting. Pathogenic variants (mutations) in this gene result in an unstable and misfolding-prone alpha A chain. The body cannot effectively clear these abnormal proteins, which aggregate to form insoluble amyloid fibrils. These fibrils slowly deposit in tissues and organs, disrupting their normal structure and function.

Unlike other hereditary amyloidoses, the FGA-associated form is characterized by marked kidney involvement. Amyloid deposits accumulate in the glomerulus, the filtration unit of the kidney, causing irreversible damage.
445.symptoms: Patients with this type of amyloidosis do not present symptoms until adulthood, typically from 40 or 50 years of age. Symptoms include proteinuria (presence of protein in urine), edema, hypertension, and in some patients, kidney failure. In some patients, kidney disease may progress and require dialysis or even a kidney transplant.
445.prevention: Currently, there is no specific treatment. Initial management is supportive, focused on preserving renal function for as long as possible through blood pressure control and management of fluid retention. As the disease progresses to end-stage renal failure, patients may require dialysis and be candidates for a kidney transplant, sometimes combined with a liver transplant.
445.technical_description:
Amyloidosis are a group of diseases caused by the deposition of abnormal proteins, called amyloid fibrils, in various tissues, leading to organ damage. They can be acquired or hereditary. To date, up to 36 proteins have been described that may be related to the formation of amyloid deposits in different organs. The specific form described here is caused by a defect in fibrinogen, a complex protein produced in the liver and essential for blood clotting. Fibrinogen is composed of three distinct chains (Aα, Bβ, and γ), each encoded by a different gene (FGA, FGB, and FGG).

Alpha-A fibrinogen amyloidosis (AFib) is due to a mutation in one of the two copies of the FGA gene. This genetic defect causes the Aα chain to misfold, becoming unstable. Over time, these abnormal proteins aggregate and accumulate in the kidneys in the form of amyloid fibrils. This deposition is the most common cause of hereditary renal amyloidosis and leads to progressive and irreversible damage to the kidney's filtering function. Although several mutations have been described, one of the most common variants worldwide is c.1634A>T (p.Glu545Val, E545V, E526V or rs121909612), identified by Benson et al., which causes the disease with having a single altered copy.
445.technical_citations:
Benson MD, Liepnieks J, Uemichi T, et al. Hereditary renal amyloidosis associated with a mutant fibrinogen alpha-chain. Nat Genet. 1993 Mar.3(3):252-5.

 Chapman J, Dogan A. Fibrinogen alpha amyloidosis: insights from proteomics. Expert Rev Proteomics. 2019 Sep.16(9):783-793.

 He L, Zhou J, Wang M, et al. Clinical manifestations, diagnosis and treatment of hereditary fibrinogen Aα-chain renal amyloidosis: one case report and systematic review. Int Urol Nephrol. 2025 Feb.57(2):517-533.

 Machado JR, Silva MV, Neves PD, et al . Fibrinogen A alpha-chain amyloidosis: report of the first case in Latin America. Amyloid. 2013 Mar.20(1):52-5.

 Taylor GW, Gilbertson JA, Sayed R, et al. Proteomic Analysis for the Diagnosis of Fibrinogen Aα-chain Amyloidosis. Kidney Int Rep. 2019 Apr 15.4(7):977-986.

446.name: Visceral Amyloidosis (APOA1 gene)
446.description: Visceral amyloidosis due to Apolipoprotein A-I (APOA1) is a very rare hereditary disease in which mutations in the APOA1 gene cause misfolding of this protein, the main component of HDL cholesterol. The abnormal protein accumulates in the form of amyloid fibrils in multiple organs, mainly in the liver, kidneys, and occasionally the larynx and heart.
446.overview:
Apolipoprotein A-I (ApoA-I), encoded by the APOA1 gene, is the main structural protein of high-density lipoproteins (HDL), commonly known as "good cholesterol". Its function is crucial in the reverse transport of cholesterol from tissues to the liver. Pathogenic variants (mutations) in the APOA1 gene cause the ApoA-I protein to be unstable and misfold. The body cannot clear these abnormal proteins, which aggregate and deposit as amyloid fibrils in the extracellular spaces of various organs.

This form of amyloidosis is a systemic, autosomal dominant, and extremely rare disorder. Unlike other amyloidoses that may have a main target organ, APOA1 amyloidosis is characterized by its multivisceral organ involvement. The most commonly affected organs are the liver and kidneys. However, deposits can also be found in other tissues.
446.symptoms: The age of onset of symptoms is variable and depends on the specific mutation, but it generally manifests in adulthood. Liver involvement can cause hepatomegaly (enlarged liver) and liver failure, while kidney damage manifests as proteinuria and progressive decline in kidney function. If the heart is affected, cardiomyopathy may develop.
446.prevention: The treatment is supportive and aims to manage specific organ dysfunction. This includes regular monitoring of liver and kidney function, management of high blood pressure, and treatment of organ failure symptoms. In patients with kidney failure, a combined liver and kidney transplant may be necessary.
446.technical_description:
The G26R variant of the APOA1 gene, also known as "apoA-IIowa", was the first identified cause of this type of amyloidosis. This variant causes specific fragments of the protein (amino acids 1-83) to misfold, forming amyloid deposits. These deposits accumulate mainly in vital organs such as the kidney, liver, and heart, causing the progressive damage that characterizes the disease.

To date, about 20 pathogenic variants have been described that could cause hereditary ApoA-1 amyloidosis. Here we analyze 4 variants of the APOA1 gene that are associated with visceral familial amyloidosis, specifically studying the variants c.126C>G (p.Tyr42Ter), c.148G>C (p.Gly50Arg, G50R or G26R), and c.220T>C (p.Trp74Arg) and c.532_533dup (p.His179fs).
446.technical_citations:
Joy T, Wang J, Hahn A, Hegele RA. APOA1 related amyloidosis: a case report and literature review. Clin Biochem. 2003 Nov.36(8):641-5.

 Kameyama H, Nakajima H, Nishitsuji K, et al. Iowa Mutant Apolipoprotein A-I (ApoA-IIowa) Fibrils Target Lysosomes. Sci Rep. 2016 Jul 28;6:30391.

 Lu C, Zuo K, Lu Y, Liang S, et al. Apolipoprotein A-1-related amyloidosis 2 case reports and review of the literature. Medicine (Baltimore). 2017 Sep.96(39):e8148.

448.name: Fanconi Anemia (FANCA gene)
448.description: Fanconi anemia is a rare inherited disorder caused by mutations in genes, such as FANCA, which are crucial for a DNA repair mechanism.
448.overview:
Fanconi anemia (FA) is an autosomal recessive genetic disease caused by the alteration of the DNA damage response pathway, a fundamental cellular pathway for the repair of intrachain crosslinks. Up to 22 genes have been described as being involved in FA, with the FANCA gene being the most frequently affected gene. This genetic defect causes chromosomal instability, which particularly affects hematopoietic stem cells, resulting in progressive bone marrow failure, congenital anomalies, and an increased risk of developing acute myeloid leukemia and other types of cancer.

Two-thirds of patients with Fanconi anemia have pathogenic variants in the FANCA gene in homozygosity or compound heterozygosity. Variants in FANCA include single nucleotide variants, small insertions and deletions, and large deletions. Large deletions are not included in this analysis.
448.symptoms: The clinical picture of Fanconi anemia is heterogeneous, characterized by congenital anomalies, bone marrow failure, and an increased risk of malignancy. Physical anomalies include short stature, skeletal abnormalities of the limbs, and skin blemishes. Secondly, the hallmark is progressive bone marrow failure, which usually manifests in the first decade of life with anemia, thrombocytopenia, and leukopenia. Finally, patients have an increased risk of acute myeloid leukemia and certain solid tumors.
448.prevention: The management of AA is complex and requires a multidisciplinary team. It includes strict monitoring with periodic blood tests to monitor bone marrow function and early cancer detection programs. Red blood cell or platelet transfusions may be necessary under certain circumstances. Oral androgen treatment, such as oxymetholone and danazol, can be used to improve blood cell counts, especially red blood cells and platelets. The only curative treatment for bone marrow failure is hematopoietic stem cell transplantation.
448.technical_description:
Fanconi Anemia (FA) is genetically heterogeneous, with at least 22 genes encoding a group of proteins that cooperate in a unique DNA repair pathway, FA/BRCA.

The FANCA gene is the most common cause of Fanconi Anemia (FA), accounting for 60-70% of cases. It is followed by the FANCC gene, with variants present in approximately 14% of cases. The protein produced by FANCA is an essential part of a complex called the "core complex," which consists of 8 FA family proteins (FANCB, FANCC, FANCE, FANCF, FANCG, FANCL, FANCM, and FANCA) along with two additional proteins (FAAP24 and FAAP100). This complex plays a crucial role in DNA repair and regulation of the S phase of the cell cycle.

Two variants of the FANCA gene have been identified in diverse populations, representing between 5% and 2% of all alleles associated with Fanconi Anemia (FA). These variants are c.1115_1118del (p.Val372fs, rs397507552) in exon 13 and c.3782TCT[2] (p.Phe1263del, rs397507553).

The symptoms of FA are highly variable among patients, and it is not easy to predict the severity of the disease based on the genetic mutation. Generally, null variants, which completely eliminate the function of the FANCA protein, produce more severe forms of the pathology. Certain missense variants, such as c.2738A>C (p.His913Pro, rs1302083447), c.2852G>A (p.Arg951Gln, rs755922289), and c.2851C>T (p.Arg951Trp, rs755546887), are associated with milder forms of the pathology, characterized by slower progression.

449.name: Fanconi Anemia (FANCG gene)
449.description: The FANCG gene (Fanconi anemia complementation group G) encodes a protein involved in the cellular response to DNA damage.
449.overview:
Fanconi anemia (FA) is a genomic instability disorder that produces congenital anomalies, progressive bone marrow failure, and cancer predisposition. It is caused by pathogenic variants in genes involved in the repair of DNA interstrand crosslinks. 

Approximately 10% of FA patients have a pathogenic variant in the FANCG gene. FANCG variants that are particularly prevalent in FA patients from certain populations have been identified, such as c.1066C>T and c.1589_1591delATA in Korea and Japan, which may originate from an ancestral haplotype.
449.symptoms: The clinical picture of Fanconi anemia is heterogeneous, characterized by congenital anomalies, bone marrow failure, and an increased risk of malignancy. Physical anomalies include short stature, skeletal abnormalities in the limbs, and skin blemishes. Secondly, the hallmark feature is progressive bone marrow failure, which usually manifests in the first decade of life with anemia, thrombocytopenia, and leukopenia. Finally, patients have an increased risk of acute myeloid leukemia and certain solid tumors.
449.prevention: The management of AF is complex and requires a multidisciplinary team. It includes strict monitoring with periodic blood tests to monitor bone marrow function and early cancer detection programs. Red blood cell or platelet transfusions may be necessary in certain circumstances. Oral androgen treatment, such as oxymetholone and danazol, can be used to improve blood cell counts, especially red blood cells and platelets. The only curative treatment for bone marrow failure is hematopoietic stem cell transplantation.
449.technical_description:
The FANCG gene is located on chromosome 9 and contains the instructions for making the FANCG protein. Mutations in this gene are the third most common cause of FA, accounting for approximately 10% of all cases. The FANCG protein is a central component of the FA "core" complex, the machinery responsible for DNA repair. It acts as a scaffold, helping to stabilize the complex, and is essential for triggering the first step in the DNA repair pathway when damage is detected.

Most FANCG variants lead to a complete loss of protein function, and in some cases, more severe cytopenia and a higher incidence of leukemia have been observed compared to patients with variants in other FA genes. The most common FANCG types include frameshift variants, nonsense variants, and large deletions.

FA has a higher frequency in the South African population than in other populations due to the presence of the pathogenic variant with a founder effect c.637_643del (p.Tyr213fs. rs587776640). This variant is found in exon 5 of FANCG and produces a truncated protein. Individuals who inherit two copies of c.637_643del typically present with a severe phenotype, including growth problems, kidney abnormalities, skin spots, and very low blood counts. More recently, it has been observed that c.637_643del can cause endocrine disturbances.

The variant c.313G > T (p.Glu105Ter, rs121434425) in exon 4 of FANCG also produces a truncated protein and accounts for 44% of mutated alleles in German FA FANCG patients. Other variants with a founder effect exist in various populations, such as c.307+1G>C (rs200479612) in Korean and Japanese populations, c.511-3_511-2del (rs1491369358) in the Mexican Mixe population, and c.1480+1G>C (rs149616199) in French Arcadians.

450.name: Fanconi Anemia (FANCE gene)
450.description: The FANCE gene encodes one of the proteins that form part of a molecular complex involved in the repair of cross-links between DNA strands and in maintaining normal chromosomal stability.
450.overview: Fanconi anemia (FA) is an autosomal recessive genetic disease caused by a failure in the DNA repair pathway. The FANCE gene (corresponding to complementation group E) is one of the genes involved in this pathway. Mutations in FANCE are considerably less common than those in the FANCA gene, accounting for approximately 3% of pathogenic variants associated with FA.
450.symptoms: The clinical picture of Fanconi anemia is heterogeneous, characterized by congenital anomalies, bone marrow failure, and an increased risk of malignancy. Physical anomalies include short stature, skeletal malformations of the extremities, and skin spots. Secondly, the hallmark is progressive bone marrow failure, which usually manifests in the first decade of life with anemia, thrombocytopenia, and leukopenia. Finally, patients have an increased risk of acute myeloid leukemia and certain solid tumors.
450.prevention: The management of AA is complex and requires a multidisciplinary team. It includes strict monitoring with periodic blood tests to monitor bone marrow function and early cancer detection programs. Red blood cell or platelet transfusions may be necessary in certain circumstances. Oral androgen therapy, such as oxymetholone and danazol, can be used to improve blood cell counts, especially red blood cells and platelets. The only curative treatment for bone marrow failure is hematopoietic stem cell transplantation.
450.technical_description:
The FANCE gene is located on chromosome 6 and contains instructions for making the FANCE protein, which is part of a nuclear multiprotein complex for DNA repair. It acts as a stabilizer within this complex, ensuring that all necessary protein components are in the correct place to repair DNA damage. 

Pathogenic variants of the FANCE gene account for only 3% of FA cases, are rare, and often "private," meaning they are unique to a single individual or family. The clinical presentation for individuals with FANCE mutations is typically that of classic FA, and increased FANCE expression has been observed to be associated with a less favorable prognosis, but a correlation between the type of FANCE mutation and phenotype has not been demonstrated.

456.name: Spastic Paraplegia (SPAST gene)
456.description: Mutations in the SPAST gene are the most common cause of hereditary spastic paraplegia, a disorder characterized by progressive stiffness and weakness in the legs. This condition results from the degeneration of the long nerve fibers of the spinal cord and affects individuals of all populations worldwide.
456.overview:
The SPAST gene contains instructions for making a protein called spastin. This protein is essential for the health and maintenance of nerve cells (neurons), particularly for regulating their internal scaffolding, known as microtubules. Microtubules are involved in transporting vital materials along the entire length of nerve fibers, or axons. Pathogenic variants (mutations) in the SPAST gene alter spastin’s function, disrupting this transport system.

This disruption particularly affects the longest motor neurons in the body, which extend from the brain, down the spinal cord, and control the leg muscles. Over time, these nerve fibers degenerate, leading to the symptoms of spastic paralysis. This specific form, caused by variants in SPAST, is known as spastic paraplegia type 4 (SPG4). The prevalence of all forms of hereditary spastic paraplegia is estimated to be between 1 and 10 cases per 100,000 people. Variants in SPAST are the most frequent cause, accounting for around 40% of autosomal dominant cases.
456.symptoms: The main clinical feature of SPG4 is a slowly progressive spasticity (stiffness) and weakness in the lower extremities, often resulting in a stiff, "scissoring" gait, and difficulty walking. Other common signs include hyperactive reflexes (hyperreflexia) in the legs and urinary urgency. The age of onset is highly variable, ranging from early childhood to late adulthood, although a higher number of cases have been described before 10 years of age or between 30 and 50 years.
456.prevention: Currently, there is no cure for hereditary spastic paraplegia. Therefore, treatment focuses on managing symptoms and maintaining mobility for as long as possible. A multidisciplinary approach, including physical therapy, is essential for stretching spastic muscles and maintaining strength and range of motion. Medications such as baclofen, tizanidine, or botulinum toxin injections may be used to reduce muscle stiffness. As the condition progresses, assistive devices such as ankle-foot orthoses, canes, or wheelchairs may be necessary.
456.technical_description:
Spastic paraplegia related to the SPAST gene is also known as spastic paraplegia type 4 (SPG4). The SPAST gene expresses the protein spastin, which is responsible for regulating the length of microtubules, the fibers that make up the cytoskeleton and are involved in organelle transport and cell division. By regulating microtubule size, they help remodel the cytoskeleton. Spastin is found especially in nerve cells (neurons).

Mutations in the SPAST gene are the most common cause of SPG4. The main pathogenic mechanism is suspected to be haploinsufficiency, meaning that having only one functional copy of the gene (of the two we inherit) is not enough to produce the necessary amount of spastin for normal neuron function. Most pathogenic variants in the SPAST gene affect the AAA domain, impacting protein function. This leads to an alteration in microtubule dynamics, disrupting axonal transport and ultimately causing the degeneration of long motor neuron terminals.

Some notable variants include c.131C>T (p.Ser44Leu) and c.134C>A (p.Pro45Gln), which have been associated with early-onset disease and a severe phenotype. However, disease penetrance is incomplete, meaning that not everyone who inherits a pathogenic mutation in SPAST develops symptoms. Furthermore, penetrance has been observed to be lower in women than in men.

Meyyazhagan A, Orlacchio A. Hereditary Spastic Paraplegia: An Update. Int J Mol Sci. 2022 Feb 1.23(3):1697.

457.name: Spastic Paraplegia (ATL1 gene)
457.description: Mutations in the ATL1 gene are the second most common cause of hereditary spastic paraplegia, a disorder characterized by progressive stiffness and weakness in the lower extremities. This condition is due to the degeneration of long nerve fibers.
457.overview:
The ATL1 gene provides instructions for making a protein called atlastin-1, which is crucial for shaping the internal cellular network known as the endoplasmic reticulum, particularly within the long axons of motor neurons. Proper functioning of atlastin-1 is essential for transporting materials along these nerve cells. Mutations in the ATL1 gene cause spastic paraplegia type 3A (SPG3A), a common form of autosomal dominant hereditary spastic paraplegia (HSP), accounting for about 10% of cases.

Mutations in ATL1 disrupt this transport system, leading to the progressive degeneration of the longest nerve fibers in the spinal cord, a process often described as "dying-back" neuropathy. This damage affects nerve signals to the leg muscles, resulting in spasticity. SPG3A affects individuals worldwide, with a prevalence for all HSPs estimated to be between 1 and 10 in every 100,000 people.
457.symptoms: SPG3A generally presents in early childhood, often before the age of 10, with a slowly progressive course. The main symptoms are spasticity (muscle stiffness) and weakness in the lower extremities, leading to difficulty walking, frequent falls, and an abnormal gait. In most cases, it is a "pure" form of HSP without complications, with symptoms confined to the lower body. Unlike other forms of hereditary spastic paraplegia, affected individuals are less likely to present with urinary urgency and decreased vibratory sensation.
457.prevention: There is currently no cure for SPG3A; management focuses on alleviating symptoms and improving quality of life. A multidisciplinary approach including physical therapy and occupational therapy is essential for maintaining mobility, stretching spastic muscles, and preventing contractures. Medications such as baclofen and tizanidine are used to reduce spasticity. The use of assistive devices such as orthoses or canes is relatively uncommon.
457.technical_description:
Hereditary spastic paraplegia type 3A (SPG3A) is related to variants in the ATL1 gene. This gene contains instructions for producing the atlastin-1 protein, a GTPase essential for neuronal health. Its main function focuses on maintaining the tubular network of the endoplasmic reticulum, an organelle key for protein and lipid synthesis. This correct functioning is especially critical for the maintenance of axons, the long neuronal extensions that transmit nerve impulses.

Mutations in the ATL1 gene are the cause of SPG3A, one of the most common autosomal dominant forms of hereditary spastic paraplegia. Different pathogenic variants have been identified in ATL1, with most being missense mutations. These mutations, which cause the change of one amino acid for another, are frequently located in the GTPase domain of the protein, affecting its function. Some examples of these variants are c.470T>G (p.Leu157Trp) and c.715C>T (p.Arg239Cys), both located in the functional GTPase domain of the protein.

459.name: Spastic Paraplegia with Pelizaeus-Merzbacher Disease (PLP1 gene)
459.description: Pathogenic variants in the PLP1 gene, located on the X chromosome, alter myelin formation in the central nervous system. This causes a spectrum of neurological disorders that include a variety of phenotypes, ranging from Pelizaeus-Merzbacher disease to Spastic Paraplegia type 2. Being an X-chromosome-linked condition, it primarily affects males and is characterized by progressive motor dysfunction and, at times, cognitive impairment.
459.overview:
The PLP1 gene contains the instructions for producing proteolipid protein 1, the most abundant component of the myelin sheath in the central nervous system. Myelin is a fatty substance that insulates nerve fibers, allowing for the rapid transmission of nerve impulses. Pathogenic variants in PLP1 alter this protein, leading to demyelination, i.e., a loss of myelin. This causes a spectrum of X-linked neurological disorders.

This spectrum ranges from the severe form, Pelizaeus-Merzbacher disease (PMD), to the milder form, Spastic Paraplegia type 2 (SPG2). PMD affects approximately 1 in 400,000 individuals, especially males. Since the gene is on the X chromosome, these conditions primarily affect males, while carrier females are usually asymptomatic or have mild symptoms in adulthood. The severity of the disease depends directly on the type of genetic alteration in the PLP1 gene.
459.symptoms: Symptoms vary by severity. The classic form of Pelizaeus-Merzbacher disease usually appears in the first year of life with nystagmus (involuntary eye movements), hypotonia (low muscle tone), and delayed motor development, progressing to spasticity and ataxia. The milder Spastic Paraplegia type 2 manifests in late childhood or adulthood with progressive weakness and stiffness in the legs, generally without significant cognitive impairment. In the case of Spastic Paraplegia, the estimated life expectancy is normal.
459.prevention: Currently, there is no cure and the management of disorders related to PLP1 requires a multidisciplinary team that includes specialists in neurology, orthopedics, physical therapy, and gastroenterology. Treatment focuses on symptoms and may include anti-epileptic drugs for seizures and comprehensive management of spasticity with physical therapy, medications (baclofen, diazepam), and surgery for contractures. Specialized education and assistive communication devices are of great benefit to patients.
459.technical_description:
Disorders related to the PLP1 gene, such as spastic paraplegia and Pelizaeus-Merzbacher disease, are a group of genetic diseases that affect myelin, the insulating layer that surrounds neurons in the central nervous system and is essential for the proper transmission of nerve impulses. The affected gene, PLP1, expresses proteolipid protein 1, a fundamental component of this myelin sheath. Due to its location on the X chromosome, the inheritance of these genetic variants is typically passed from carrier mothers to their sons.

Genetic variants in PLP1 alter myelin through different mechanisms. The most common cause is gene duplication, which leads to an overproduction of the PLP1 protein, proving toxic to oligodendrocytes and causing their death. Other variants, such as missense variants, generate a misfolded protein that accumulates in the cell's endoplasmic reticulum, triggering a stress response that also leads to cell death. On the other hand, gene deletion or variants causing loss of function, although initially leading to milder myelin defects, can result in more severe axonal damage over time. Variants in non-coding (intronic) regions that alter gene expression have even been identified and have been linked to hypomyelination of early myelinated structures, as is the case with c.453+164G>A.

467.name: Hereditary Cancer (PALB2 gene)
467.description: Pathogenic variants in the PALB2 gene, a crucial collaborator of the well-known BRCA2 gene, affect the cell's ability to repair DNA damage. This malfunction significantly increases an individual's risk of developing several types of cancer throughout their lifetime, particularly breast, pancreatic, and ovarian cancer.
467.overview:
The PALB2 (Partner and Localizer of BRCA2) gene expresses a protein that acts as a tumor suppressor. It works directly with the BRCA2 protein to repair damaged DNA, a crucial process for preventing uncontrolled cell growth that can lead to cancer. When an individual inherits a pathogenic variant in one of the two copies of PALB2, this DNA repair mechanism is compromised, increasing their susceptibility to cancer. Breast cancer rates in individuals with pathogenic variants in PALB2 are similar to those associated with individuals carrying mutations in BRCA2. However, individuals with variants in both PALB2 and BRCA2 have a higher risk of triple-negative breast cancer.

Carriers of a pathogenic variant in PALB2 have a significantly elevated risk of cancer, particularly breast cancer in women (lifetime risk of 20%-60%) and also in men. The risks of pancreatic and ovarian cancer also increase notably. The prevalence rate of pathogenic PALB2 variants is estimated to be between 0.3% and 1.8% in countries worldwide. However, founder variants have been described in Finnish, Polish, Canadian, Russian, Argentinian, Greek, Italian, Australian, and Hispanic populations.
467.symptoms: Being a carrier of a pathogenic variant in PALB2 does not cause daily symptoms. Instead, the main manifestation is the development of cancer, often at an earlier age than is typical in the general population. The most common indicators are a personal or family history of breast cancer (including triple-negative breast cancer), pancreatic, or ovarian cancer, particularly if diagnosed before age 50. Furthermore, individuals with two altered copies of the PALB2 gene have also been shown to develop a subtype of Fanconi anemia (FA-N).
467.prevention: Management for individuals with a pathogenic variant in PALB2 focuses on intensive cancer surveillance and risk reduction strategies. This includes earlier and more frequent screening, such as annual mammograms and breast MRIs starting at age 30. In men with pathogenic variants, screening for breast cancer with annual mammograms starting at age 50 may be considered. Risk-reducing surgeries, such as prophylactic mastectomy or salpingo-oophorectomy (removal of ovaries and fallopian tubes), may be considered. For cancers that do develop, targeted therapies like poly (ADP-ribose) polymerase (PARP) inhibitors may be effective, as they exploit the underlying DNA repair deficiency.
467.technical_description:
Hereditary cancer associated with PALB2 is a condition that significantly increases the risk of developing certain types of cancer throughout life. The PALB2 gene is a fundamental tumor suppressor, whose protein acts as an indispensable partner to BRCA2 in repairing DNA damage. When a person inherits a faulty copy of this gene, the genetic maintenance system becomes inefficient, allowing errors in the DNA to accumulate and increase the probability of developing cancer.

Some of the genetic variants that have been linked to hereditary cancer are more common in specific populations due to a founder effect, such as the c.1592delT variant in the Finnish population or the c.3113G>A in the Australian population, making them key genetic markers for people with those ancestries. On the other hand, the c.751C>T variant has been frequently described in Asian populations, while c.172_175delTTGT, c.2323C>T, and c.3549C>G have been described in European, American, and Australian populations.

Finally, it is important to highlight the genotype-phenotype correlation: while inheriting a single mutated copy predisposes to cancer, inheriting two faulty copies causes a rare and severe disease called Fanconi anemia (FANCN), demonstrating how genetic dose can drastically change the condition.
467.technical_citations:
PDQ Cancer Genetics Editorial Board. PALB2: Cancer Risks and Management (PDQ®): Health Professional Version. 2025 Feb 14. In: PDQ Cancer Information Summaries [Internet]. Bethesda (MD): National Cancer Institute (US). 2002–.

  Wu S, Zhou J, Zhang K, et al. Molecular Mechanisms of PALB2 Function and Its Role in Breast Cancer Management. Front Oncol. 2020 Feb 28.10:301.

471.name: Dilated Cardiomyopathy 1D (TNNT2 gene)
471.description: Dilated cardiomyopathy 1D (CMD1D) is a heart muscle disease caused by mutations in the TNNT2 gene. These mutations affect the function of a key protein in heart contraction, which can lead to progressive heart failure.
471.overview: Dilated cardiomyopathy 1D (CMD1D) is a genetic heart muscle disease characterized by the thinning and stretching of the left ventricle walls, which reduces the heart's ability to pump blood efficiently. This condition is inherited in an autosomal dominant manner, meaning that a single copy of a mutated gene is sufficient to cause the disease. The TNNT2 gene encodes cardiac troponin T, a protein essential for heart muscle contraction. Mutations in this gene alter the function of troponin T, weakening the ability of heart muscle cells to contract properly. This leads to a gradual dilation of the ventricle and, over time, to heart failure. The incidence of DCM in the general population is approximately 1 in 2500 people, and mutations in TNNT2 are estimated to account for about 3-5% of familial DCM cases. While DCM can affect individuals of any ethnic background, specific populations with a higher prevalence of certain mutations have been observed, highlighting the importance of family history and genetic variability.
471.symptoms: CMD1D symptoms usually appear in adolescence or early adulthood. The main symptoms include shortness of breath (dyspnea), especially during exercise, extreme fatigue, swelling in the ankles and feet (edema), and palpitations.
471.prevention: The management of dilated cardiomyopathy focuses on treating symptoms and preventing complications. Pharmacological treatments include angiotensin-converting enzyme (ACE) inhibitors, beta-blockers, and diuretics. In cases, devices such as pacemakers or defibrillators may be needed, and ultimately, a heart transplant.
471.technical_description:
Dilated cardiomyopathy is a heterogeneous disease that can be caused by multiple factors. In 30-50% of cases, it is of genetic origin and more than 50 associated genes have been identified, with TNNT2 being one of the main ones and responsible for subtype 1D.

CMD1D is inherited in an autosomal dominant pattern, although penetrance is reduced, meaning that not all carrier individuals will develop the disease.

Variants in the TNNT2 gene tend to cluster in important regions involved in the stabilization of the sarcomere structure or the regulation of cardiac contraction. To date, dozens of associated mutations of varying natures have been described.

Missense variants: these are common and cause the alteration of a single nucleotide in troponin. An example of this is p.Arg141Trp (c.421C>T, rs74315380), one of the most studied and associated with a high risk of disease progression.Splicing variants: these affect the messenger RNA processing, leading to a truncated or defective protein. An example of this is c.851+1G>C (rs111377893).Nonsense variants: these introduce a premature stop codon, generating an incomplete and non-functional protein. This can have a very significant impact on cardiac contraction. An example of this is p.Trp297Ter (c.891G>A, rs730881116).
471.technical_citations:
Eldemire R, Mestroni L, Taylor MRG. Genetics of Dilated Cardiomyopathy. Annu Rev Med. 2024 Jan 29.75:417-426.

  Keyt LK, Duran JM, Bui QM, et al. Thin filament cardiomyopathies: A review of genetics, disease mechanisms, and emerging therapeutics. Front Cardiovasc Med. 2022 Sep 7.9:972301.

  Hershberger RE, Jordan E. Dilated Cardiomyopathy Overview. 2007 Jul 27 [Updated 2024 Dec 12]. In: Adam MP, Feldman J, Mirzaa GM, et al., editors. GeneReviews® [Internet].
