472.name: Dilated Cardiomyopathy 1DD (RBM20 gene)
472.description: Dilated cardiomyopathy 1DD (DCM1DD) is a heart muscle disease caused by mutations in the RBM20 gene. These mutations alter a key protein in the regulation of gene expression, leading to progressive cardiac dysfunction.
472.overview: Dilated cardiomyopathy 1DD (DCM1DD) is a form of heart disease characterized by the enlargement and thinning of the heart's chambers, which impairs its ability to pump blood effectively. This condition is inherited in an autosomal dominant pattern, meaning that a single copy of a mutated gene variant is sufficient to cause the disease. The RBM20 gene encodes RNA-binding motif protein 20, a protein that plays a crucial role in the splicing of messenger RNA from cardiac genes, such as that of titin (TTN). Mutations in RBM20 cause defective splicing, leading to the production of abnormal cardiac proteins and the disorganization of the sarcomere structure, the contractile unit of cardiac muscle. This results in the gradual degeneration of heart muscle tissue and subsequent heart failure. The global incidence of DCM is approximately 1 in 2,500 people. Mutations in RBM20 are a relatively common genetic cause in familial cases of DCM, accounting for about 3-5% of hereditary forms. The disease has been identified in various populations without a specific ethnic predisposition, but with strong familial aggregation.
472.symptoms: The symptoms of CMD1DD usually manifest in the second or third decade of life. The main signs include severe cardiac arrhythmias, progressive heart failure, fatigue, shortness of breath (dyspnea), and, in some cases, sudden death.
472.prevention: The management of dilated cardiomyopathy due to mutations in RBM20 focuses on treating symptoms and preventing complications. Treatments include the use of medications such as beta-blockers and diuretics. Due to the high risk of arrhythmias, the implantation of an implantable cardioverter-defibrillator (ICD) is often considered. In stages, a heart transplant may be necessary.
472.technical_description:
Dilated cardiomyopathy 1DD is caused by the presence of mutations in the RBM20 gene and is inherited in an autosomal dominant pattern. This gene encodes for RNA-binding motif protein 20, essential for the correct processing of messenger RNA from the titin gene (TTN), among many others. Titin is fundamental for the elasticity and structure of the sarcomere, the contractile unit of cardiac muscle. The presence of certain variants in the RBM20 gene causes defective processing of titin such that a functional protein is not generated.

The presence of pathogenic variants in the RBM20 gene is associated with a severe phenotype and early-onset disease.

Most pathogenic variants are of the missense type and are concentrated in the region of the protein called the RNA-binding motif, suggesting that the ability to bind messenger RNA is affected. One of the most studied variants is p.Arg636His (c.1907G>A, rs267607004), which causes aberrant splicing of titin and other sarcomeric proteins.

Although much less frequent, small insertions and deletions associated with the pathology have also been described.
472.technical_citations:
Eldemire R, Mestroni L, Taylor MRG. Genetics of Dilated Cardiomyopathy. Annu Rev Med. 2024 Jan 29.75:417-426.

473.name: Dilated Cardiomyopathy 1E (SCN5A gene)
473.description: Dilated cardiomyopathy 1E (CMD1E) is a heart disease caused by mutations in the SCN5A gene. These mutations affect an ion channel crucial for heart rhythm, which can lead to progressive heart failure and severe arrhythmias.
473.overview: Dilated cardiomyopathy 1E (CMD1E) is a disease of the heart muscle characterized by progressive enlargement and weakening of the heart chambers, reducing their ability to pump blood effectively. This condition is inherited in an autosomal dominant manner, meaning that a single copy of the mutated gene is sufficient to cause the disease. The SCN5A gene encodes the alpha subunit of the cardiac sodium channel (NaV1.5), a vital protein for the generation and propagation of the electrical impulses that control heartbeats. Mutations in this gene can lead to defective sodium channel function, altering the excitability and electrical conduction of the heart. This electrical dysfunction can contribute to the development of arrhythmias, which in turn can cause long-term heart dilation. The incidence of dilated cardiomyopathy in the general population is approximately 1 in 2,500 people. Although mutations in SCN5A are a less frequent cause of DCM compared to other genes, they represent one of the main causes of inherited arrhythmias, and their association with DCM highlights the complex interplay between the electrical and mechanical function of the heart.
473.symptoms: Symptoms of CMD1E can vary, but often include problems with the heart's electrical conduction. The most common symptoms are palpitations, dizziness, shortness of breath (dyspnea), and, in some cases, syncope (loss of consciousness) due to severe arrhythmias.
473.prevention: The management of dilated cardiomyopathy due to mutations in SCN5A focuses on the control of heart failure and the prevention of dangerous arrhythmias. Treatments include medications for heart failure management (beta-blockers, diuretics) and, frequently, the implantation of an implantable cardioverter-defibrillator (ICD) due to the high risk of lethal ventricular arrhythmias.
473.technical_description:
The cause of dilated cardiomyopathy 1E is the presence of mutations in the SCN5A gene, which plays a fundamental role in the electrical conduction of the heart. The presence of loss-of-function variants in this gene, in addition to being related to dilated cardiomyopathy, is associated with Brugada syndrome and long QT syndrome type 3, conditions that predispose to lethal ventricular arrhythmias. On the other hand, there are also gain-of-function mutations associated with CMD1E as they can also generate abnormal electrical activity that overloads heart cells. Pathogenic variants are usually found in specific regions such as P-loops or transmembrane domains, which are essential for ion flow across the cell membrane.

The penetrance of variants associated with CMD1E is highly variable, so not all carriers develop the disease.
473.technical_citations:
Eldemire R, Mestroni L, Taylor MRG. Genetics of Dilated Cardiomyopathy. Annu Rev Med. 2024 Jan 29.75:417-426.

  Keyt LK, Duran JM, Bui QM, et al. Thin filament cardiomyopathies: A review of genetics, disease mechanisms, and emerging therapeutics. Front Cardiovasc Med. 2022 Sep 7.9:972301.

474.name: Dilated Cardiomyopathy 1G (TTN gene)
474.description: Dilated cardiomyopathy 1G (CMD1G) is a heart disease caused by mutations in the TTN gene. These genetic variants affect titin, a protein essential for the structure and contractile function of the heart muscle, leading to a progressive weakening of the heart.
474.overview: Dilated cardiomyopathy 1G (CMD1G) is a heart muscle disorder that causes the heart's chambers to enlarge and weaken, decreasing its ability to pump blood efficiently. The most frequent cause of this condition is mutations in the TTN (titin) gene. This gene is the largest known in the human genome and encodes for the titin protein, which acts as a molecular scaffold within heart muscle cells. Titin is essential for the structure of the sarcomere, the contractile unit of muscle, and is crucial for maintaining the elasticity and strength of the heart during systole and diastole. Mutations in TTN are typically autosomal dominant, meaning that inheriting a single copy of an abnormal genetic variant can cause the disease. These mutations often result in a truncated version of the titin protein, which is less functional or unstable. The incidence of CMD in general is approximately 1 in 2500 people. Mutations in TTN are the most common genetic cause of CMD, accounting for between 20% and 25% of familial cases and around 18% of sporadic cases. The disease presents globally in all populations.
474.symptoms: The symptoms of CMD1G usually manifest in adulthood, although the age of onset can vary widely. The main symptoms include fatigue, shortness of breath (dyspnea), especially during exercise, swelling in the legs or feet, and palpitations.
474.prevention: The management of dilated cardiomyopathy due to mutations in TTN focuses on controlling the symptoms of heart failure and preventing complications. Pharmacological treatment includes beta-blockers, angiotensin-converting enzyme (ACE) inhibitors, and diuretics. In cases, device implantation such as pacemakers or defibrillators may be required, and in critical situations, a heart transplant may be the only option.
474.technical_description:
Dilated cardiomyopathy is a heterogeneous disease with a percentage of cases whose origin is genetic. Of all the genetic causes, mutations in the TTN gene are the most frequent, responsible for 20% of cases. The TTN gene is the largest in the human genome and encodes titin, a protein of structural function of the sarcomere, the contractile unit of cardiac muscle. It has 364 exons and a molecular weight of 4200kDa.

Most of the mutations described in the TTN gene are loss-of-function, producing a defective protein or insufficient quantities. This weakens the structure of the sarcomere, reduces the force of contraction, and leads to progressive dilation of the ventricle. To date, more than 500 mutations have been described in the TTN gene, which are associated with disease onset in adulthood and high variability in penetrance. The severity and onset of the disease can be variable even among members of the same family carrying the same mutation.

Among the described mutations, truncating variants are the most common and studied, being the main genetic cause of DCM. They are found throughout the entire gene, but are especially frequent in the A-band region, crucial for providing stability to the sarcomere.
474.technical_citations:
Jolfayi, A.G., Kohansal, E., Ghasemi, S, et al. Exploring TTN variants as genetic insights into cardiomyopathy pathogenesis and potential emerging clues to molecular mechanisms in cardiomyopathies. Sci Rep 14, 5313 (2024).

475.name: Dilated Cardiomyopathy 1HH (BAG3 gene)
475.description: Dilated cardiomyopathy 1HH (CMD1HH) is a heart disease caused by mutations in the BAG3 gene. These mutations affect a protein that protects the heart from stress, which can lead to progressive weakening of the heart muscle and the development of heart failure.
475.overview: Dilated cardiomyopathy 1HH (DCM1HH) is a genetic heart muscle condition that causes the heart chambers to enlarge and become thinner, losing their strength to pump blood efficiently. This condition is inherited in an autosomal dominant manner, meaning that a single copy of an abnormal gene variant can cause the disease. The BAG3 (BCL2-Associated AthanoGene 3) gene encodes a protein that plays a crucial role in the heart's response to cellular stress, helping to clear damaged proteins and maintain the structure of the heart muscle. Mutations in BAG3 can compromise this protective function, leading to an accumulation of defective proteins that disrupt the structure of heart cells. This results in gradual degeneration and weakening of heart muscle tissue, leading to chamber dilation and, over time, heart failure. The incidence of DCM in the general population is approximately 1 in 2,500 people. Mutations in BAG3 are a relatively rare cause of DCM, but are associated with a form of the disease that often presents with rapid and severe progression, affecting people from diverse populations worldwide.
475.symptoms: The symptoms of CMD1HH usually appear in adulthood, with an onset that can be relatively early. The most common signs include muscle weakness (myopathy) along with fatigue, shortness of breath (dyspnea), and swelling of the lower extremities due to heart failure.
475.prevention: The management of dilated cardiomyopathy due to mutations in BAG3 focuses on symptom stabilization and delaying progression. Pharmacological treatment includes standard medications for heart failure, such as beta-blockers and ACE inhibitors. Due to the severity and rapid progression of the disease, patients often require the implantation of devices for arrhythmia management, and many end up needing a heart transplant.
475.technical_description:
Dilated cardiomyopathy is a heart disease with remarkable genetic variability. CMD1HH is a rare but particularly severe form of the disease and is caused by mutations in the BAG3 gene, which encodes a molecular chaperone that aids in the folding of other proteins. 

Mutations in the BAG3 gene are associated with an aggressive clinical phenotype and usually progress rapidly to heart failure. Furthermore, the penetrance of these variants is high, and they are often also associated with myopathy or weakness of the cardiac muscle. Most of the identified variants are of the missense type or frameshift type, such as the p.Ser268Ter, p.Ile337fs, or p.Glu455Lys variants.
475.technical_citations:
Eldemire R, Mestroni L, Taylor MRG. Genetics of Dilated Cardiomyopathy. Annu Rev Med. 2024 Jan 29.75:417-426.

  Keyt LK, Duran JM, Bui QM, et al. Thin filament cardiomyopathies: A review of genetics, disease mechanisms, and emerging therapeutics. Front Cardiovasc Med. 2022 Sep 7.9:972301.

476.name: Dilated Cardiomyopathy 1I (DES gene)
476.description: Dilated cardiomyopathy 1I (CMD1I) is a heart disease caused by mutations in the DES gene. These mutations affect the desmin protein, a vital structural component of the heart muscle, leading to progressive weakening and the development of heart failure.
476.overview: Dilated cardiomyopathy 1I (CMD1I) is a genetic disorder that affects the heart muscle, causing its chambers to enlarge and weaken, reducing its ability to pump blood effectively. This condition is inherited in an autosomal dominant pattern, meaning that only one copy of the altered gene is necessary for the disease to manifest. The DES gene encodes the desmin protein, an intermediate filament that is crucial for the organization and structural stability of muscle cells. Desmin connects the sarcomeres (the contractile units of muscle) to each other and to the cell membrane, forming a network that maintains the mechanical integrity of the heart muscle. When the DES gene is mutated, defective desmin cannot form this network properly, leading to disorganization of the muscle structure and accumulation of damaged proteins. This process results in progressive muscle damage, culminating in the dilation of the heart chambers and heart failure. The incidence of CMD in the general population is approximately 1 in 2,500 people. Mutations in DES are a rare cause of CMD, but their impact is significant, and the disease is usually associated with skeletal muscle weakness in addition to cardiac involvement.
476.symptoms: The symptoms of CMD1I often manifest in adulthood, and can include both cardiac and muscular involvement. The main symptoms are fatigue, shortness of breath (dyspnea), skeletal muscle weakness (myopathy), and electrical conduction problems in the heart.
476.prevention: The management of dilated cardiomyopathy due to mutations in DES focuses on treating the symptoms of heart failure and associated myopathy. Pharmacological treatment includes standard heart failure medications, such as beta-blockers and ACE inhibitors. Due to electrical conduction involvement, a pacemaker may be necessary. Treatment for myopathy is primarily supportive.
476.technical_description:
Dilated cardiomyopathy (DCM) is a heart disease with a diverse genetic etiology. DCM1I is a particular form of the disease caused by mutations in the DES gene, which are inherited in an autosomal dominant pattern. This gene encodes desmin, a type of intermediate filament that is crucial for maintaining the structure and mechanical integrity of muscle cells, both in the heart and in skeletal muscles.

Mutations in the DES gene often lead to misfolding of the protein, which tends to aggregate and accumulate inside the cell. This causes progressive damage that leads to heart dilation and muscle weakness. Desmin-related diseases are called desminopathies.

Eldemire R, Mestroni L, Taylor MRG. Genetics of Dilated Cardiomyopathy. Annu Rev Med. 2024 Jan 29.75:417-426.

477.name: Dilated Cardiomyopathy 1S (MYH7 gene)
477.description: Dilated cardiomyopathy, like other cardiac pathologies, can be caused by the presence of pathogenic variants in the MYH7 gene, which encodes a motor protein essential for cardiac muscle contraction.
477.overview:
Dilated cardiomyopathy type 1S is a genetic form of familial cardiomyopathy caused by pathogenic variants in the MYH7 gene. This gene contains instructions for producing the beta-myosin heavy chain, a motor protein that is a fundamental component of the sarcomere, the basic contractile unit of cardiac muscle cells. Beta-myosin plays a direct role in generating the force needed for the heart to contract and pump blood to the rest of the body.

Certain pathogenic variants in the MYH7 gene alter the structure or function of this protein, resulting in ineffective muscle contraction (hypocontractility). This intrinsic contractile weakness leads to a progressive enlargement or "dilation" of the heart chambers, particularly the left ventricle, in an attempt to compensate for the lack of force.
477.symptoms: The symptoms are characteristic of congestive heart failure and their onset can be gradual. The most common include shortness of breath, especially with exertion, persistent fatigue, decreased exercise tolerance, and swelling in the legs, ankles, and feet. Palpitations or syncope (fainting) may also occur due to arrhythmias. The age of onset of symptoms is highly variable, potentially manifesting from childhood to late adulthood.
477.prevention: The management of CMD1S focuses on relieving the symptoms of heart failure, slowing disease progression, and preventing complications such as arrhythmias. Treatment is based on standard medical therapy guided by clinical guidelines, which includes drugs such as beta-blockers and ACE inhibitors. In some patients, the implantation of an automatic defibrillator is recommended, and a heart transplant may become necessary.
477.technical_description: The MYH7 gene is one of the most common genetic causes of inherited cardiomyopathies, and although it is best known for causing hypertrophic cardiomyopathy, a specific subset of its mutations consistently leads to the dilated phenotype. The condition is inherited in an autosomal dominant manner, meaning that a single altered copy of the gene can cause the heart disease.
477.technical_citations:
de Frutos F, Ochoa JP, Navarro-Peñalver M, et al. Natural History of MYH7-Related Dilated Cardiomyopathy. J Am Coll Cardiol. 2022 Oct 11.80(15):1447-1461.

478.name: Dilated Cardiomyopathy 1Z (TNNC1 gene)
478.description: Dilated cardiomyopathy 1Z is a heart muscle disease caused by mutations in the TNNC1 gene. This gene is vital for heart contraction, and its alterations cause the heart to enlarge and weaken, leading to progressive heart failure and arrhythmias.
478.overview:
Dilated cardiomyopathy type 1Z (DCM1Z) is a rare form of inherited cardiomyopathy caused by defects in the TNNC1 gene. This gene encodes cardiac troponin C, a crucial protein in the troponin complex. This complex, additionally composed of troponin I and T, functions as a calcium sensor that regulates the contraction and relaxation of heart muscle cells. When calcium binds to troponin C, a series of conformational changes are triggered, allowing actin and myosin proteins to interact, generating the force necessary for the heartbeat. The heart, unable to pump blood efficiently, progressively dilates and weakens, especially in the left ventricle, which is characteristic of dilated cardiomyopathy.

The condition is inherited in an autosomal dominant manner, meaning a single defective copy of the TNNC1 gene is sufficient to cause the disease, which affects both men and women equally. The global prevalence of familial dilated cardiomyopathy is approximately 1 in 2,500 people, but cases attributable to the TNNC1 gene are infrequent within this group.
478.symptoms: The symptoms of CMD1Z are those of heart failure and may take years to appear. They include shortness of breath, especially during physical activity, extreme fatigue, weakness, swelling in the legs and feet, and reduced exercise capacity. Arrhythmias are also common, which can cause strong palpitations, dizziness, or fainting. The age of onset is variable, and the severity of symptoms can differ even among members of the same family.
478.prevention: The current treatment for CMD1Z focuses on controlling the symptoms of heart failure, slowing the progression of the disease, and preventing complications with healthy lifestyle habits. There is no specific cure, so standard pharmacological therapies such as beta-blockers, angiotensin-converting enzyme inhibitors, and diuretics are used. In patients at high risk of severe ventricular arrhythmias, devices may be used for arrhythmia management. In stages of the disease, a heart transplant may become necessary.
478.technical_description:
Familial dilated cardiomyopathy (DCM) causes dilation of the ventricles associated with a decrease in left ventricular ejection fraction. Compared to hypertrophic cardiomyopathy, there is a greater variety of genes involved, both sarcomeric and non-sarcomeric, that lead to DCM. Although DCM is less common.

Myocardial contractile dysfunction has been shown to be a consequence of pathogenic variants in the TNNC1 gene that modify the affinity of troponin C for calcium. This dysfunction has been associated with both dilated and hypertrophic cardiomyopathy phenotypes.

The present analysis focuses on three variants of clinical interest: c.23C>T (p.Ala8Val), c.91G>T (p.Ala31Ser), and c.168G>C (p.Glu56Asp). These variants have been observed in patients with both pathologies.

481.name: Familial Arrhythmogenic Right Ventricular Cardiomyopathy
481.description: Familial arrhythmogenic right ventricular cardiomyopathy (ARVC) is a heart muscle disease caused by mutations in genes such as PKP2, DSP, and others, which are crucial for maintaining the structural integrity of the heart. This condition leads to the replacement of heart muscle with fatty and fibrous tissue.
481.overview:
Familial arrhythmogenic right ventricular cardiomyopathy is a genetic disorder of the myocardium characterized by the progressive replacement of ventricular muscle tissue with fibroadipose tissue. Although it primarily affects the right ventricle, the left ventricle can also be affected. Pathogenic variants (mutations) in genes such as PKP2, DSP, DSG2, DSC2, and TMEM43 are the underlying cause in most cases. Most of these genes encode desmosomal proteins, which are essential components of the junctions that hold cardiac muscle cells (cardiomyocytes) together. Mutations in these genes compromise the integrity of these junctions, leading to the death of cardiomyocytes, especially under mechanical stress such as exercise.

As the disease progresses, the damaged heart muscle is replaced by fibrous and adipose tissue. This structural change weakens the heart wall and alters normal electrical conduction, creating a substrate for dangerous ventricular arrhythmias. ARVC is estimated to affect between 1 in 2,000 and 1 in 5,000 people in European countries, such as Italy and Germany, being more prevalent in men than in women. Likewise, this condition is responsible for sudden cardiac deaths in young people and athletes.
481.symptoms: The clinical presentation of ARVC is highly variable and symptoms usually first appear in adolescence or early adulthood (between 20 and 40 years of age). Individuals may experience ventricular and supraventricular arrhythmias, palpitations, fainting (syncope), or dizziness, especially during or after physical activity. Some may report chest pain, difficulty breathing, or swelling in the lower extremities or abdomen. Unfortunately, in some individuals, sudden cardiac death is the first and only manifestation of the disease.
481.prevention: The management of ARVD focuses on preventing sudden cardiac death and controlling symptoms. Restriction from participation in competitive and endurance sports is a crucial lifestyle modification. Medications such as beta-blockers are used to reduce heart rate and the risk of arrhythmias. For individuals at high risk for life-threatening arrhythmias, implantation of an implantable cardioverter-defibrillator (ICD) is the most effective therapy. In cases with severe heart failure, heart transplantation may be considered.
481.technical_description:
Arrhythmogenic right ventricular cardiomyopathy (ARVC) is a hereditary heart disease characterized by the progressive replacement of healthy heart muscle tissue, mainly in the right ventricle, with fibroadipose tissue. This structural alteration weakens the heart muscle and interferes with the heart's electrical signals, leading to ventricular arrhythmias and an increased risk of sudden death, especially in young people and athletes. The underlying cause of the disease lies in genetic defects affecting desmosomes, crucial protein structures that act as an intercellular glue, holding cardiac muscle cells together.

The genes involved in ARVC are, for the most part, responsible for encoding these desmosomal proteins. The PKP2 gene, which encodes plakophilin-2, is the most commonly affected and is identified in up to 46% of genetically confirmed cases. Other important desmosomal genes include DSP (desmoplakin), DSG2 (desmoglein-2), and DSC2 (desmocollin-2), mutations in which also prevent proper adhesion between cardiac cells, making them vulnerable to the mechanical stress of the heartbeat. Less commonly, the disease can be caused by mutations in non-desmosomal genes, such as TMEM43, which encodes a protein of the inner nuclear membrane and demonstrates that heart integrity depends on a complex network of protein interactions beyond simple cell adhesion.

ARVC is inherited, in most cases, in an autosomal dominant manner. This means that a single copy of a mutated gene is sufficient to cause the disease. However, there are exceptions, such as the case of the DSC2 and DSG2 genes, which, on rare occasions, have also been linked to an autosomal recessive form of the disease. For example, the case of a founder variant in DSC2 found in the Hutterite population (c.1660C>T), where cases requiring two mutated copies of the gene have been described.

Genetic variants that cause ARVC typically lead to loss of function, meaning they prevent the production of a functional protein. ARVC due to pathogenic variants in PKP2 or DSP has been correlated with a higher risk of fatal arrhythmic events.

Arrhythmogenic right ventricular cardiomyopathy. MedlinePlus. National Library of medicine (NIH) [February 2018].

483.name: X-linked Congenital Stationary Night Blindness
483.description: X-linked congenital stationary night blindness is a non-progressive genetic disorder of the retina, caused by variants in the NYX or CACNA1F genes that alter communication between photoreceptor cells. This condition, present from birth and primarily affecting men, is characterized by severe difficulty seeing in low light conditions, often accompanied by myopia and reduced visual acuity.
483.overview:
X-linked congenital stationary night blindness (CSNB) affects the retina, causing difficulty seeing in low light (night blindness) and other vision problems. It is considered a non-progressive retinal disorder as symptoms do not worsen over time.

CSNB is caused by pathogenic variants in genes that are key for signal transmission from photoreceptors (rods and cones) to the bipolar cells of the retina, a process essential for night vision. There are two forms of the disease: "complete" and "incomplete." Both share similar signs and symptoms, but the main difference is that the incomplete form does not always present with night blindness.

As it is an X-linked disorder, it predominantly affects men. A male who has this type of mutation will present with the disease, as he only has one X chromosome. However, a female who has one copy of a mutation is generally not affected. The estimated prevalence is 1 in 100,000 men, and it has been reported in populations worldwide without a specific ethnic predilection.
483.symptoms: The main symptom is nyctalopia or night blindness, a marked difficulty seeing in dim light that is present from birth. Affected children have difficulty moving around in dimly lit rooms. Other common signs accompanying the disease include myopia (usually moderate to high), nystagmus (involuntary, rhythmic eye movements), strabismus (eye misalignment), and reduced visual acuity that does not improve with glasses.
483.prevention: Currently, there is no cure to restore night vision in patients with CSNB. Management of the condition focuses on correcting refractive errors and treating associated symptoms to maximize daytime visual function. Glasses or contact lenses are prescribed to correct myopia. In some cases, the use of lenses with colored filters may help reduce glare and improve visual comfort during the day. Regular ophthalmological follow-up is essential to monitor eye health.
483.technical_description:
The NYX gene encodes the protein nyctalopin, which acts as a key "organizer" at the synapse between photoreceptors and "ON" bipolar cells, which are those activated by low light. Nyctalopin ensures that the correct receptors are in the right place to receive the signal from the rods. Pathogenic variants in NYX severely disrupt the transmission of the night vision signal.

The CACNA1F gene produces the CaV1.4 calcium channel, located in the photoreceptor membrane and involved in phototransduction signaling. Mutations in CACNA1F alter the communication of both rods and cones.
483.technical_citations:
Hassan A, Mir YR, Kuchay RAH.Ocular findings and genomics of X-linked recessive disorders: A review. Indian J Ophthalmol. 2022 Jul.70(7):2386-2396.

 MacDonald IM, Hoang S, Tuupanen S. X-Linked Congenital Stationary Night Blindness. 2008 Jan 16 [updated 2019 Jul 3]. In: Adam MP, Feldman J, Mirzaa GM, Pagon RA, Wallace SE, Amemiya A, editors. GeneReviews® [Internet]. Seattle (WA): University of Washington, Seattle. 1993–2025.

 Sadeh TT, Black GC, Manson F. A Review of Genetic and Physiological Disease Mechanisms Associated With Cav1 Channels: Implications for Incomplete Congenital Stationary Night Blindness Treatment. Front Genet. 2021 Jan 28.12:637780.

485.name: Congenital Stationary Night Blindness (Oguchi Disease)
485.description: Oguchi disease is a rare form of congenital stationary night blindness, of autosomal recessive inheritance, caused by variants in the SAG or GRK1 genes. These genes are crucial for turning off the light signaling cascade in the retinal rods, and their failure causes extremely slow adaptation to darkness. Its most distinctive clinical sign is the Mizuo-Nakamura phenomenon: a change in the color of the fundus that disappears after a long period in the dark.
485.overview:
Oguchi disease is a type of congenital stationary night blindness (CSNB) that was first described by Dr. Chuta Oguchi in 1907. This form of night blindness specifically affects the function of the rods, the photoreceptor cells responsible for vision in low-light conditions. 

The condition is caused by pathogenic variants in both alleles (autosomal recessive inheritance) of genes that "turn off" the visual signal. The SAG gene encodes arrestin-1 and the GRK1 gene encodes rhodopsin kinase, two key proteins for the deactivation of rhodopsin after it has been activated by light. When these proteins do not function correctly, the rods remain in a prolonged state of activation, preventing them from recovering and adapting to darkness.

Although it is an extremely rare disease worldwide, it has a notably higher prevalence in the Japanese population.
485.symptoms: The main symptom is a non-progressive night blindness (nyctalopia) that manifests from infancy. The characteristic clinical sign of the disease is the Mizuo-Nakamura phenomenon. In light conditions, the fundus shows an unusual golden to gray-whitish coloration. After a prolonged period of dark adaptation (3 to 4 hours), this coloration disappears and the fundus returns to its normal appearance, a change that is reversible upon re-exposure to light. In addition, patients present with an altered electroretinogram (ERG). Otherwise, daytime visual acuity, visual field, and color vision are usually completely normal. In elderly patients, it can lead to a reduction in visual acuity or a contraction of the visual field.
485.prevention: There is no specific treatment or cure for Oguchi disease. Since the condition is stationary and daytime vision is unaffected, the prognosis is good. Management focuses on educating the patient and their family about the nature of the disease, recommending precautions in low-light environments to prevent accidents. No intensive ophthalmological follow-up is required beyond routine check-ups.
485.technical_description:
The causative mutations have been identified in genes encoding arrestin-1 (SAG gene) responsible for Oguchi disease type 1 and rhodopsin kinase (GRK1 gene) which produces Oguchi type 2. Both proteins are crucial for night vision.

To date, fewer than 100 variants are considered pathogenic for Oguchi disease in ClinVar. One of the best known in Oguchi disease type 1 is c.926del (p.Asn309fs, 1147delA or rs587776778) from the SAG gene, which was discovered in Japanese patients from different families. The GRK1 variant c.923T>C (p.Leu308Pro, rs753470112) is one of the most prominent for Oguchi type 2. This variant affects a phylogenetically conserved region and produces the change of a leucine to a proline at position 308.
485.technical_citations: Colombo L, Abeshi A, Maltese PE, et al. Oguchi type I caused by a homozygous missense variation in the SAG gene. Eur J Med Genet. 2019 Sep.62(9):103548. Dai Y, Sun T. Oguchi's disease: two cases and literature review. J Int Med Res. 2021 May.49(5):3000605211019921. Deng Z, Fan F, Tang D, et al. A compound heterozygous mutation in the S-Antigen Visual Arrestin SAG gene in a Chinese patient with Oguchi type one: a case report. BMC Ophthalmol. 2022 Mar 4.22(1):99. Sergouniotis PI, Davidson AE, Sehmi K, et al. Mizuo-Nakamura phenomenon in Oguchi disease due to a homozygous nonsense mutation in the SAG gene. Eye (Lond). 2011 Aug.25(8):1098-101.

488.name: Rhizomelic Chondrodysplasia Punctata Type 2
488.description: Rhizomelic chondrodysplasia punctata type 2 (RCDP2) is a severe peroxisomal disorder, of autosomal recessive inheritance, caused by variants in the GNPAT gene. This genetic defect prevents the production of plasmalogens, essential lipids, severely affecting bone and neurological development. The disease is characterized by extreme shortening of the proximal limbs (rhizomelia), congenital cataracts, profound intellectual disability, and a very limited life expectancy.
488.overview:
Rhizomelic chondrodysplasia punctata type 2 is one of the most severe forms of skeletal dysplasia and is classified within peroxisome biogenesis disorders. It is inherited in an autosomal recessive manner, meaning an individual must receive a copy of the altered gene from each of their parents to develop the disease. The molecular cause is pathogenic variants in the GNPAT gene, which encodes a key enzyme (GNPAT) for the first step in plasmalogen synthesis.

Plasmalogens are crucial lipids for the structure of cell membranes and the myelination of neurons. Their systemic deficiency causes a cascade of developmental problems. RCDP2 is an extremely rare disease, affecting all populations equally, with devastating consequences from birth, with a life expectancy that rarely exceeds the first decade.
488.symptoms: Affected individuals present a severe and recognizable clinical picture from birth. The most prominent feature is a symmetrical and extreme rhizomelia, meaning a very marked shortening of the bones closest to the trunk (humerus and femur). Other constant findings include bilateral cataracts present at birth or of very early onset, punctate calcifications in the cartilages (visible on radiographs), and distinctive facial features. Neurological development is profoundly affected, with severe intellectual disability, hypotonia, epileptic seizures, and growth delay.
488.prevention: Currently, there is no cure for CLN2 disease. Management is purely palliative and supportive, with the aim of improving the patient's quality of life. A multidisciplinary team is required which includes pediatricians, neurologists, ophthalmologists, orthopedicians, and physiotherapists. Cataract surgery may be performed to allow some visual perception. Nutritional support, often via a gastrostomy tube, is fundamental for feeding. Antiepileptic drugs are used to control seizures and physiotherapy helps manage joint contractures.
488.technical_description:
More than 90% of patients with rhizomelic chondrodysplasia punctata (RCDP) present with pathogenic variants in the gene encoding the peroxisomal transporter or PEX7. Less than 10% of RCDP patients have alterations in the genes encoding one of the two peroxisomal enzymes necessary to initiate plasmalogen synthesis, glyceronephosphate O-acyltransferase (GNPAT) and alkylglyceronephosphate synthase (AGPS).

One of the variants associated with severe clinical manifestations and that produces a drastic reduction in GNPAT enzymatic activity is c.1483delG, a variant that produces a premature stop codon and has been observed in homozygosis. The c.1483delG variant has an allelic frequency in non-Finnish Europeans of 0.00001 according to gnomAD. Biochemically, plasmalogen C16:0 and/or C18:0 levels are the main determinants of RCDP severity.
488.technical_citations:
Fallatah W, Schouten M, Yergeau C, et al. Clinical, biochemical, and molecular characterization of mild (nonclassic) rhizomelic chondrodysplasia punctata. J Inherit Metab Dis. 2021 Jul.44(4):1021-1038.

 Sayed J, Gamal A, Theyab A, et al. Neonatal rhizomelic chondrodysplasia punctata type 2 caused by a novel homozygous variant in the GNPAT gene. Clin Case Rep. 2023 Jun 13.11(6):e7504.

 Itzkovitz B, Jiralerspong S, Nimmo G, et al. Functional characterization of novel mutations in GNPAT and AGPS, causing rhizomelic chondrodysplasia punctata (RCDP) types 2 and 3. Hum Mutat. 2012 Jan.33(1):189-97.

 Luisman T, Smith T, Ritchie S, Malone KE. Genetic epidemiology approach to estimating birth incidence and current disease prevalence for rhizomelic chondrodysplasia punctata. Orphanet J Rare Dis. 2021 Jul 6.16(1):300.

489.name: Rhizomelic Chondrodysplasia Punctata Type 3
489.description: Rhizomelic chondrodysplasia punctata type 3 (RCDP3) is an autosomal recessive inherited peroxisomal disease, caused by variants in the AGPS gene. This gene is vital for plasmalogen synthesis. The disease is defined by extreme shortening of the proximal limbs (rhizomelia), congenital cataracts, punctate calcifications, and neurological disability.
489.overview:
Rhizomelic chondrodysplasia punctata type 3 is a serious genetic disorder that affects the development of multiple body systems. It is inherited in an autosomal recessive manner, requiring an individual to inherit two altered copies of the AGPS gene, one from each parent. The AGPS gene encodes the enzyme alkyl-dihydroxyacetone phosphate synthase, which catalyzes the second essential step in the biosynthesis pathway of plasmalogens, a type of lipid crucial for the integrity of cell membranes and nervous system myelin.

The deficiency of this enzyme leads to a lack of plasmalogens, which explains the severity and multisystemic nature of the disease. From a clinical standpoint, RCDP3 is indistinguishable from RCDP2 (caused by variants in the GNPAT gene), as both genes affect consecutive steps in the same metabolic pathway. It is an extremely rare condition with a poor prognosis.
489.symptoms: The clinical picture is severe and manifests from birth. Symptoms include symmetrical and very marked rhizomelia (severe shortening of humeri and femurs), bilateral congenital cataracts, and the presence of punctate calcifications in the epiphyses of the long bones and spine, visible on neonatal radiographs. Patients present characteristic facial features, hypotonia, significant failure to thrive, and psychomotor delay. Seizures are common. Less severe forms have been described in recent years.
489.prevention: There is no cure for RCDP3. The therapeutic approach is exclusively supportive and aimed at maximizing the patient's quality of life. A multidisciplinary team (pediatrics, neurology, ophthalmology, physiotherapy) coordinates care. Cataract surgery is performed to allow light entry and prevent total blindness. Feeding often requires a gastrostomy tube to ensure adequate caloric intake. Seizures are managed with antiepileptic drugs, and physical therapy is important to minimize joint contractures.
489.technical_description:
Rhizomelic chondrodysplasia punctata type 3 (RCDP3) is a very rare inherited disorder caused by alterations in the AGPS gene, which provides instructions for making the enzyme alkylglycerone phosphate synthase. This enzyme is crucial for the first step in the production of plasmalogens, a type of lipid essential for the proper formation and protection of cell membranes, especially in the brain. The deficiency of this enzyme disrupts plasmalogen production, leading to the characteristic signs of the disease, such as shortening of the long bones and other developmental problems.

RCDP3 is an extremely rare peroxisomal disorder that has been detected in some families, with consanguinity. Some of the pathogenic or potentially pathogenic variants in the AGPS gene are: c.1256G>A (p.Arg419His) and c.544C>T (p.Arg182Ter). For the disease to manifest, a person must inherit two altered copies of the AGPS gene, one from each parent (autosomal recessive inheritance).
489.technical_citations:
İli EG, Gezdirici A, Di Pietro E, et al. Expanding the genotypic and phenotypic landscapes of rhizomelic chondrodysplasia punctata type 3 (RCDP3) with two novel families, and a review of the literature. Am J Med Genet A. 2022 Nov.188(11):3229-3235.

 Itzkovitz B, Jiralerspong S, Nimmo G, et al. Functional characterization of novel mutations in GNPAT and AGPS, causing rhizomelic chondrodysplasia punctata (RCDP) types 2 and 3. Hum Mutat. 2012 Jan.33(1):189-97.

490.name: X-linked Recessive Rhizomelic Chondrodysplasia Punctata
490.description: X-linked recessive chondrodysplasia punctata (CDPX1) is a rare skeletal disorder caused by variants in the ARSL gene, which lead to a deficiency of the enzyme arylsulfatase L. Primarily affecting males, it is defined by a triad of symptoms: stippled calcifications in the cartilage, midfacial hypoplasia, and short distal phalanges (fingertips), with a generally favorable prognosis and normal intelligence.
490.overview:
X-linked recessive chondrodysplasia punctata (CDPX1) is a skeletal dysplasia caused by pathogenic variants in the ARSL gene, located on the X chromosome. This gene encodes the enzyme arylsulfatase L. Although its exact function is not fully understood, it is believed to be important for the proper development of cartilage and bones. The deficiency of this enzyme leads to the characteristic features of the disease, which manifests recessively and therefore affects almost exclusively males.

It is crucial to distinguish it from the rhizomelic forms of chondrodysplasia (RCDP), as CDPX1 is not characterized by severe shortening of the arms and thighs. Women who are carriers of a variant in the ARSL gene are usually asymptomatic but have a 50% chance of passing it on to their sons. It is a very rare disease, with cases described in various populations worldwide, although its exact incidence is unknown.
490.symptoms: The clinical picture is based on a classic triad of findings. The first is punctate calcifications (stippled epiphyses) in the cartilage, especially in the heels and spine, which are visible on radiographs during infancy and tend to disappear with age. The second is midfacial hypoplasia, with a depressed nasal bridge and anteverted nares. The third distinguishing feature is hypoplasia of the distal phalanges, resulting in notably short fingers and toes. Unlike other chondrodysplasias, final stature is usually normal or only slightly short, and intellectual development is unaffected.
490.prevention: Management of CDPX1 focuses on symptomatic and supportive treatment by a multidisciplinary team. During infancy, respiratory support may be necessary due to the narrowness of the nasal airways. Orthopedic monitoring is recommended to observe the spine and skeletal development. Some patients may develop ichthyosis (dry, scaly skin), which is managed with emollient creams, as well as hearing loss, which should be evaluated periodically. In general, the long-term prognosis is good, and most affected individuals lead normal lives.
490.technical_description:
X-linked recessive rhizomelic chondrodysplasia punctata (CDPX1) is a genetic bone development disorder whose main characteristics include punctate calcifications in cartilage, midface hypoplasia, and distal phalangeal hypoplasia. Due to its X-linked recessive inheritance pattern, the disease primarily affects men. Women are usually asymptomatic carriers, as their second functional copy of the gene compensates for the defect.

The gene responsible for this condition is ARSL, which encodes the enzyme arylsulfatase L (ARSE). This enzyme is located in the cell's Golgi apparatus and is thought to process sulfate-containing compounds, which are essential for proper cartilage and skeletal development. It is suspected that the disease is caused by a loss of function of the ARSE enzyme, caused by deletions, nonsense, or missense variants in the ARSL gene.
490.technical_citations:
Braverman NE, Bober MB, Brunetti-Pierri N, et al. Chondrodysplasia Punctata 1, X-Linked. 2008 Apr 22 [Updated 2020 Oct 15]. In: Adam MP, Feldman J, Mirzaa GM, et al., editors. GeneReviews® [Internet]. Seattle (WA): University of Washington, Seattle. 1993-2025.

 Brunetti-Pierri N, Andreucci MV, Tuzzi R, et al. X-linked recessive chondrodysplasia punctata: spectrum of arylsulfatase E gene mutations and expanded clinical variability. Am J Med Genet A. 2003 Mar 1.117A(2):164-8.

 Matos-Miranda C, Nimmo G, Williams B, et al. A prospective study of brachytelephalangic chondrodysplasia punctata: identification of arylsulfatase E mutations, functional analysis of novel missense alleles, and determination of potential phenocopies. Genet Med. 2013 Aug.15(8):650-7.

491.name: X-linked Dominant Rhizomelic Chondrodysplasia Punctata
491.description: X-linked chondrodysplasia punctata 2 (CDPX2) is a rare genetic disorder caused by variants in the EBP gene, which is essential for cholesterol metabolism. This alteration leads to abnormal development of the bones, skin, and eyes, primarily affecting women. The condition is highly variable and is characterized by asymmetric skeletal features, distinctive skin lesions, and cataracts.
491.overview:
X-linked chondrodysplasia punctata 2, also known as Conradi-Hünermann-Happle syndrome, is a skeletal dysplasia belonging to a group of diseases characterized by punctate calcifications ("punctata") in the cartilage, visible on newborn radiographs. The cause is pathogenic variants in the EBP gene, located on the X chromosome. This gene encodes an enzyme essential for the final steps of cholesterol biosynthesis, so its deficiency alters the development of multiple tissues.

Being an X-linked dominant inheritance, the disease affects almost exclusively women, manifesting with great clinical variability. In most cases, it is lethal for male fetuses who inherit the variant. The incidence of CDPX2 is estimated between 1 in 40,000 and 1 in 100,000 live births, with no higher prevalence observed in any particular ethnic group.
491.symptoms:
The clinical picture is very heterogeneous. Characteristic findings include skeletal abnormalities such as short stature, scoliosis, asymmetric shortening of the limbs, and the aforementioned punctate calcifications in the long bones and spine. Skin manifestations are very frequent, presenting at birth as a red, scaly rash (ichthyosis) that follows a whorled pattern known as Blaschko lines. Over time, these lesions give way to areas of thinner skin (follicular atrophy) and patchy alopecia. Cataracts are also a common sign and may be present from birth.

Increased concentrations of 8(9)-cholestenol and 8-dehydrocholesterol are characteristic of CDPX2.
491.prevention: Currently, there is no cure for CDPX2. Treatment is symptomatic, focuses on improving the patient's quality of life, and requires a multidisciplinary approach coordinated by geneticists, pediatricians, orthopedists, dermatologists, and ophthalmologists. Orthopedic management is key to correcting limb length discrepancies or scoliosis, and may require surgery. Skin problems are treated with moisturizing creams and keratolytic agents. Cataracts usually require surgical intervention to preserve vision. Regular follow-up is crucial to anticipate and manage potential complications.
491.technical_description:
The accumulation of specific sterols (8(9)-cholestenol and 8DHC) is the main biochemical indicator of CDPX2, caused by a failure in the enzyme EBP (3β-hydroxysteroid-Δ8,Δ7-isomerase or emopamil-binding protein) which is essential for cholesterol biosynthesis. More than 70 pathogenic variants in EBP have been described that can cause CDPX2.

One of the variants that has been identified in several cases of CDPX2 is the missense variant c.440G>A (p.Arg147His, rs28935174). Other types of alterations in EBP have also been found, such as small insertions and deletions, nonsense (e.g. c.328C>T, p.Arg110Ter, rs587783613) and splicing alterations.

Intrafamilial variation in the severity of CDPX2 is probably caused by X chromosome inactivation, and sporadic cases of males with CDPX2 who survive due to functional mosaicism in XXY karyotypes or genetic mosaicism have also been observed.
491.technical_citations:
Cañueto J, Girós M, Ciria S, et al. Clinical, molecular and biochemical characterization of nine Spanish families with Conradi-Hünermann-Happle syndrome: new insights into X-linked dominant chondrodysplasia punctata with a comprehensive review of the literature. Br J Dermatol. 2012 Apr.166(4):830-8.

 Kumble S, Savarirayan R. Chondrodysplasia Punctata 2, X-Linked. 2011 May 31 [Updated 2020 Jan 9]. In: Adam MP, Feldman J, Mirzaa GM, et al., editors. GeneReviews® [Internet]. Seattle (WA): University of Washington, Seattle. 1993-2025.

 Shi X, Lv Y, Jiang Y, Pan P, Cai Y. X-linked dominant chondrodysplasia punctata due to novel mutation in EBP gene: A case report. Medicine (Baltimore). 2025 Apr 4.104(14):e42086.

502.name: Primary Pyruvate Dehydrogenase Complex Deficiency
502.description: Pyruvate dehydrogenase complex deficiency is a rare mitochondrial disorder of carbohydrate metabolism that impairs cellular energy production. This defect leads to a toxic accumulation of lactic acid and neurological damage.
502.overview:
Genetic alterations in the pyruvate dehydrogenase complex (PDC) are one of the most frequent causes of primary lactic acidosis in children. The PDHA1 gene encodes the E1 alpha subunit, which is part of the PDC. This complex plays a crucial role in mitochondria, converting pyruvate, a product of glucose breakdown, into acetyl-CoA for use in the Krebs cycle. This metabolic cycle is essential for generating energy in our cells. Pathogenic variants in PDHA1 affect the body's ability to produce energy from carbohydrates. As a consequence, pyruvate is diverted to an alternative pathway, leading to the accumulation of lactic acid in the blood (lactic acidosis). The brain, which relies heavily on glucose for energy, is particularly vulnerable to this energy deficit and toxicity.

PDC deficiency is a rare disorder, with an estimated incidence of less than 1 in 100,000 live births, although it is likely underdiagnosed. It affects all ethnic populations worldwide. Because the PDHA1 gene is located on the X chromosome, the inheritance pattern is X-linked. Consequently, males who inherit a mutated copy are usually severely affected. Females, who have two X chromosomes, can be carriers and exhibit a wide spectrum of clinical signs, from being completely asymptomatic to having severe symptoms, depending largely on the random X chromosome inactivation pattern.
502.symptoms: The clinical presentation is a spectrum, but it is most frequently characterized by severe neurological problems. Onset is typically in the neonatal period or early infancy with symptoms such as developmental delay, intellectual disability, low muscle tone (hypotonia), seizures, and difficulty with coordination (ataxia). Structural abnormalities of the brain, such as agenesis of the corpus callosum, are common findings on MRI scans.
502.prevention: There is no cure for CPD deficiency, so management focuses on supportive care and avoiding metabolic blockade. The primary treatment is a ketogenic diet—high in fat, adequate in protein, and low in carbohydrates—which forces the body to use fat-derived ketones as an energy source instead of glucose. This strategy reduces the metabolic burden on the defective complex and can help control seizures and reduce lactic acid levels. Supplementation with thiamine (vitamin B1), a cofactor of CPD, may be beneficial for some individuals.
502.technical_description:
Primary pyruvate dehydrogenase complex (PDC) deficiency is a rare inherited metabolic disorder in which the body cannot efficiently process sugars and carbohydrates to produce energy. This failure occurs in the mitochondria, the powerhouses of our cells, and leads to a toxic buildup of a substance called lactic acid, particularly affecting the nervous system. The most common cause of this disease is mutations in the PDHA1 gene, which contains the instructions for a key piece of the PDC. 

The severity of the disease often correlates with the type of genetic variant, for example, deleterious mutations that completely abolish enzyme function, such as c.1132C>T (p.Arg378Cys) and c.905G>A (p.Arg302His), tend to cause severe neurological symptoms from birth. In contrast, variants that allow for residual enzyme function can lead to milder phenotypes, such as episodes of ataxia or developmental delay.
502.technical_citations:
Savvidou A, Sofou K, Eklund EA, et al. Manifestations of X-linked pyruvate dehydrogenase complex deficiency in female PDHA1 carriers. Eur J Neurol. 2024 Jul.31(7):e16283.

 Ganetzky R, McCormick EM, Falk MJ. Primary Pyruvate Dehydrogenase Complex Deficiency Overview. 2021 Jun 17. In: Adam MP, Feldman J, Mirzaa GM, Pagon RA, Wallace SE, Amemiya A, editors. GeneReviews® [Internet]. Seattle (WA): University of Washington, Seattle. 1993–2025.

507.name: Mitochondrial Trifunctional Protein Deficiency
507.description: Mitochondrial trifunctional protein deficiency is a metabolic disorder, of autosomal recessive inheritance, that prevents the body from using long-chain fats as an energy source.
507.overview:
Trifunctional protein (TFP) deficiency is a congenital disorder of mitochondrial fatty acid beta-oxidation. The TFP protein is an enzyme composed of subunits (alpha and beta) that are essential for breaking down long-chain fats and converting them into energy, a vital process during fasting or during times of high energy demand such as illness. It is inherited in an autosomal recessive manner, meaning an individual must inherit an altered copy of the gene from each parent.

Trifunctional protein (TFP) deficiency is caused by the presence of pathogenic variants in the HADHA and HADHB genes. This report focuses on variants of the HADHB gene, which encodes the beta subunit of the TFP protein. This subunit performs the last step of the process. A defect in the HADHB gene blocks the metabolic pathway, resulting in profound energy deficiency and the accumulation of fatty acid intermediates that are toxic to the heart, liver, and muscles.
507.symptoms: The clinical spectrum is wide. TFP deficiency can have a neonatal onset which is the most severe and produces cardiomyopathy and metabolic decompensation. The intermediate form manifests in children or adults with hypoglycemia and neuropathy. Neuromuscular symptoms such as muscle weakness, exercise intolerance, and hypotonia occur. Episodic rhabdomyolysis after prolonged exercise, cold exposure, fasting, or infection.
507.prevention: There is no cure, so treatment focuses on preventing metabolic decompensation and is lifelong. Its management centers on a strict diet, low in long-chain fats and high in carbohydrates, along with absolute prevention of fasting. It is frequently supplemented with medium-chain triglyceride oil, as its metabolism does not require the TFP protein.
507.technical_description:
TFP is an enzyme composed of 4 alpha subunits and 4 beta subunits encoded by the genes HADHA and HADHB, respectively, which binds to the inner mitochondrial membrane. TPP is “trifunctional” due to its three characteristic enzymatic activities: long-chain enoyl-CoA hydratase, long-chain 3-hydroxyacyl-CoA dehydrogenase, and long-chain 3-ketoacyl-CoA thiolase. Long-chain 3-hydroxyacyl-CoA dehydrogenase or LCHAD deficiency affects one of the three activities and is due to the presence of pathogenic variants in the HADHA gene (results are included in the report “Long-chain 3-hydroxyacyl CoA dehydrogenase deficiency” or LCHAD). Deficiency of all three enzymes occurs in individuals with TFP deficiency and is caused by the presence of pathogenic variants in both the HADHA and HADHB genes.

The c.1528G>C (p.Glu510Gln, E510Q, rs137852769) variant is the most common in patients with LCHAD. In some cases, it has been observed in compound heterozygosity, and since certain variants in HADHA can also cause trifunctional deficiency, an enzymatic study should be performed to differentiate both conditions. Approximately 50% of variants in patients with TFP deficiency are found in HADHA.
507.technical_citations:
Dagher R, Massie R, Gentil BJ. MTP deficiency caused by HADHB mutations: Pathophysiology and clinical manifestations. Mol Genet Metab. 2021 May.133(1):1-7.

 Diebold I, Schön U, Horvath R, et al. HADHA and HADHB gene associated phenotypes - Identification of rare variants in a patient cohort by Next Generation Sequencing. Mol Cell Probes. 2019 Apr.44:14-20.

 Kang E, Kim YM, Kang M, et al. Clinical and genetic characteristics of patients with fatty acid oxidation disorders identified by newborn screening. BMC Pediatr. 2018 Mar 8.18(1):103.

 Prasun P, LoPiccolo MK, Ginevic I. Long-Chain Hydroxyacyl-CoA Dehydrogenase Deficiency / Trifunctional Protein Deficiency. 2022 Sep 1. In: Adam MP, Feldman J, Mirzaa GM, et al., editors. GeneReviews® [Internet]. Seattle (WA): University of Washington, Seattle. 1993-2025.

510.name: Ornithine Transcarbamylase Deficiency
510.description: Ornithine transcarbamylase deficiency is the most common X-linked urea cycle disorder, caused by variants in the OTC gene. This condition affects the elimination of ammonia, a neurotoxic substance, leading to episodes of hyperammonemia.
510.overview:
Ornithine transcarbamylase deficiency (OTCD) is a congenital metabolic disorder that affects the urea cycle, the body's main biochemical pathway for converting toxic ammonia, a byproduct of protein metabolism, into urea for excretion. The disease is caused by variants in the OTC gene, which encodes the key enzyme ornithine transcarbamylase. As an X-linked disorder, it affects males and females differently. Its prevalence in Europe is 1-9 /100,000 inhabitants.

Males who inherit the defective OTC gene typically present with the most severe form of the disease, as they only have one copy of the X chromosome. Females, having two X chromosomes, are typically carriers; however, due to random X chromosome inactivation (lyonization), they can have a highly variable expression of the disease, ranging from being completely asymptomatic to presenting with severe late-onset symptoms. The accumulation of ammonia in the blood (hyperammonemia) is particularly toxic to the brain and is the cause of all complications.
510.symptoms: The disease has two main forms of presentation. The early-onset neonatal form, which affects males, manifests in the first few days of life with feeding refusal, vomiting, progressive lethargy, hypotonia, and seizures, rapidly progressing to hyperammonemic coma that requires urgent treatment. The late-onset form can appear at any time from infancy to adulthood, in both males with residual enzymatic activity and in symptomatic carrier females. Symptoms are episodic, often triggered by illness or high protein intake, and include vomiting, migraines, confusion, ataxia, lethargy, and behavioral changes.
510.prevention:
Management focuses on preventing and treating hyperammonemia crises. An acute episode is a medical emergency requiring immediate hospitalization to reduce ammonia levels. This is achieved by stopping protein intake, administering high-calorie intravenous fluids (glucose and lipids), and using ammonia-scavenging drugs (such as sodium benzoate and sodium phenylbutyrate). In severe cases, dialysis may be necessary.

Long-term management is lifelong and is based on a very strict, low-protein diet, supplementation with essential amino acids, and chronic medication with ammonia scavengers. Liver transplantation is the only curative treatment, as it restores urea cycle function.

In patients with urea cycle disorders such as ornithine transcarbamylase deficiency, the use of valproic acid is contraindicated.
510.technical_description:
The OTC gene is located on the short arm of the X chromosome and produces the enzyme ornithine transcarbamylase. In the liver, the metabolic process occurs in 5 reactions controlled by several enzymes, known as the urea cycle, in which ammonia (produced by protein metabolism) is converted into a water-soluble substance that is eliminated in the urine, urea. The OTC enzyme participates in the second reaction, which takes place in the mitochondria of hepatocytes, in which citrulline is formed from carbamoyl phosphate and ornithine.

The correlation between a specific pathogenic variant of the OTC gene (genotype) and the severity of the disease (phenotype) is not absolute. Severe environmental stressors, such as illness, can trigger life-threatening hyperammonemia crises in individuals with variants that are typically considered "mild".

Generally, variants that completely eliminate enzyme function (e.g., nonsense or frameshift variants) cause severe, neonatal-onset disease in males. Conversely, variants that only partially reduce enzyme activity typically result in a milder, late-onset form. Heterozygous females can become symptomatic, especially if X chromosome inactivation in their liver cells is skewed. Furthermore, it has been observed that when a male in the family has post-neonatal onset deficiency, the risk of heterozygous females in his family presenting symptoms is much lower than in families where a male has the severe neonatal onset form.
510.technical_citations:
Donovan K, Vaqar S, Guzman N. Ornithine Transcarbamylase Deficiency. 2024 Feb 26. In: StatPearls [Internet]. Treasure Island (FL): StatPearls Publishing. 2025 Jan–.

 Lichter-Konecki U, Caldovic L, Morizono H, et al. Ornithine Transcarbamylase Deficiency. 2013 Aug 29 [Updated 2022 May 26]. In: Adam MP, Feldman J, Mirzaa GM, et al., editors. GeneReviews® [Internet]. Seattle (WA): University of Washington, Seattle. 1993-2025.

 Wang H, Ran J, Jiang T. Urea. Subcell Biochem. 2014.73:7-29.

524.name: Autosomal Dominant Multiple Epiphyseal Dysplasia
524.description: Autosomal dominant multiple epiphyseal dysplasia is caused by mutations in genes such as COL9A1, COL9A2, COL9A3, MATN3 and COMP, which affect the growth of the long bones of the arms and legs.
524.overview:
The COL9A1, COL9A2, COL9A3, MATN3, and COMP genes are fundamental for the proper formation and maintenance of cartilage, the smooth, flexible tissue that cushions the ends of bones in our joints. The COL9 genes encode type IX collagen, while MATN3 encodes the matrilin-3 protein. The COMP gene encodes a glycoprotein. Together, these proteins are part of the extracellular matrix, a complex network that gives cartilage its strength, structure, and ability to withstand compression. Pathogenic variants in any of these genes disrupt this intricate structure, resulting in weaker, disorganized cartilage that degenerates prematurely under normal daily stress.

This abnormal cartilage development primarily affects the epiphyses, the growth centers at the ends of long bones, causing them to appear flattened or irregular on X-rays. Dyggve Melchior-Eide Syndrome (DMES) is a rare condition, estimated to affect approximately 1 in 10,000 individuals, and occurs equally across all ethnic populations. As an autosomal dominant disorder, an individual with a pathogenic variant has a 50% chance of passing the condition on to each of their children.
524.symptoms: The most common symptom is joint pain, which generally begins in childhood (often between 2 and 10 years of age), particularly in weight-bearing joints such as the hips and knees. This can lead to a characteristic waddling gait and reduced exercise tolerance. Although adult height is usually in the low-normal range, the most significant long-term consequence is the development of premature osteoarthritis.
524.prevention: There is no cure for OA, so management focuses on relieving symptoms and preserving joint function. The multidisciplinary approach includes physiotherapy to maintain muscle strength around the joints and low-impact exercises such as swimming to maintain cardiovascular health without damaging cartilage. Pain can be managed with nonsteroidal anti-inflammatory drugs (NSAIDs). Weight control is crucial to minimize joint load. Finally, many individuals will require surgical intervention, such as a total hip or knee replacement, at a much younger age than the general population.
524.technical_description:
Autosomal dominant multiple epiphyseal dysplasia (EDM) is a heterogeneous disorder both phenotypically and genotypically. It is caused by mutations in the genes of type IX collagen (COL9A1, COL9A2, and COL9A3), matrilin-3 (MATN3), and cartilage oligomeric matrix protein (COMP).

The presence of certain variants in COMP can also cause another pathology known as pseudoachondroplasia, which presents other characteristic clinical manifestations. Mutations in the solute carrier family 26 member 2 gene (SLC26A2) cause autosomal recessive EDM, also analyzed in the section on hereditary conditions. Here we include the analysis of variants related to autosomal dominant EDM.

527.name: Autosomal Dominant Limb-Girdle Muscular Dystrophy
527.description: Autosomal dominant limb-girdle muscular dystrophy type D1 is a rare disease caused by mutations in the DNAJB6 gene that causes progressive muscle weakness in the hip and shoulder muscles in early adulthood, and is notably more common in Finland due to a founder mutation.
527.overview:
The DNAJB6 gene provides instructions for making a co-chaperone protein that is a key part of the cell's "quality control" system. This system is responsible for ensuring that proteins fold into their correct three-dimensional shape and for removing damaged or misfolded proteins through a recycling process known as autophagy. This cleanup function is essential for preventing the toxic accumulation of faulty proteins, especially in long-lived cells such as muscle fibers. Pathogenic variants in DNAJB6 impair this crucial maintenance function. The defective DNAJB6 protein not only fails in its cleanup task but can itself form abnormal clumps or aggregates.

This dysfunction leads to the accumulation of toxic protein aggregates within muscle fibers, causing significant cellular stress and damage over time. This process triggers the gradual death of muscle cells, which are progressively replaced by fat and scar tissue, resulting in characteristic muscle weakness and wasting. LGMDD1 is very rare worldwide but has a higher prevalence in Finland, where the first cases were described. As an autosomal dominant condition, an affected individual has a 50% chance of passing the mutation to each of their children.
527.symptoms: The hallmark of this condition is a slow progressive muscle weakness and atrophy that primarily affects the muscles of the lower extremities, particularly the soleus, adductor magnus, semimembranosus, and biceps femoris. Symptoms typically begin in early adulthood (between the ages of 25 and 50) and often start in the pelvic girdle, causing difficulty climbing stairs, rising from a chair, etc. Weakness in the shoulder girdle (shoulders) usually develops later. A key feature is that, in most cases, the heart and respiratory muscles are not significantly affected. Patients with LGMD1D exhibit slightly elevated creatine kinase levels.
527.prevention: Currently there is no cure for LGMDD1, so management is supportive and focuses on maintaining functionality, mobility, and quality of life. Physical therapy and gentle low-impact exercise (such as swimming and stretching) are crucial for preserving muscle strength and preventing joint stiffness and contractures. Regular follow-up with a neurologist is recommended to monitor progression.
527.technical_description: The pathogenic changes in DNAJB6 that have been described are mainly point mutations, such as c.279C>G (p.Phe93Leu) which has been identified in Finnish families with LGMDD1. Pathogenic variants that have been associated with the pathology affect the Gly/Phe-rich domain of the DNAJB6 protein (p.Phe89Ile, p.Phe93Leu, and p.Pro96Arg). Pathogenic variants have been described in other genes such as DES, TNPO3 and HNRPDL that can cause limb-girdle muscular dystrophy (not analyzed here).
527.technical_citations:
Kuiper EFE, Gallardo P, Bergsma T, et al. The chaperone DNAJB6 surveils FG-nucleoporins and is required for interphase nuclear pore complex biogenesis. Nat Cell Biol. 2022 Nov.24(11):1584-1594.

531.name: Fabry Disease
531.description: Fabry disease is a rare X-linked lysosomal storage disorder caused by mutations in the GLA gene. These mutations lead to a deficiency of the enzyme alpha-galactosidase A, resulting in the accumulation of a fatty substance called globotriaosylceramide.
531.overview:
The GLA gene, located on the X chromosome, provides instructions for making an enzyme called alpha-galactosidase A (α-Gal A). This enzyme works inside lysosomes, which are the recycling centers of our cells. α-Gal A is involved in breaking down and recycling a fatty substance known as globotriaosylceramide, or Gb3. 

Pathogenic variants in the GLA gene result in the production of a deficient or completely non-functional α-Gal A enzyme. Without a functional enzyme, Gb3 cannot be broken down and accumulates to toxic levels within lysosomes. This progressive buildup disrupts normal cellular function and leads to widespread organ damage, particularly affecting blood vessels, kidneys, heart, skin, and the nervous system.

Fabry disease is an X-linked disorder, meaning that men who inherit the mutated gene are typically more severely affected than women. Carrier females can exhibit a broad spectrum of symptoms, ranging from being completely asymptomatic to having disease severity comparable to that of men. The estimated incidence of the classic form of the disease is approximately 1 in 50,000 to 117,000 males, although there are populations where it may be higher.
531.symptoms: The classic form of the condition occurs in males with less than 1% of α-Gal A enzyme activity, and the first symptoms typically begin in infancy or adolescence. Early signs often include episodes of pain in the hands and feet (acroparesthesia), the appearance of small, dark red skin spots called angiokeratomas, a decreased ability to sweat (hypohidrosis), and a characteristic swirling pattern in the eye's cornea (cornea verticillata). As the disease progresses into adulthood, Gb3 accumulation leads to complications, including progressive kidney failure, heart rhythm abnormalities, and an increased risk of stroke.
531.prevention: The management of Fabry disease has been revolutionized by specific therapies targeting the underlying cause. The primary treatment is enzyme replacement therapy (ERT), which involves regular intravenous infusions of a lab-made α-Gal A enzyme. This helps to clear accumulated Gb3 in cells, slowing or preventing organ damage. For patients with certain types of GLA mutations, an oral medication called Chaperone Therapy (migalastat) is available. This drug helps the patient's own misfolded enzyme to fold correctly, restoring some of its function. In addition to this, managing specific symptoms and organ complications with pain medications, renal protection, and cardiac issues is essential.
531.technical_description:
Fabry disease is an X-linked lysosomal storage disorder that affects the body's ability to break down a specific type of fat, globotriaosylceramide (Gb3). This condition is caused by a deficiency of the enzyme alpha-galactosidase A (α-Gal A). Without this enzyme, a fatty substance called globotriaosylceramide (Gb3) progressively accumulates to toxic levels in cells throughout the body.

The gene responsible for this dysfunction is the GLA gene, which contains the instructions for producing the α-Gal A enzyme. Hundreds of variants or mutations in this gene have been identified that can trigger Fabry disease. Individuals with an atypical late-onset phenotype (such as the renal, cardiac, or cerebrovascular variant) typically present with missense or splicing variants, which allow for residual expression of α-Gal A enzyme activity. Variants such as c.335G>A (p.Arg112His) and c.902G>A (p.Arg301Gln) have been identified in both individuals with the classic phenotype and those with the cardiac variant (late-onset phenotype), supporting the theory of the existence of modifying factors. On the other hand, the variant c.644A>G (p.Asn215Ser) has been associated with a milder form of the disease.

Newborn screening in Taiwan has revealed a high prevalence of the splicing variant c.640-801G>A, a founder effect in that population, which is associated with a late-onset cardiac phenotype.
531.technical_citations:
Ortiz A, Germain DP, Desnick RJ, et al. Fabry disease revisited: Management and treatment recommendations for adult patients. Mol Genet Metab. 2018 Apr.123(4):416-427.

 Mehta A, Hughes DA. Fabry Disease. 2002 Aug 5 [Updated 2024 Apr 11]. In: Adam MP, Feldman J, Mirzaa GM, et al., editors. GeneReviews® [Internet]. Seattle (WA): University of Washington, Seattle. 1993-2025.

538.name: Niemann-Pick Disease Type C
538.description: Niemann-Pick disease type C (NPC) is a rare genetic disorder caused by mutations in the NPC1 or NPC2 genes, which lead to a toxic accumulation of lipids (cholesterol and fats) within cells. This accumulation progressively damages tissues, primarily affecting the spleen, liver, and, critically, the brain, causing severe neurological deterioration.
538.overview:
Niemann-Pick disease type C is an autosomal recessive lysosomal storage disorder. The NPC1 (in approximately 95% of cases) and NPC2 genes are crucial for transporting cholesterol and other lipids out of lysosomes, the cell's recycling centers. Mutations in either of these genes prevent this process, leading to the buildup of these fatty substances. This accumulation is toxic to cells and causes widespread damage to multiple organs.

The estimated incidence is 1 in 100,000 births, but it is thought to be higher, especially for the late-onset form of the disease. Diagnosis is based on clinical suspicion, biochemical analysis, and confirmation by genetic testing that identifies pathogenic variants in NPC1 or NPC2.
538.symptoms: The symptoms and age of onset of NPC are highly variable, from the neonatal period to adulthood. Manifestations can include enlargement of the liver and spleen (hepatosplenomegaly), prolonged jaundice after birth, and feeding difficulties. The hallmark is progressive neurological deterioration, including difficulty with coordination of movement (ataxia), problems with speech (dysarthria), difficulty swallowing (dysphagia), and a characteristic sign: supranuclear vertical gaze palsy. Cognitive impairment, seizures, and cataplexy (sudden loss of muscle tone) are also common.
538.prevention: Currently, there is no cure for NPC, and management focuses on treating symptoms and improving quality of life. A multidisciplinary approach including physical therapy, occupational therapy, and speech therapy is essential for managing motor and swallowing difficulties. In several regions, including Europe, a medication called miglustat has been approved, which can slow the progression of key neurological symptoms in some patients.
538.technical_description:
95% of patients with NPC present mutations in the NPC1 gene. Over 300 pathogenic variants have been identified in the NPC1 gene. While 5% of patients carry variants in NPC2. One of the most prevalent variants in Europeans is c.3182T>C (p.Ile1061Thr, I1061T or rs80358259) in NPC1. This alteration causes the protein to misfold and be degraded. Founder variants have been identified in certain populations, notably those detected in the Acadians of Nova Scotia (c.2974G>T) and in a Bedouin group from Israel (c.1211G>A).

Approximately 50 pathogenic or potentially pathogenic mutations have been identified in the NPC2 gene. Among these, c.58G>T (p.Glu20Ter, E20X or rs80358260) stands out, producing a truncated NPC2 protein and has been observed in homozygosis and compound heterozygosis.
