543.name: Autosomal Dominant Polycystic Kidney Disease
543.description: Autosomal dominant polycystic kidney disease is the most common hereditary kidney disorder, mainly caused by mutations in the PKD1 or PKD2 genes. It is characterized by the progressive development of numerous cysts in both kidneys, which destroy healthy tissue, increase kidney size, and can lead to kidney failure.
543.overview:
Autosomal dominant polycystic kidney disease (ADPKD) is a genetic disorder that affects approximately 1 in 400 to 1,000 people worldwide, with no predilection for ethnicity or gender. It is inherited in an autosomal dominant manner, meaning that a single mutated copy of the gene is sufficient to cause the disease and that an affected parent has a 50% chance of passing it on to each of their children. Mutations in the PKD1 gene cause about 78% of cases and are associated with more severe disease, while mutations in PKD2 account for 15% and typically have a milder course.

The PKD1 and PKD2 genes encode the polycystin-1 and polycystin-2 proteins, which are key to the normal function of renal tubule cells. When these proteins are defective, cell signaling is disrupted, leading to uncontrolled cell growth and the formation of fluid-filled cysts. More recently, mutations in other genes (GANAB, DNAJB11, ALG9, etc.) have been identified that can cause atypical forms of the disease, broadening the genetic spectrum of ADPKD.
543.symptoms: Symptoms usually appear between 30 and 40 years of age, although cyst development begins much earlier. The earliest and most common sign is high blood pressure before age 35. Other symptoms include pain in the back or sides, blood in the urine (hematuria), recurrent urinary tract infections, and kidney stones. ARPKD can also affect other organs, primarily the liver. A serious, though uncommon, complication is the development of intracranial aneurysms.
543.prevention: There is no cure for ADPKD, but treatment can slow its progression and manage complications. Strict blood pressure control with medications (such as ACE inhibitors or ARBs) is crucial. A healthy lifestyle is recommended, including a low-salt diet, avoiding high caffeine intake, not smoking, maintaining a healthy weight, and good hydration. For adults at risk of rapid progression, the medication Tolvaptan has been shown to reduce the rate of cyst growth and the decline in kidney function. When the disease progresses to a terminal stage, treatment options are dialysis or kidney transplant.
543.technical_description:
Autosomal dominant polycystic kidney disease (ADPKD) is a genetic disorder characterized by the progressive development of multiple cysts in the kidneys, which can lead to kidney failure. Although ADPKD is a single disease, genetic variants in different genes can influence its severity and progression. The most relevant associated genes are detailed below.

The PKD1 and PKD2 genes are responsible for the majority of ADPKD cases. The proteins they encode, polycystin-1 (PKD1) and polycystin-2 (PKD2), work together in the cilia of kidney cells, structures responsible for detecting fluid flow. When these proteins do not function correctly, cell signaling is altered, favoring cyst formation. Mutations in these genes produce similar phenotypes, although mutations in PKD2 are associated with a milder form of the disease, while variants in PKD1 are linked to more severe and rapidly progressing disease. Fully penetrant biallelic pathogenic variants (i.e., non-hypomorphic) in PKD1 or PKD2 are expected to be incompatible with life.

Recently, other genes have been identified that can also cause ADPKD, although they represent a very small percentage of cases (GANAB gene encodes a subunit of glucosidase II, an enzyme that facilitates the correct folding of proteins, including polycystin-1. Variants in GANAB are generally associated with mild kidney disease but with liver cysts. On the other hand, the DNAJB11 gene produces a chaperone protein that also aids in the proper folding and transport of other proteins, such as polycystin-1. As with GANAB, variants in DNAJB11 are linked to an atypical and milder form of ADPKD. Regarding the ALG5, ALG9, and IFT140 genes, a kidney phenotype of mild to moderate severity has been observed.

547.name: Tuberous Sclerosis
547.description: Tuberous Sclerosis Complex (TSC) is a genetic disorder caused by mutations in the TSC1 or TSC2 genes, leading to the growth of noncancerous tumors in multiple organs by altering the normal regulation of cell growth. This condition affects approximately 1 in 6,000 people worldwide.
547.overview:
Tuberous sclerosis complex (TSC) is a multisystem genetic disease caused by pathogenic variants in the TSC1 or TSC2 genes. These genes encode the hamartin and tuberin proteins, which bind together to form a complex that slows cell proliferation by controlling the mTOR signaling pathway. A mutation in either of these genes inactivates this complex, leading to the overactivation of the mTOR pathway. This triggers uncontrolled cell growth, resulting in benign tumors (hamartomas) in multiple organs, primarily affecting the brain, kidneys, heart, lungs, and skin.

TSC follows an autosomal dominant inheritance pattern, meaning that a single mutated copy of the gene is sufficient to develop the disease. However, in about two-thirds of cases, the mutation is de novo (new); that is, it appears spontaneously in the individual without being inherited, which is why there is usually no family history.
547.symptoms:
The signs and symptoms of TSC vary widely from person to person, depending on the organs affected and the severity of the involvement. Symptoms may be present at birth or develop over time.

Common features include skin abnormalities (such as "ash-leaf" spots), seizures (including infantile spasms in the first year of life), developmental delay, and intellectual disability. Brain tumors (cortical tubers and subependymal giant cell astrocytomas, or SEGAs), kidney cysts and tumors (angiomyolipomas), and cardiac tumors (rhabdomyomas, typically in the fetal and neonatal stage) are also hallmarks of the disease. Lymphangioleiomyomatosis, a progressive lung involvement, can occur in adult women with TSC. Retinal lesions may occur in 30-50% of cases.
547.prevention: The management of TSC requires a lifelong multidisciplinary approach, with regular surveillance to monitor and treat complications early. This involves performing periodic imaging tests such as brain MRIs, renal ultrasounds, and echocardiograms. Treatment focuses on managing specific symptoms, such as the use of anticonvulsant medications for epilepsy. An important advance in treatment is the use of mTOR inhibitor drugs, such as everolimus and sirolimus. These drugs act directly on the overactivated mTOR pathway and are approved to treat specific TSC-related tumors, such as SEGAs and renal angiomyolipomas.
547.technical_description:
Tuberous sclerosis complex (TSC) belongs to a group of neurodevelopmental disorders known as mTORopathies. These are caused by mutations in more than 16 genes, including TSC1 and TSC2, which alter the mTOR signaling pathway, a key regulator of cell growth and metabolism.

The TSC2 gene encodes tuberin, a tumor suppressor protein that associates with hamartin (encoded by TSC1) to form the functional TSC1-TSC2 complex. Since tuberin is the catalytic subunit of the complex, pathogenic variants in TSC2 typically cause a more severe phenotype than those in TSC1.

Approximately 70% of TSC cases are due to alterations in TSC2 and 25% to TSC1. Nearly 3,000 pathogenic variants have been described in TSC2 and over 900 in TSC1. In TSC2, missense variants are more frequent, accounting for 26% of cases. Small insertions and deletions represent 38% of pathogenic variants, and large deletions and rearrangements make up the remaining 36%.

553.name: Glycogen Storage Disease Type IV
553.description: Glycogenosis type 4, or Andersen disease, is a rare hereditary metabolic disorder caused by mutations in the GBE1 gene. Deficiency of the glycogen branching enzyme leads to the accumulation of an abnormal and insoluble glycogen (polyglucosan bodies) that damages cells, mainly in the liver, muscles, and nervous system.
553.overview:
Glycogenosis type 4 is an autosomal recessive disease, meaning that an individual must inherit two mutated copies of the GBE1 gene to develop the condition. This gene provides instructions for making the glycogen branching enzyme, which is essential for building the correct, branched structure of this energy storage molecule. Without this enzyme, glycogen forms with long chains and few branches, becoming insoluble and accumulating as toxic deposits within cells.

This cellular accumulation is the underlying cause of progressive tissue damage. The disease is extremely rare, with an estimated incidence of 1 in 600,000 to 800,000 births, and affects all populations. Diagnosis is suspected by clinical and biochemical findings, and confirmed by measuring enzyme activity and/or genetic testing of the GBE1 gene.
553.symptoms: The clinical presentation of the disease is highly variable. The classic and most common form is progressive hepatic disease, which manifests in the first 18 months of life with growth retardation, hepatomegaly (enlarged liver), and progressive cirrhosis leading to liver failure. Other forms include a congenital neuromuscular variant, with severe hypotonia and respiratory weakness. There are also juvenile and adult-onset forms, such as adult polyglucosan body disease, which is characterized by progressive neurological deterioration, muscle weakness, and bladder dysfunction.
553.prevention: Currently, there is no cure for glycogen storage disease type 4, and treatment is largely supportive and depends on the clinical form. For the classic progressive hepatic form, the only effective treatment is liver transplantation, which corrects the metabolic defect in the liver. However, the accumulation of abnormal glycogen may continue in other tissues. For neuromuscular forms, management focuses on physical therapy, respiratory support, and symptomatic treatment of neurological and cardiac complications, with the goal of maintaining function and quality of life.
553.technical_description:
Glycogenosis type 4 is a hereditary genetic disorder with autosomal recessive inheritance. Individuals affected by this condition have difficulty producing a normal form of glycogen due to mutations (or genetic variants) in the GBE1 gene.

The GBE1 gene provides instructions for the production of a crucial enzyme, known as glycogen branching enzyme 1,4-alpha-glucan. When this gene has mutations, the enzyme does not function correctly, leading to the production of glycogen with few branches and extremely long chains. This abnormal structure is called polyglucosan bodies. These anomalous molecules accumulate in liver, muscle, and nerve cells, causing progressive damage and the characteristic symptoms of the disease.

The GBE1 gene exhibits a wide variety of variants that can cause the disease. The severity of the disorder usually depends on the type of variant and the amount of residual function the enzyme retains. Some of the most studied variants include:

c.986A>C (p.Tyr329Ser): One of the most common missense variants, associated with both the non-progressive hepatic form and adult polyglucosan body disease.

c.1571G>A (p.Arg524Gln): Another missense variant linked to the non-progressive hepatic form, adult polyglucosan body disease, and also with the classic hepatic form.

Adult polyglucosan body disease is another phenotype related to mutations in the GBE1 gene. In this type, GBE activity is reduced or normal.
553.technical_citations:
Akman HO, Lossos A, Kakhlon O. GBE1 Adult Polyglucosan Body Disease. 2009 Apr 2 [Updated 2020 Sep 17]. In: Adam MP, Feldman J, Mirzaa GM, editors. GeneReviews® [Internet]. Seattle (WA): University of Washington, Seattle. 1993-2025.

 Magoulas PL, El-Hattab AW. Glycogen Storage Disease Type IV. 2013 Jan 3 [updated 2019 Aug 1]. In: Adam MP, Feldman J, Mirzaa GM, Pagon RA, Wallace SE, Amemiya A, editors. GeneReviews® [Internet]. Seattle (WA): University of Washington, Seattle. 1993–2025.

554.name: Glycogen Storage Disease Type VI
554.description: Glycogenosis type VI (GSD VI), also known as Hers' disease, is a hereditary and generally mild metabolic disorder caused by mutations in the PYGL gene. The deficiency of the enzyme liver glycogen phosphorylase prevents the correct breakdown of glycogen in the liver, leading to its accumulation (hepatomegaly) and mild hypoglycemia during fasting. Treatment is dietary and the prognosis is excellent, with a notable improvement in symptoms with age.
554.overview:
Hers disease is an autosomal recessive disorder, requiring an individual to inherit two mutated copies of the PYGL gene, one from each parent, to manifest the disease. This gene encodes the enzyme hepatic glycogen phosphorylase, which plays a crucial role in glycogenolysis, the process of breaking down stored glycogen in the liver to release glucose into the bloodstream and maintain stable blood sugar levels, especially between meals.

Mutations in PYGL reduce or eliminate the activity of this enzyme. As a result, glycogen accumulates in the liver, causing it to enlarge, and the body cannot access this energy reserve efficiently, leading to hypoglycemia. The disease is considered one of the milder forms of hepatic glycogenosis.

The incidence is estimated at approximately 1 in 100,000 births; however, GSD-VI affects 1 in 1,000 people in the Mennonite population due to the c.1620+1G>A variant.
554.symptoms: Symptoms usually appear in early childhood and are predominantly mild. The most common finding and often the first to be detected is a noticeable enlargement of the abdomen due to hepatomegaly (enlarged liver). Children may experience ketotic hypoglycemia after prolonged fasting (e.g., overnight), manifesting with tremors or irritability, elevated liver transaminases, hyperlipidemia, and low prealbumin levels. Growth delay is commonly observed, suboptimal metabolic control can lead to shorter stature, osteopenia, and osteoporosis. Unlike other glycogenoses, blood lactate and uric acid levels are typically normal. It is characteristic for symptoms to improve significantly after puberty.
554.prevention: Nutritional monitoring is essential, with the aim of preventing hypoglycemia and ensuring adequate growth. This is achieved through frequent meals rich in complex carbohydrates, and avoiding prolonged fasting periods. In some cases, raw cornstarch is used as a source of slow-release glucose. With adequate dietary management, the prognosis is usually favorable and most adults lead a completely normal and asymptomatic life.
554.technical_description:
Glycogenosis type VI, also known as Hers' disease, is an inherited metabolic disorder caused by pathogenic variants in the PYGL gene. This gene encodes the enzyme glycogen phosphorylase, which is responsible for breaking down glycogen in the liver to release glucose into the blood. When the enzyme does not function correctly, glycogen accumulates in the liver, leading to the characteristic symptoms of the disease.

Most of the pathogenic variants described in relation to glycogenosis type VI are of the missense type and affect critical points of the enzyme, preventing it from activating correctly or from binding to the molecules it needs to work, such as glycogen itself (substrate) or pyrophosphate (an essential cofactor).

In the case of the PYGL gene, a clear founder effect has been identified in the Mennonite population. In this group, a large proportion of glycogenosis type VI cases are caused by the same variant: c.1620+1G>A.
554.technical_citations: Labrador E, Weinstein DA. Glycogen Storage Disease Type VI. 2009 Apr 23 [Updated 2019 Nov 27]. In: Adam MP, Feldman J, Mirzaa GM, et al., editors. GeneReviews® [Internet]. Seattle (WA): University of Washington, Seattle. 1993-2025.

556.name: Hereditary Hemochromatosis Type 2A (HJV gene)
556.description: Hemochromatosis type 2A, or juvenile hemochromatosis, is a rare and severe form of early-onset iron overload, caused by autosomal recessive mutations in the HJV gene.
556.overview:
Unlike hereditary hemochromatosis type 1 (associated with the HFE gene), which is more common and slow-progressing, type 2A is a much more severe and early-manifesting disease. The HJV gene provides instructions for making the hemojuvelin protein. This protein is a key regulator of hepcidin production, the master hormone that controls iron absorption in the intestine and its release from storage cells.

Mutations in both copies of the HJV gene result in drastically reduced or absent hepcidin production. This leads to uncontrolled absorption of dietary iron, regardless of existing stores. This excess iron is deposited in tissues such as the heart, liver, and endocrine glands, where it generates toxic and progressive cellular damage.
556.symptoms: The symptoms of juvenile hemochromatosis typically appear in the first or second decade of life, almost always before the age of 30. The first manifestations are usually endocrine, such as hypogonadotropic hypogonadism, which causes lack of pubertal development, amenorrhea in women, and loss of libido in men. The most dangerous complication is iron-deposit cardiomyopathy, which can lead to heart failure and arrhythmias. Other symptoms include liver cirrhosis, diabetes, joint pain, and skin darkening.
556.prevention: Early diagnosis and treatment are crucial. Excess iron must be removed from the body through frequent and intensive phlebotomies (blood withdrawal or venipuncture). The goal is to reduce serum ferritin levels to a low-normal range. In patients with severe heart disease or anemia who do not tolerate phlebotomies, iron chelation therapy with drugs is used. In addition, established complications must be treated, such as hormone replacement therapy for hypogonadism, administration of medications or insulin to control diabetes, and management of heart failure.
556.technical_description:
Loss-of-function mutations in the HJV, TFR2, and HAMP genes cause more severe hemochromatosis compared to that caused by the HFE gene. The three are collectively termed "non-HFE hemochromatosis" and are characterized by low hepcidin levels in comparison to iron overload. In addition, more severe iron overload occurs accompanied by hormonal deficiency.

The most frequent variant identified in patients with type 2A hemochromatosis is c.959G>T (p.Gly320Val, G320V, or rs74315323) which has been observed in homozygosity and compound heterozygosity. Depending on the HJV variant, hemochromatosis can have an earlier or later onset. The c.1006G>T variant (p.Gly336Ter, G336X, or rs1553769428) was identified in individuals who were between 38 and 49 years of age while patients with G320V were around 26 years old.
556.technical_citations:
Brissot P, Pietrangelo A, Adams PC, et al. Hemochromatosis. Nat Rev Dis Primers. 2018 Apr 5.4:18016. doi: 10.1038/nrdp.2018.16.

 Camaschella C, Nai A, Silvestri L. Iron metabolism and iron disorders revisited in the hepcidin era. Haematologica. 2020 Jan 31.105(2):260-272.

 Piperno A, Bertola F, Bentivegna A. Juvenile Hemochromatosis. 2005 Feb 17 [Updated 2020 Jan 9]. In: Adam MP, Feldman J, Mirzaa GM, et al., editors. GeneReviews® [Internet]. Seattle (WA): University of Washington, Seattle. 1993-2025.

 Takami A, Tatsumi Y, Sakai K, et al. Juvenile Hemochromatosis: A Case Report and Review of the Literature. Pharmaceuticals (Basel). 2020 Aug 15.13(8):195.

557.name: Hereditary Hemochromatosis Type 2B (HAMP gene)
557.description: Hereditary Hemochromatosis type 2B is a subtype of juvenile hemochromatosis, caused by autosomal recessive mutations in the HAMP gene. These mutations prevent the production of the hormone hepcidin, which is involved in iron regulation, leading to massive and uncontrolled intestinal iron absorption from birth.
557.overview:
Hereditary hemochromatosis type 2B is, along with type 2A, the cause of juvenile hemochromatosis. Although the affected gene is different, the final result is similar: a marked deficiency of hepcidin. The HAMP gene encodes the peptide hormone hepcidin, which is produced in the liver.

Hepcidin acts at the intestinal level by inhibiting iron absorption, meaning it acts as a negative regulator of iron absorption by binding to ferroportin. Ferroportin is a protein in the enterocyte membrane responsible for iron transport; when it binds to hepcidin, it forms a complex that is degraded in the lysosome. The absence of hepcidin leaves the body without any defense against iron overload. Iron is absorbed continuously and accumulates at a very accelerated rate in parenchymal organs.

This form of the disease is autosomal recessive and is even rarer than type 2A, having been described in only a limited number of families worldwide.
557.symptoms: Clinically, hemochromatosis type 2B is indistinguishable from type 2A. The disease manifests early, typically in adolescence, and always before the age of 30. The main symptoms are hypogonadism (delayed or absent puberty), severe cardiomyopathy, early-onset liver cirrhosis, diabetes mellitus, and arthropathy (joint pain). Without early diagnosis and treatment, progression is rapid and the prognosis is severe.
557.prevention: The management of hemochromatosis type 2B is identical to that of type 2A and requires urgent and aggressive intervention. The basis of treatment is iron depletion through an intensive program of phlebotomies (therapeutic blood draws) to eliminate accumulated iron and prevent further organ damage. Iron chelation therapy is an alternative for patients who do not tolerate phlebotomies. The management of established complications, such as heart failure or hypogonadism, is fundamental and requires a multidisciplinary team.
557.technical_description:
Hereditary hemochromatosis type 2B is a rare genetic disease characterized by early and excessive iron accumulation in vital organs. This iron overload is toxic and can cause severe damage, usually before the age of 30.

The disease is due to variants in the HAMP gene, which is responsible for producing the hormone hepcidin, key to regulating the absorption of dietary iron and its release into the blood. Mutations that cause loss of hepcidin function alter this control, allowing the body to absorb iron in an uncontrolled manner and leading to the characteristic overload of the disease. Among these genetic variants, c.223C>T has been described in the Japanese population.
557.technical_citations:
Altes Hernández A, Aranalde Fortó J. Role of hepcidin in the pathogenesis of hemochromatosis [Papel de la hepcidina en la patogenia de la hemocromatosis]. Gastroenterol Hepatol. 2009 Nov.32(9):622-6.

 Brissot P, Pietrangelo A, Adams PC, et al. Haemochromatosis. Nat Rev Dis Primers. 2018 Apr 5.4:18016.

 Hattori A, Miyajima H, Tomosugi N, et al. Clinicopathological study of Japanese patients with genetic iron overload syndromes. Pathol Int. 2012 Sep.62(9):612-8.

558.name: Hereditary Hemochromatosis Type 3 (TFR2 gene)
558.description: Hereditary hemochromatosis type 3 is a rare form of iron overload, caused by autosomal recessive mutations in the TFR2 gene. The first symptoms appear in early adulthood and iron accumulation is more severe than in the common type 1 form.
558.overview:
Hemochromatosis type 3 is clinically and genetically situated between the common adult form (type 1) and severe juvenile forms (type 2). The TFR2 gene encodes the "transferrin receptor 2" protein, which is found mainly in liver cells. This protein is thought to act as a sensor of the amount of iron circulating in the blood. When iron levels are high, TFR2 participates in signaling to increase the production of hepcidin, the hormone that blocks iron absorption.

In individuals with mutations in both copies of the TFR2 gene, this iron detection mechanism is defective. The liver does not recognize iron overload and, consequently, does not produce enough hepcidin. This hepcidin deficiency allows for excessive and continuous absorption of iron from the diet, leading to its toxic deposition in various organs. The disease is inherited in an autosomal recessive manner and is much less common than hemochromatosis type 1.
558.symptoms: Symptoms usually appear before age 40, an earlier age than in type 1 hemochromatosis. Iron overload is more pronounced, leading to a higher risk of developing complications. Initial symptoms include chronic fatigue, weakness, and joint pain (arthropathy). As iron accumulates, significant liver damage occurs with elevated liver enzymes, fibrosis, and an increased risk of progression to cirrhosis. Diabetes, hypogonadism, and heart problems can also occur, although they are usually less severe than in juvenile forms.
558.prevention: The treatment of Hemochromatosis type 3 is similar to that of other forms and aims to eliminate excess iron from the body. As with other hemochromatoses, phlebotomies (bloodletting) should be performed regularly. The frequency of phlebotomies in the initial phase is usually more intensive than in type 1 to rapidly reduce iron stores. Once ferritin levels normalize, a maintenance program is established. Regular monitoring and management of established organ complications are crucial.
558.technical_description:
Hereditary hemochromatosis is a genetic disorder that results in excessive iron absorption from the diet. Type 3 hemochromatosis is associated with variants in the TFR2 gene.

The TFR2 gene encodes the "transferrin receptor 2", a key protein found primarily on the surface of liver cells. This receptor plays a fundamental role in regulating iron levels in the body by acting as a sensor of its concentration. When pathogenic variants in the TFR2 gene affect its function, the body absorbs iron uncontrollably, leading to progressive iron overload.

Although the age of symptom onset is usually before 40 years of age, exceptions have been documented. One example is the case of a patient diagnosed at 82 years of age, who was found to be homozygous for the c.88dupC (p.Arg30fs) variant, showing variability in the age of disease onset.

Furthermore, hereditary hemochromatosis can be caused by variants in other genes, such as HFE. However, studies have observed that homozygosity for the pathogenic variant c.2343G>A (p.Trp781Ter) in TFR2, when combined with one copy of the c.845G>A (p.Cys282Tyr) variant in HFE, does not necessarily lead to a more severe phenotype of hereditary hemochromatosis.
558.technical_citations:
Hernández G, Ferrer-Cortès X, Venturi V, et al. New Mutations in HFE2 and TFR2 Genes Causing Non HFE-Related Hereditary Hemochromatosis. Genes (Basel). 2021 Dec 13.12(12):1980.

 Joshi R, Shvartsman M, Morán E, et al. Functional consequences of transferrin receptor-2 mutations causing hereditary hemochromatosis type 3. Mol Genet Genomic Med. 2015 May.3(3):221-32.

 Kowdley DS, Kowdley KV. Appropriate Clinical Genetic Testing of Hemochromatosis Type 2-4, Including Ferroportin Disease. Appl Clin Genet. 2021 Aug 12.14:353-361.

559.name: Hereditary Hemochromatosis Type 4 (SLC40A1 gene)
559.description: Hereditary hemochromatosis type 4, or ferroportin disease, is a unique form of iron overload caused by mutations in the SLC40A1 gene and is inherited in an autosomal dominant manner.
559.overview:
Unlike other forms of hemochromatosis, type 4 is autosomal dominant, meaning that a single mutated copy of the SLC40A1 gene, inherited from one parent, is sufficient to cause the disease. This gene produces the ferroportin protein, which is the only known mammalian protein with an iron "exporter" function. It works by transporting iron from intestinal cells into the bloodstream. The hormone hepcidin regulates iron levels by closing this gateway to the blood.

Mutations in SLC40A1 cause two types of disease. The classic form (loss of function) results in a defective ferroportin that cannot export iron, trapping it within macrophages in the liver and spleen. This causes very high ferritin, but blood iron (transferrin saturation) is normal or low. The non-classic (gain of function) form, much rarer, produces a ferroportin resistant to hepcidin; the gateway remains permanently open, releasing iron uncontrollably into the bloodstream, similar to types 1, 2, and 3 hemochromatosis.
559.symptoms: Symptoms depend on the type of mutation. In the classic form, many individuals are asymptomatic and are incidentally discovered due to elevated ferritin. Progression to liver fibrosis is slow, and cirrhosis is rare. Patients may develop anemia if treated aggressively with phlebotomies. In the non-classic form, symptoms are indistinguishable from classic hemochromatosis (HFE), with fatigue, joint pain, and a significant risk of liver damage, diabetes, and other problems if left untreated, as iron is deposited directly in the organs.
559.prevention: Management must be carefully adapted to the type of mutation. For the classical form, many patients only require monitoring. If ferritin is very high (generally >1000 µg/L), phlebotomies can be performed cautiously and less frequently, carefully monitoring for the possible onset of anemia. For the non-classical form, the treatment is the same as for type 1 hemochromatosis: regular phlebotomies to normalize ferritin levels and transferrin saturation, and it is a well-tolerated treatment.
559.technical_description:
Ferroportin disease, with autosomal dominant inheritance, presents two types: 

Type 4A ferroportin disease, or classic form, which is related to SLC40A1 loss-of-function variants that produce a decrease in ferroportin in the cell membrane and a lower capacity to export iron to the blood. Patients present high serum ferritin levels, low or normal transferrin saturation, and low tolerance to phlebotomies.

Type 4B ferroportin disease, or non-classic form, associated with the presence of gain-of-function variants that make ferroportin resistant to the action of the hormone hepcidin. These produce an increase in iron export to the bloodstream and a phenotype similar to hemochromatosis caused by mutations in the HFE gene. Patients present an increase in ferritin and transferrin saturation levels.

The c.977G>T mutation (p.Cys326Phe, C326S or rs1227198230) causes one of the most severe forms of type 4B hemochromatosis. This SLC40A1 variant produces a phenotype with increased iron absorption, hyperferritinemia, high transferrin saturation, and iron accumulation in the liver and other tissues such as the lungs. C326S is located at a ferroportin residue involved in binding with hepcidin.
559.technical_citations:
Uguen K, Ka C, Collod-Béroud G, et al. The Spectra of Disease-Causing Mutations in the Ferroportin 1 (SLC40A1) Encoding Gene and Related Iron Overload Phenotypes (Hemochromatosis Type 4 and Ferroportin Disease). Hum Mutat. 2023 Jun 13.2023:5162256.

  Vlasveld LT, Janssen R, Bardou-Jacquet E, et al. Twenty Years of Ferroportin Disease: A Review or An Update of Published Clinical, Biochemical, Molecular, and Functional Features. Pharmaceuticals (Basel). 2019 Sep 9.12(3):132.

 Wu LY, Song ZY, Li QH, et al. Iron chelators reverse organ damage in type 4B hereditary hemochromatosis: Case reports. Medicine (Baltimore). 2021 Apr 2.100(13):e25258.

560.name: Hemophilia B
560.description: Hemophilia B is an X-linked genetic disorder caused by mutations in the F9 gene, which is responsible for producing the blood clotting protein Factor IX. This deficiency affects the body's ability to form clots, leading to episodes of prolonged bleeding and spontaneous hemorrhages, especially in joints and muscles.
560.overview:
The F9 gene, located on the X chromosome, provides instructions for making coagulation factor IX, a protein crucial to the cascade of reactions that leads to blood clotting. Genetic variants in the F9 gene can result in a deficiency in factor IX activity or significantly reduce the amount of factor IX in the blood. Without sufficient factor IX activity, the clotting cascade is interrupted, making it difficult for the body to effectively stop bleeding after an injury.

This condition primarily affects males. The incidence of hemophilia B is estimated to be approximately 1 in 25,000 to 30,000 male births worldwide.
560.symptoms: The main symptoms include prolonged bleeding after injuries, surgeries, or dental procedures, and easy bruising. Spontaneous bleeding into large joints (hemarthrosis) and muscles is characteristic of severe forms, causing pain, swelling, and potential long-term joint damage. The age of onset is usually in early childhood, often becoming apparent when the infant begins to become more active. The severity of hemophilia B depends on the level of activity of coagulation factor IX, and is classified as severe (5% to <40%).
560.prevention:
Management focuses on replacing the deficient clotting factor to treat or prevent bleeding episodes. This is achieved through intravenous infusions of recombinant or plasma-derived Factor IX concentrates (replacement therapy), which can be administered on demand or as regular prophylaxis. 

Additionally, innovative therapies such as Fitusiran (Qfitlia®) exist. This prophylactic medication, based on RNAi technology, does not replace the factor but works by reducing antithrombin production in the liver. This increases the body's natural ability to generate thrombin, improving clotting and effectively preventing bleeding.

Regardless of pharmacological treatment, lifestyle management is crucial. Individuals at risk of hemophilia B should avoid activities with a high risk of trauma, especially head injuries, and be cautious with medications that affect platelet function, such as aspirin.
560.technical_description:
Males with pathogenic variants in the F9 gene present with hemophilia B, and the severity is usually the same if there are several affected individuals in the same family, although other environmental factors can influence it. Approximately 30% of heterozygous women (carriers of the pathogenic variant) have a factor IX coagulation activity below 40% and therefore have a risk of bleeding (even if the affected family member has mild hemophilia B). As with males, the severity of bleeding generally correlates with factor IX levels. Following major trauma or an invasive procedure, prolonged or excessive bleeding typically occurs, regardless of disease severity.

In patients with hemophilia B, the most frequent type of variant is a point mutation, mostly missense, affecting the serine protease domain of factor IX. In milder cases of hemophilia B, the affected domain of the protein is usually the EGF1 domain. Variants in the promoter region of the F9 gene have also been identified, such as the c.-35G>A variant (rs1166164399), which produces the Leyden hemophilia B phenotype. This is characterized by F9 deficiency of variable severity in childhood that spontaneously improves after puberty.
560.technical_citations:
Kartal-Kaess M, Pinto F, Labarque V, et al. Hemophilia B Leyden: characteristics and natural history in the International Pediatric Network of Hemophilia Management Registry. J Thromb Haemost. 2025 Mar.23(3):921-927.

 Konkle BA, Nakaya Fletcher S. Hemophilia B. 2000 Oct 2 [Updated 2025 Aug 7]. In: Adam MP, Feldman J, Mirzaa GM, et al., editors. GeneReviews® [Internet]. Seattle (WA): University of Washington, Seattle. 1993-2025.

 Labarque V, Mancuso ME, Kartal-Kaess M, et al. F8/F9 variants in the population-based PedNet Registry cohort compared with locus-specific genetic databases of the European Association for Haemophilia and Allied Disorders and the Centers for Disease Control and Prevention Hemophilia A or Hemophilia B Mutation Project. Res Pract Thromb Haemost. 2023 Jan 10.7(1):100036.

562.name: HNF1A-related Diabetes (MODY 3)
562.description: MODY3 diabetes is the most common form of MODY, an autosomal dominant disorder caused by mutations in the HNF1A gene.
562.overview:
Maturity-onset diabetes of the young type 3 (MODY 3) is the most prevalent form of monogenic diabetes in many populations, especially European ones. It is caused by heterozygous pathogenic variants in the HNF1A (Hepatocyte Nuclear Factor 1-alpha) gene. HNF1A is a transcription factor, meaning it regulates the expression of other genes, among which are genes essential for the proper function and insulin secretion of pancreatic beta cells.

A mutation in one of the two copies of the HNF1A gene causes this switch to be defective, leading to a progressive decline in beta cell function. Over time, these cells lose their ability to secrete enough insulin in response to glucose, causing a progressive increase in blood glucose. The disease is inherited in an autosomal dominant pattern and, unlike MODY2 or GCK-MODY, carries a high risk of diabetic complications if inadequately treated.
562.symptoms: MODY3 diabetes typically manifests clinically during adolescence or early adulthood, typically before the age of 25. Patients usually present with gradual progressive hyperglycemia and generally without diabetic ketoacidosis. Glycosuria (elevated urine glucose levels) can be observed before the onset of hyperglycemia. Without adequate treatment, patients are at risk of developing long-term vascular complications similar to those of type 1 and type 2 diabetes.
562.prevention: Treatment is fundamental in MODY3 to maintain good glycemic control and prevent complications. The most notable characteristic of this condition is hypersensitivity to sulfonylureas, a type of oral antidiabetic. Low doses of sulfonylureas (e.g., Gliclazide or Glibenclamide) are the first-line treatment. With disease progression over several decades, it may be necessary to add other drugs or transition to insulin. Regular follow-up by an endocrinologist is essential.
562.technical_description:
HNF1A-associated diabetes, also known as MODY 3, is a form of monogenic diabetes caused by variants in the HNF1A gene. This gene encodes hepatocyte nuclear factor 1-alpha (HNF1A) which is a transcription factor that is expressed in the pancreas, kidney, liver, and intestine. This factor is essential for the proper functioning of insulin-producing pancreatic beta cells. A defect in this gene causes the pancreas to not release the adequate amount of insulin, leading to hyperglycemia.

Hundreds of genetic variants in HNF1A have been described that cause this disease, ranging from missense, nonsense, or frameshift variants to duplications, insertions, or partial or total deletions of the gene. This diversity explains why the age of diagnosis can vary, although it generally occurs before the age of 25. Furthermore, it has been observed that in heterozygous patients (a single mutated copy) of HNF1A, insulin secretion in response to glucose is deficient and worsens over time due to progressive beta-cell dysfunction.
562.technical_citations:
Miyachi Y, Miyazawa T, Ogawa Y. HNF1A Mutations and Beta Cell Dysfunction in Diabetes. Int J Mol Sci. 2022 Mar 16.23(6):3222.

 Serbis A, Kantza E, Siomou E, et al. Monogenic Defects of Beta Cell Function: From Clinical Suspicion to Genetic Diagnosis and Management of Rare Types of Diabetes. Int J Mol Sci. 2024 Sep 29.25(19):10501.

563.name: GCK-related Diabetes (MODY 2)
563.description: MODY2 diabetes is a monogenic form of dominantly inherited diabetes, caused by mutations in the GCK gene, which is usually asymptomatic and characterized by a mild and stable increase in glucose levels.
563.overview:
Maturity-onset diabetes of the young type 2 (MODY 2) is one of the most common forms of monogenic diabetes. It is caused by heterozygous pathogenic variants in the GCK gene, which encodes the enzyme glucokinase that is primarily expressed in the liver and pancreas. This enzyme catalyzes the first reaction of glycolysis by transforming glucose into glucose-6-phosphate and modulates insulin secretion in response to changes in glucose levels, acting as a “glucose sensor” in the insulin-producing beta cells of the pancreas.

In individuals with MODY2, one of the two copies of the GCK gene is defective, which “detunes” the glucose sensor, setting a higher threshold. The pancreatic beta cells become less sensitive to elevated glucose levels, causing an inadequate amount of insulin to be released until glucose levels exceed a higher than usual level.
563.symptoms: The main characteristic of MODY2 is that it is usually asymptomatic. Fasting hyperglycemia (elevated glucose level) is mild, stable, and present from birth, but it usually does not cause the classic symptoms of diabetes. Generally, it is discovered incidentally in routine blood tests or during gestational diabetes screening in pregnancy. The risk of developing long-term vascular complications is equivalent to that of people without the disease, even though they have mild and persistent hyperglycemia.
563.prevention: In the vast majority of cases, MODY2 diabetes does not require pharmacological treatment. Neither insulin nor oral antidiabetics are necessary, as hyperglycemia is mild and non-progressive. A healthy lifestyle, with a balanced diet and regular exercise, is recommended, just as for the general population. The only important exception is during pregnancy, where pharmacological treatment may be necessary, making specialized monitoring crucial.
563.technical_description:
MODY associated with the GCK gene is the result of inactivating heterozygous mutations, whereas activating mutations cause hyperinsulinism and hypoglycemia.

Although rare, the presence of an inactivating pathogenic variant in homozygosity or compound heterozygosity with another pathogenic variant can lead to permanent neonatal diabetes, with a more severe course. 

To date, more than 600 inactivating mutations have been described in the 10 exons of the GCK gene that result in GCK-MODY of variable severity. 

Complete deletions or deletions of specific exons of the GCK, HNF1A, HNF1B, and HNF4A genes have been described that are not analyzed here.
563.technical_citations:
Bhattacharya S, Pappachan JM. Monogenic diabetes in children: An underdiagnosed and poorly managed clinical dilemma. World J Diabetes. 2024 Jun 15.15(6):1051-1059.

 Bonnefond A, Unnikrishnan R, Doria A, et al. Monogenic diabetes. Nat Rev Dis Primers. 2023 Mar 9.9(1):12.

 Dickens LT, Naylor RN. Clinical Management of Women with Monogenic Diabetes During Pregnancy. Curr Diab Rep. 2018 Feb 15.18(3):12.

564.name: HNF4A-related Diabetes (MODY 1)
564.description: Mutations in the HNF4A gene, a key transcription factor for pancreatic development and function, can cause early-onset diabetes sensitive to sulfonylurea treatment.
564.overview:
Maturity-onset diabetes of the young type 1 (MODY1) was the first form of monogenic, non-autoimmune diabetes mellitus to be identified. It is caused by heterozygous pathogenic variants in the HNF4A (Hepatocyte Nuclear Factor 4-alpha) gene. The HNF4A gene encodes a transcription factor that acts by regulating gene expression. In the pancreas, HNF4A is involved in the expression of several genes that regulate insulin production in the beta cells of the islets of Langerhans. A mutation in one of the two copies of the HNF4A gene leads to a progressive failure in the beta cells' ability to produce insulin.

The inheritance of HNF4A is autosomal dominant with incomplete penetrance, and together with the HNF1B (MODY5) gene, it accounts for approximately 10% of all MODY cases.
564.symptoms: MODY1 diabetes usually appears in adolescence or early adulthood, before age 25, with a clinical picture of progressive hyperglycemia very similar to MODY3. However, the clinical history often reveals key clues from birth: a birth weight above normal weight (>800 g), and some neonates require treatment for hypoglycemia in their first few days. As diabetes is established, symptoms such as increased thirst and urination may appear. If left untreated, the risk of developing long-term diabetic vascular complications increases.
564.prevention: Treatment is crucial for preventing the long-term complications of hyperglycemia. Very similarly to patients with MODY3, individuals with MODY1 present an increased response to treatment with sulfonylureas (e.g., gliclazide). These oral drugs are the therapy of choice and allow for good glycemic control with low doses for many years. Given the progressive nature of the disease, it is likely that over the decades the patient will need to intensify treatment, potentially requiring insulin. Follow-up by an endocrinologist is essential.
564.technical_description:
Maturity-onset diabetes in the young (MODY) refers to a form of monogenic diabetes characterized by early onset, non-insulin-dependent diabetes, and autosomal dominant inheritance.

MODY is clinically and genetically heterogeneous, with at least 13 associated genes described, each defining a MODY subtype. It differs from type 1 diabetes due to the absence of pancreatic autoantibodies and from type 2 diabetes due to its young age at diagnosis, absence of obesity, and absence of insulin resistance/dyslipidemia.

Pathogenic variants in the HNF1A, HNF4A, and GCK genes account for more than 80% of monogenic diabetes cases. Variants in the HNF1B gene, MODY type 5, account for less than 5% of MODY cases and, in addition to being associated with diabetes, are related to abnormal kidney development.

Complete deletions or deletions of specific exons of the GCK, HNF1A, HNF1B, and HNF4A genes have been described and are not discussed here.
564.technical_citations:
Colclough K, Patel K. How do I diagnose Maturity Onset Diabetes of the Young in my patients? Clin Endocrinol (Oxf). 2022 Oct.97(4):436-447.

 Mirshahi UL, Colclough K, Wright CF, et al. Reduced penetrance of MODY-associated HNF1A/HNF4A variants but not GCK variants in clinically unselected cohorts. Am J Hum Genet. 2022 Nov 3.109(11):2018-2028. Naylor R, Knight Johnson A, del Gaudio D. Maturity-Onset Diabetes of the Young Overview. 2018 May 24. In: Adam MP, Feldman J, Mirzaa GM, et al., editors. GeneReviews® [Internet]. Seattle (WA): University of Washington, Seattle. 1993-2025.

 Pearson ER, Starkey BJ, Powell RJ, et al. Genetic cause of hyperglycaemia and response to treatment in diabetes. Lancet. 2003 Oct 18.362(9392):1275-81.

 Pihoker C, Gilliam LK, Ellard S, et al. Prevalence, characteristics and clinical diagnosis of maturity onset diabetes of the young due to mutations in HNF1A, HNF4A, and glucokinase: results from the SEARCH for Diabetes in Youth. J Clin Endocrinol Metab. 2013 Oct.98(10):4055-62.

 Walker JT, Saunders DC, Brissova M, Powers AC. The Human Islet: Mini-Organ With Mega-"

568.name: HNF1B-related Diabetes (MODY 5)
568.description: Pathogenic variants in the HNF1B gene cause a multisystem disorder characterized mainly by renal anomalies and early-onset diabetes, termed renal cysts and diabetes syndrome (RCAD). This autosomal dominant condition requires comprehensive monitoring of the pancreas and kidneys due to the gene's critical role in the development and function of these organs.
568.overview:
The HNF1B gene encodes a transcription factor called hepatocyte nuclear factor 1-beta. This protein is essential in regulating the organ-specific gene expression of several organs, particularly the pancreas, kidneys, liver, and genitourinary tract. Pathogenic variants (mutations) in this gene disrupt these developmental processes, leading to a broad spectrum of clinical features. Although often classified as a form of maturity-onset diabetes of the young (MODY type 5), the associated diabetes is only one component of a broader syndrome.

The condition is rare, accounting for approximately less than 5% of all MODY cases. Additionally, it is inherited in an autosomal dominant pattern, meaning that a single copy of the altered gene is sufficient to cause the disorder. Approximately 50% of individuals affected by an HNF1B mutation present with a complete deletion of the gene.
568.symptoms: The main symptoms include structural abnormalities of the kidney or urinary tract (such as renal cysts, a single kidney, or hydronephrosis) present from birth and the development of diabetes, usually before the age of 25. Other common features include microvascular complications, pancreatic atrophy (leading to maldigestion), elevated liver enzymes, and genital tract abnormalities. Furthermore, affected individuals tend to develop end-stage renal disease requiring renal replacement therapy, irrespective of diabetic nephropathy. Nevertheless, the severity and combination of symptoms can vary significantly, even among members of the same family.
568.prevention: The management of HNF1B-associated disease is multidisciplinary and focuses on monitoring and treating specific symptoms. Diabetes management often requires early initiation of insulin therapy, as oral medications such as sulfonylureas are usually not effective due to pancreatic insulin deficiency. Regular monitoring of renal function, blood pressure, and liver enzymes is crucial. Pancreatic enzyme replacement therapy may be necessary for individuals with exocrine pancreatic insufficiency. Genetic counseling is recommended for affected individuals and their families.
568.technical_description:
HNF1B-associated diabetes, also known as MODY 5, is a form of monogenic diabetes characterized by its high clinical variability. Unlike other diabetes forms, this condition is actually a syndrome affecting multiple organs. In addition to early-onset diabetes, it is very common for patients to present with kidney abnormalities, such as cysts and other structural defects.

The responsible gene is HNF1B, which encodes a transcription factor essential for the proper embryonic development of the pancreas, kidneys, and urogenital system. The alterations that cause the disease can be due to various types of genetic variants. Causes include single nucleotide changes (point variants) and large deletions of the HNF1B gene, which account for 50% of cases. Furthermore, larger microdeletions in the chromosomal region 17q12 have also been identified (these are not evaluated in our study). These microdeletions eliminate not only HNF1B but also 14 other genes, and for this reason, they have been associated with an increased risk of neurodevelopmental disorders, such as autism spectrum disorder and attention deficit hyperactivity disorder.
568.technical_citations:
Hoffman LS, Fox TJ, Anastasopoulou C, et al. Maturity Onset Diabetes in the Young. 2023 Aug 14. In: StatPearls [Internet]. Treasure Island (FL): StatPearls Publishing. 2025 Jan–.

  Salguero MV, Arosemena M, Pollin T, et al. Monogenic Forms of Diabetes. 2023 Dec 20. In: Lawrence JM, Casagrande SS, Herman WH, Wexler DJ, Cefalu WT, editors. Diabetes in America [Internet]. Bethesda (MD): National Institute of Diabetes and Digestive and Kidney Diseases (NIDDK). 2023–.

572.name: Primary Hyperoxaluria Type 3 (PH3)
572.description: Primary hyperoxaluria type 3 (PH3) is a genetic disorder of glyoxylate metabolism with autosomal recessive inheritance caused by mutations in the HOGA1 gene. It is characterized by an overproduction of oxalate resulting in recurrent formation of calcium oxalate kidney stones, typically from early childhood.
572.overview:
The HOGA1 gene provides instructions for making the mitochondrial enzyme 4-hydroxy-2-oxoglutarate aldolase 1, which is found to be active mainly in the liver and kidneys. This enzyme plays a key role in the breakdown of the amino acid hydroxyproline. When variants in the HOGA1 gene result in a deficient or non-functional enzyme, the metabolic pathway is blocked, causing an accumulation of precursors, especially the substrate of the HOGA1 enzyme, 4-hydroxy-2-oxoglutarate (HOG), which eventually converts to an excess of oxalate. 

This overproduction of oxalate, a substance that is normally excreted as waste, causes it to combine with calcium in the urine, forming kidney stones (nephrolithiasis). PH3 is inherited in an autosomal recessive pattern, meaning an individual must inherit a mutated copy of the HOGA1 gene from both parents to be affected. It is a rare type of hyperoxaluria, representing about 10% of all Primary Hyperoxaluria cases, and affects all populations. Nonetheless, the frequency of carrier individuals suggests that the prevalence of the disease is likely underestimated.
572.symptoms: The main, and often only, manifestation of PH3 is recurrent kidney stones (urolithiasis) and a marked increase in urine calcium levels (hypercalciuria). The age of onset is typically very early, with a median age at diagnosis between 2 and 4 years. Symptoms are related to kidney stones and may include abdominal or flank pain, blood in the urine (hematuria), frequent urination, and urinary tract infections. Unlike other types of primary hyperoxaluria, progression to chronic kidney disease or kidney failure is very rare in PH3, and most patients preserve kidney function.
572.prevention: In the treatment of PH3, the goal is to prevent the formation of kidney stones. This involves a high fluid intake (hyperhydration) to maintain a high urine volume, which dilutes the oxalate concentration. Potassium citrate treatment is also standard, as it helps to alkalinize the urine and is a potent inhibitor of calcium oxalate crystal formation. Close monitoring by a nephrologist is essential for managing symptoms and preserving kidney function.
572.technical_description:
The ClinVar database collects more than 80 pathogenic variants in the HOGA1 gene associated with Primary Hyperoxaluria type 3 (PH3). The frequency of these variants varies significantly among different populations, with some founder mutations predominating in specific ethnic groups.

In Europe, the most common variant is c.700+5G>T (rs185803104), which accounts for approximately 50% of the alleles causing PH3. On the other hand, the p.Glu315del (rs397509360) variant is mainly found in individuals of Ashkenazi Jewish descent.

In Asian populations, specifically in patients from China, the most frequent pathogenic variants are c.834G>A (rs770050262), c.834_834+1delinsTT (rs1564760008), and c.834+1G>T (rs749315029). Finally, the c.569C>T (rs202047589) variant has been identified in patients of Arab descent from Lebanon and Tunisia.

Abid A. Possible ethnic associations in primary hyperoxaluria type-III-associated HOGA1 sequence variants. Mol Biol Rep. 2021 Apr.48(4):3841-3844.

 Cellini B. A molecular journey on the pathogenesis of primary hyperoxaluria. Curr Opin Nephrol Hypertens. 2024 Jul 1.33(4):398-404.

577.name: Pontocerebellar Hypoplasia Type 9 (AMPD2 gene)
577.description: Pontocerebellar Hypoplasia type 9 is a severe neurodegenerative disorder of prenatal onset, caused by mutations in the AMPD2 gene that alter brain development. This condition, of autosomal recessive inheritance, causes profound developmental delay and is extremely rare in the general population.
577.overview:
Pontocerebellar hypoplasia type 9 (PCH9) is a neurodegenerative genetic disease caused by pathogenic variants in the AMPD2 gene. This gene is fundamental for the production of the enzyme adenosine monophosphate deaminase 2, which plays a crucial role in purine synthesis, a vital process for the correct development and survival of neurons, especially during neurogenesis. The deficiency of this enzyme critically affects the formation of the brainstem and cerebellum, leading to tissue loss (hypoplasia or atrophy) in these key brain areas for motor control and other essential functions.

PCH9 is an extremely rare condition, with an unknown precise incidence, although it falls within the group of pontocerebellar hypoplasias, whose general prevalence is estimated at less than 1 in every 200,000 births. Being an autosomal recessive disorder, it affects men and women equally. Although cases have been reported in various populations worldwide, its frequency may be slightly higher in communities where there is a higher degree of consanguinity, due to the nature of its inheritance pattern.
577.symptoms: The symptoms of PCH9 are severe and manifest from birth or during the first months of life. Affected individuals present with very severe global developmental delay, postnatal microcephaly (the head does not grow properly after birth), spasticity (muscle stiffness), and frequently, epileptic seizures. Truncal hypotonia, swallowing difficulties, and cortical visual impairment are also common.
577.prevention: Currently, there is no cure for Pontocerebellar Hypoplasia type 9, so management is purely symptomatic and supportive. The approach is multidisciplinary and focuses on improving the patient's quality of life, including physiotherapy to manage spasticity, antiepileptic drugs to control seizures, and nutritional support via gastrostomy tube if swallowing is unsafe. Genetic counseling is essential for families.
577.technical_description:
Pontocerebellar hypoplasia type 9 is an autosomal recessive hereditary disorder. The cause is the presence of mutations in the AMPD2 gene, a fundamental gene in the purine synthesis pathway. The pathogenic variants described so far are, fundamentally, loss-of-function, preventing the enzyme from being produced or from functioning correctly. 

This disease is extremely rare and most of the variants described are family-specific. These can be of various types, including missense variants, such as p.Arg620Ser, in which an amino acid in the protein is changed, nonsense variants, such as p.Gln107Ter, which introduce a premature stop signal, splicing variants, such as 1698+1G>A, which affect the messenger RNA maturation process, and small deletions or insertions, such as c.252_253insAGTGAGC.

To date, no clear genotype-phenotype correlation has been established. All diagnosed individuals present with homogeneous clinical manifestations, suggesting that the presence of any variant causing impairment in enzyme function is sufficient to trigger the phenotype.
577.technical_citations:
Pontocerebellar hypoplasia type 9. Orphanet. ORPHA369920 [June 2025].

  Pontocerebellar hypoplasia. Medline [November 1, 2014]. 

  Abdelrahman HA, Akawi N, Al-Shamsi AM, Al-Gazali L, Ali BR. Pontocerebellar Hypoplasia Type 9: A New Case with a Novel Mutation and Review of Literature. J Pediatr Genet. 2022 May 20.13(3):215-222.

  van Dijk T, Baas F, Barth PG, Poll-The BT. What's new in pontocerebellar hypoplasia? An update on genes and subtypes. Orphanet J Rare Dis. 2018 Jun 15.13(1):92.

578.name: Pontocerebellar Hypoplasia Type 8 (CHMP1A gene)
578.description: Pontocerebellar hypoplasia type 8 is an autosomal recessive neurodevelopmental disorder, caused by mutations in the CHMP1A gene. This condition is characterized by insufficient development of the cerebellum and brainstem pons, resulting in progressive microcephaly and a very severe global developmental delay.
578.overview:
Pontocerebellar hypoplasia type 8 (PCH8) is a rare genetic disorder caused by pathogenic variants in the *CHMP1A* gene. This gene encodes a protein that is part of the ESCRT-III complex (endosomal sorting complexes required for transport), which is essential for vital cellular processes such as protein trafficking, autophagy, and cell division. Loss-of-function mutations in *CHMP1A* disrupt these pathways, which appears to be particularly detrimental to neuron survival and development during brain formation. The impact is particularly severe in the cerebellum and brainstem, structures that do not develop properly, leading to the characteristic hypoplasia.

PCH8 is an extremely infrequent condition, with very few cases described in the medical literature, so its exact incidence in the general population is unknown. As an autosomal recessive disorder, it affects males and females equally. A founder mutation (a specific mutation that originated in a common ancestor) has been identified in the Hutterite population, a religious community of European origin living in North America, which explains a higher frequency of the disease within this specific population group.
578.symptoms: PCH8 symptoms are evident from the prenatal period or in the first months of life. The main clinical finding is progressive microcephaly (abnormally small head), often detected even in utero. Affected children present with profound global developmental delay, severe intellectual disability, spasticity or stiffness in the limbs, and truncal hypotonia. Seizures are very frequent and often difficult to control.
578.prevention: Currently, there is no curative treatment for Pontocerebellar Hypoplasia type 8, so the therapeutic approach is supportive and focuses on managing symptoms to improve quality of life. This multidisciplinary management includes antiepileptic drugs for seizure control, physical therapy to address spasticity and improve mobility, and nutritional support, which may require a feeding tube. Genetic counseling is an essential component for affected families.
578.technical_description:
Pontocerebellar hypoplasia type 8 is caused by the presence of loss-of-function mutations in the CHMP1A gene, which follow an autosomal recessive inheritance pattern. Loss of function of the CHMP1A protein severely affects neuronal proliferation, migration, and survival during embryonic development. 

It is an extremely rare disease for which very few associated variants have been described, all of them loss-of-function, causing the production of a non-functional protein or its absence.
578.technical_citations:
Pontocerebellar hypoplasia type 8. Orphanet. ORPHA324569 [June 2025].

  Pontocerebellar hypoplasia. Medline [November 1, 2014]. 

  van Dijk T, Baas F, Barth PG, Poll-The BT. What's new in pontocerebellar hypoplasia? An update on genes and subtypes. Orphanet J Rare Dis. 2018 Jun 15.13(1):92.

579.name: Pontocerebellar Hypoplasia Type 10 (CLP1 gene)
579.description: Pontocerebellar hypoplasia type 10 is a severe neurological disorder of autosomal recessive inheritance, caused by mutations in the CLP1 gene. This condition interrupts an essential cellular process for RNA maturation, leading to neuron loss in key brain areas and causing progressive microcephaly with severe neurological deterioration from birth.
579.overview:
Pontocerebellar hypoplasia type 10 (PCH10) is an extremely rare neurodegenerative disease, with only a small number of cases described worldwide. Its genetic cause lies in mutations in both copies of the CLP1 gene, which encodes a crucial enzyme (Clp1 kinase) for the processing and maturation of transfer RNAs (tRNAs). In the absence of a functional CLP1 enzyme, tRNA precursors are not processed correctly, accumulating in neurons and causing their programmed cell death (apoptosis). This damage disproportionately affects neurons in the pons, cerebellum, and motor cortex, brain structures vital for coordination, movement, and other complex functions.

The disease is inherited in an autosomal recessive manner, meaning an individual must receive a defective copy of the CLP1 gene from each of their parents. Unlike other genetic disorders, PCH10 does not appear to have a higher incidence in a specific population or ethnicity, with cases identified in families of diverse backgrounds. The result of these genetic alterations is progressive deterioration that begins in the prenatal stage and manifests with severity from birth.
579.symptoms: The symptoms of PCH10 are evident in the neonatal period. Newborns present with microcephaly (small head) that worsens over time, severe hypotonia (low muscle tone), and feeding difficulties. As the months pass, profound psychomotor developmental delay, spasticity, and epileptic seizures, which are often resistant to treatment, become apparent. Neurological function progressively deteriorates during childhood.
579.prevention: Currently, there is no cure for PCH10, so treatment is palliative and supportive, focused on managing symptoms and improving the patient's quality of life. This management requires a multidisciplinary team that includes neurologists, pediatricians, and therapists. Pharmacological treatment focuses on seizure control with antiepileptic drugs. Nutritional support, often through a gastrostomy tube, is essential to ensure adequate caloric intake.
579.technical_description: Pontocerebellar hypoplasia type 10 is caused by the presence of pathogenic variants in the CLP1 gene. Curiously, the identified mutations are almost exclusively of the missense type. In these variants, a single amino acid of the protein is altered, causing the loss of its function. An example is the p.Arg140His (c.419G>A) variant, one of the most frequent and considered a founder mutation in the Turkish population, although it has been found in patients of other origins.
579.technical_citations:
Pontocerebellar hypoplasia. Medline [November 1, 2014]. 

  Guler S, Aslanger AD, Uygur Sahin T, et al. Long-Term Disease Course of Pontocerebellar Hypoplasia Type 10. Pediatr Neurol. 2024 Sep.158:1-10.

  van Dijk T, Baas F, Barth PG, Poll-The BT. What's new in pontocerebellar hypoplasia? An update on genes and subtypes. Orphanet J Rare Dis. 2018 Jun 15.13(1):92.

580.name: Pontocerebellar Hypoplasia Type 1B (EXOSC3 gene)
580.description: Pontocerebellar hypoplasia type 1B (PCH1B) is a severe neurodegenerative disorder, of autosomal recessive inheritance, resulting from mutations in the EXOSC3 gene. Clinically similar to PCH1A, the condition involves underdevelopment (hypoplasia) of the pons and cerebellum along with progressive degeneration of spinal motor neurons.
580.overview:
The EXOSC3 gene provides instructions for making a core component of the RNA exosome complex. This complex is a crucial piece of cellular machinery responsible for processing and degrading various types of RNA molecules. It plays a vital role in RNA quality control, ensuring that only properly formed RNA molecules are used to create proteins. When mutations in both copies of the EXOSC3 gene alter the function of this complex, cellular RNA metabolism is disrupted.

This disruption is particularly detrimental to the development and survival of highly active cells such as neurons. In PCH1B, loss of function specifically affects neurons in the cerebellum, pons, and motor neurons of the spinal cord. This leads to the characteristic combination of brain malformation and severe, progressive muscle weakness, similar to spinal muscular atrophy (SMA). PCH1B is inherited in an autosomal recessive pattern and is an extremely rare cause of congenital neurological disease.
580.symptoms: The symptoms of PCH1B are usually present at birth, beginning with severe muscle weakness (hypotonia) and progressive muscle atrophy due to the loss of motor neurons. This leads to profound difficulties with movement, swallowing, and often breathing. Over time, individuals may develop joint stiffness, seizures, and various ophthalmological problems such as nystagmus or optic atrophy.
580.prevention: Currently there is no cure or treatment that modifies the course of PCH1B. Management is entirely supportive and focuses on improving the patient's quality of life. This requires a multidisciplinary team and often includes respiratory support, often with mechanical ventilation, and nutritional support via a feeding tube (gastrostomy tube) to ensure adequate caloric intake. Physical therapy can help manage joint stiffness (contractures).
580.technical_description:
Although numerous variants in the EXOSC3 gene can cause PCH1B, some have been studied in greater detail due to their frequency or specific clinical associations.

The most common pathogenic variant described in the scientific literature is c.395A>C (p.Asp132Ala, rs141138948). Generally, it is associated with a milder form of the disease. According to the gnomAD database, this variant has an allelic frequency of approximately 0.0009 in the non-Finnish European population, meaning it is found in about 1 in 1,100 individuals. Affected patients typically inherit this variant from both parents (homozygosity) or inherit it from one parent and a different pathogenic variant from the other (compound heterozygosity), such as when combined with the variant c.238G>T (p.Val80Phe, rs374550999).

Furthermore, certain variants have been observed to have a significantly higher prevalence in specific populations. A notable example is the variant c.92G>C (p.Gly31Ala). This variant shows a high carrier frequency, reaching 4% in the Romani population of the Czech Republic. This implies that approximately 1 in 25 individuals in this group carries one copy of this specific variant.
