Baas F, van Dijk T. EXOSC3 Pontocerebellar Hypoplasia. 2014 Aug 21 [Updated 2020 Sep 24]. In: Adam MP, Feldman J, Mirzaa GM, et al., editors. GeneReviews® [Internet]. Seattle (WA): University of Washington, Seattle. 1993-2025.

 Ivanov I, Atkinson D, Litvinenko I, et al. Pontocerebellar hypoplasia type 1 for the neuropediatrician: Genotype-phenotype correlations and diagnostic guidelines based on new cases and overview of the literature. Eur J Paediatr Neurol. 2018 Jul.22(4):674-681.

 Schwabova J, Brozkova DS, Petrak B, et al. Homozygous EXOSC3 mutation c.92G→C, p.G31A is a founder mutation causing severe pontocerebellar hypoplasia type 1 among the Czech Roma. J Neurogenet. 2013 Dec.27(4):163-9.

582.name: Pontocerebellar Hypoplasia Type 6 (RARS2 gene)
582.description: Pontocerebellar hypoplasia type 6 is an autosomal recessive neurodegenerative disorder, caused by mutations in the RARS2 gene that alter energy production in the mitochondria. This condition, which manifests at birth, is characterized by intractable epilepsy and profound developmental delay due to insufficient brain development.
582.overview:
Pontocerebellar hypoplasia type 6 (PCH6) is a severe mitochondrial encephalopathy caused by pathogenic variants in the RARS2 gene. This gene contains the instructions for producing the mitochondrial enzyme arginyl-tRNA synthetase, which is essential for protein synthesis within the mitochondria, the cell's powerhouses. This enzyme is responsible for attaching the amino acid arginine to its transport molecule (tRNA), a crucial step for building the proteins that make up the mitochondrial respiratory chain, key for cellular energy production (ATP). RARS2 dysfunction collapses this process, critically affecting tissues with high energy demands, such as the developing brain, resulting in neuronal death and atrophy or hypoplasia of the pons and cerebellum.

PCH6 is an extremely rare disease, with few cases described worldwide. Being an autosomal recessive disorder, it affects males and females equally. Although it has been identified in various ethnicities, a remarkably higher prevalence has been observed in the population of Finland. This is due to a specific founder mutation (an ancestral genetic variant) that is more common in this population group, leading to a higher incidence of the disease in this region.
582.symptoms: The onset of PCH6 symptoms typically occurs in the neonatal period or in the first months of life, with severe and treatment-resistant epileptic seizures often being the first warning sign. Affected infants present with profound global developmental delay, failing to reach psychomotor milestones. Also characteristic are microcephaly (small head), hypotonia (low muscle tone), feeding difficulties, and respiratory problems.
582.prevention: There is currently no cure for Pontocerebellar Hypoplasia type 6, and treatment is purely supportive and palliative. Management focuses on controlling seizures, although these are often refractory to multiple antiepileptic drugs. Care is multidisciplinary and includes nutritional support, often via a gastrostomy tube, physical therapy, and management of respiratory complications. The prognosis of the disease is severe.
582.technical_description:
PCH6 is a disease with an autosomal recessive inheritance pattern, caused by the presence of mutations in the RARS2 gene. This gene encodes for the mitochondrial arginyl-tRNA synthetase enzyme, which is essential in the process of mitochondrial protein synthesis. The pathogenic variants described are loss-of-function, which reduce or abolish enzyme activity, leading to a severe energy deficit that particularly affects the neurons of the developing brain. 

The mutations described to date are of different natures, including nonsense variants, which introduce a premature stop codon (such as p.Gln200Ter), missense variants, in which a single amino acid is changed (such as p.Ile150Thr), splicing variants (such as c.298-1G>A), and small deletions/insertions (such as c.284_285del).
582.technical_citations:
Pontocerebellar hypoplasia type 6. Orphanet. ORPHA166073 [June 2025].

  Pontocerebellar hypoplasia. Medline [November 1, 2014]. 

  van Dijk T, Baas F, Barth PG, Poll-The BT. What's new in pontocerebellar hypoplasia? An update on genes and subtypes. Orphanet J Rare Dis. 2018 Jun 15.13(1):92.

  Zhao S, Lian R, Jin L, et al. Clinical and genetic analysis of infants with pontocerebellar hypoplasia type 6 caused by RARS2 variations. Epilepsia Open. 2024 Feb.9(1):250-257.

583.name: Pontocerebellar Hypoplasia Type 2D (SEPSECS gene)
583.description: Pontocerebellar hypoplasia type 2D is a neurodegenerative pathology of autosomal recessive inheritance, caused by pathogenic variants in the SEPSECS gene. This gene is crucial for the synthesis of the amino acid selenocysteine.
583.overview:
Pontocerebellar hypoplasia type 2D (PCH2D) is a rare and severe form of neurological disease that affects brain development. The cause lies in mutations in the SEPSECS gene, whose function is indispensable for the production of selenoproteins. Selenoproteins are essential for the proper development of the mammalian central nervous system. These proteins are fundamental for protecting cells from oxidative stress damage and for other vital cellular functions.

When the SEPSECS gene is altered, the synthesis of functional selenoproteins is severely affected. The developing brain is extremely vulnerable to oxidative stress, so the absence of these protective proteins leads to neuronal death, disproportionately affecting the cerebellum and brainstem and causing their underdevelopment (hypoplasia). PCH2D is inherited in an autosomal recessive pattern. Although it is an ultra-rare disease, it has been described in diverse ethnicities, with certain founder mutations identified in specific populations, such as in families of Sephardic Jewish origin.
583.symptoms: Clinically, PCH2D manifests very similarly to other subtypes of PCH2, with symptoms appearing in the neonatal period or in the first months of life. The symptoms of PCH2D appear to be somewhat less severe than other forms of pontocerebellar hypoplasia. The most characteristic sign is progressive microcephaly, in which the head circumference does not increase adequately. It is accompanied by global developmental delay, initial hypotonia that often evolves to spasticity, the onset of movement disorders, and epileptic seizures.
583.prevention: Currently, there is no curative treatment for PCH2D. Management is supportive and focuses on treating symptoms to improve the patient's quality of life. A multidisciplinary team is essential to address complex medical needs, which include controlling epileptic seizures with antiepileptic drugs and managing spasticity and dystonia.
583.technical_description:
Pontocerebellar hypoplasia is a group of autosomal recessive disorders affecting different brain structures, particularly the brainstem and cerebellum. Most types of pontocerebellar hypoplasia are due to mutations in genes involved in tRNA processing or RNA transport: EXOSC3, TSEN, SEPSECS, RARS2 and CLP1.

SEPSECS encodes for Sec tRNA synthase or SepSecS, which participates in one of the final steps of selenocysteine amino acid synthesis. Two of the missense variants of SEPSECS that completely reduce its function are the variant c.715G>A (p.Ala239Thr, rs267607035) which has an allele frequency of 0.00001 and the variant c.1001A>G (p.Tyr334Cys, rs267607036).
583.technical_citations:
Pettinato F, Marzà V, Ciantia F, et al. Acute neurological regression following fever as presenting sign of pontocerebellar hypoplasia type 2D (SEPSECS mutation). Biomed Rep. 2025 Feb 14.22(4):67.

 Puppala AK, French RL, Matthies D, et al. Structural basis for early-onset neurological disorders caused by mutations in human selenocysteine synthase. Sci Rep. 2016 Aug 31.6:32563.

 Namavar Y, Barth PG, Poll-The BT, Baas F. Classification, diagnosis and potential mechanisms in pontocerebellar hypoplasia. Orphanet J Rare Dis. 2011 Jul 12.6:50.

 Schoenmakers E, Chatterjee K. Human Genetic Disorders Resulting in Systemic Selenoprotein Deficiency. Int J Mol Sci. 2021 Nov 29.22(23):12927. doi: 10.3390/ijms222312927.

584.name: Pontocerebellar Hypoplasia Type 11 (TBC1D23 gene)
584.description: Pontocerebellar hypoplasia type 11 is an autosomal recessive neurodevelopmental disorder caused by mutations in the TBC1D23 gene. This condition, which manifests from birth, is characterized by insufficient development of the pons and cerebellum, leading to severe psychomotor delay and intellectual disability.
584.overview:
Pontocerebellar hypoplasia type 11 (PCH11) is a rare neurological disorder, with an estimated prevalence of less than 1 in 1,000,000 people. It originates from loss-of-function mutations in both copies of the TBC1D23 gene. This gene is crucial for proper brain development, as it encodes a protein involved in vesicle transport within cells, a process essential for neuronal communication and organization, especially in the Golgi apparatus. The alteration of this function disrupts the proper formation of key brain structures during fetal development, specifically the pontine brainstem and the cerebellum.

Unlike other types of pontocerebellar hypoplasia, PCH11 is considered a non-degenerative form, meaning that the primary neurological damage occurs during development and does not significantly progress over time. The inheritance of this condition is autosomal recessive, implying that an individual must inherit one mutated copy of the TBC1D23 gene from each of their parents to manifest the disease. No increased incidence has been described in specific populations, with cases reported in families of diverse ethnic backgrounds worldwide.
584.symptoms: Symptoms of PCH11 are evident from birth or during the first months of life. The clinical picture includes microcephaly (small head size), a global and severe delay in psychomotor development, and intellectual disability. Affected individuals present with ataxia (difficulty coordinating movements), hypotonia (low muscle tone), and in many cases, epileptic seizures and characteristic dysmorphic facial features.
584.prevention: Currently, there is no cure for pontocerebellar hypoplasia type 11, so management is purely symptomatic and supportive. It requires a multidisciplinary approach that may include pediatricians, neurologists, physical therapists, occupational therapists, and speech therapists. Pharmacological treatments focus on controlling seizures with antiepileptic drugs. Physical therapy and occupational therapy are crucial for managing motor problems and improving quality of life.
584.technical_description: Pontocerebellar hypoplasia type 11 is caused by the presence of mutations that inactivate the TBC1D23 gene. Therefore, the variants identified to date are of the loss-of-function type. The most studied and recurrent genetic variants include frameshift mutations, such as p.Phe205fs, which result in the production of a truncated protein, and splicing mutations, such as c.1092+2T>A, which affect the boundaries between exons and introns, causing misprocessing and an abnormal protein.
584.technical_citations:
Pontocerebellar hypoplasia. Medline [November 1, 2014]. 

  Laugwitz L, Buchert R, Groeschel S, et al. Pontocerebellar hypoplasia type 11: Does the genetic defect determine timing of cerebellar pathology? Eur J Med Genet. 2020 Jul.63(7):103938.

  van Dijk T, Baas F, Barth PG, Poll-The BT. What's new in pontocerebellar hypoplasia? An update on genes and subtypes. Orphanet J Rare Dis. 2018 Jun 15.13(1):92.

585.name: Pontocerebellar Hypoplasia Type 7 (TOE1 gene)
585.description: Pontocerebellar hypoplasia type 7 is a neurodegenerative disorder of autosomal recessive inheritance, caused by mutations in the TOE1 gene. This extremely rare condition prevents the proper development of the brain, primarily the pons and cerebellum, and is characteristically associated with gonadal development alterations in 46,XY individuals.
585.overview:
Pontocerebellar hypoplasia type 7 (PCH7) is a severe genetic disorder caused by pathogenic variants in the TOE1 gene. This gene encodes an exonuclease, an enzyme crucial for the maturation and processing of small nuclear RNAs (snRNAs), molecules essential for proper cellular function. Deficiency in TOE1 function disrupts these fundamental processes, proving especially devastating to neurons during fetal development and leading to insufficient formation (hypoplasia) of the brainstem pons and cerebellum, vital brain structures for motor coordination and other essential functions.

PCH7 is an exceptionally rare condition, with an estimated prevalence of less than 1 in 1,000,000 people. It affects both sexes equally and is inherited in an autosomal recessive pattern. Although cases have been described in diverse populations, no ethnic group with a higher predisposition has been identified. A distinctive characteristic of PCH7 is its strong association with disorders of sex development (DSD) in individuals with a 46,XY karyotype, which differentiates it from other types of pontocerebellar hypoplasia.
585.symptoms: PCH7 symptoms are severe and manifest in the neonatal period or in the first months of life. Affected individuals present progressive microcephaly (small head size), severe truncal hypotonia (lack of muscle tone in the trunk) combined with limb hypertonia, and profound psychomotor developmental delay. Seizures, respiratory difficulties, and feeding problems are common. In children with a 46,XY karyotype, the presence of ambiguous or underdeveloped genitalia is characteristic.
585.prevention: Currently, there is no cure for Pontocerebellar Hypoplasia type 7, so management is supportive and focuses on symptoms. A multidisciplinary team addresses complications, using antiepileptic drugs to control seizures, nutritional support (often via feeding tube), and respiratory assistance when needed. Physical therapy is important for managing muscle tone issues. Genetic counseling is a cornerstone for families.
585.technical_description:
PCH7 is associated with the presence of mutations in the TOE1 gene and follows an autosomal recessive inheritance pattern. This gene encodes a protein of the same name, involved in the process of splicing or messenger RNA processing. A dysfunction of this basic cellular mechanism affects all tissues, but has a particularly significant impact on the development of the central nervous system and gonads.

The identified variants are loss-of-function, causing a non-functional protein or its absence. This is an extremely rare pathology, and most of the described mutations are exclusive to the few affected families. The spectrum of mutations includes nonsense variants, which introduce a premature stop codon (e.g., p.Arg182Ter), missense variants, which alter a single amino acid (such as p.Arg184Leu), splicing variants (such as c.237-2A>G), or small deletions or insertions (such as c.940_941del).
585.technical_citations:
Pontocerebellar hypoplasia type 7. Orphanet. ORPHA284339 [June 2025].

  Pontocerebellar hypoplasia. Medline [November 1, 2014]. 

  van Dijk T, Baas F, Barth PG, Poll-The BT. What's new in pontocerebellar hypoplasia? An update on genes and subtypes. Orphanet J Rare Dis. 2018 Jun 15.13(1):92.

587.name: Pontocerebellar Hypoplasia Type 2B (TSEN2 gene)
587.description: Pontocerebellar hypoplasia type 2B is an autosomal recessive neurodegenerative disorder, caused by pathogenic variants in the TSEN2 gene. This gene is vital for tRNA maturation, molecules essential for protein synthesis.
587.overview:
Pontocerebellar Hypoplasia Type 2B (PCH2B) is a rare genetic disease that severely affects brain development. It is caused by mutations in the TSEN2 gene, which encodes a subunit of the "tRNA splicing endonuclease" enzyme complex. This complex is an indispensable molecular editor that processes and matures transfer RNAs (tRNAs). tRNAs are crucial in the cell, as they act as translators that read the genetic code and transport the correct amino acids to build all proteins. A dysfunction in the TSEN2 gene causes this editing process to fail, preventing the correct production of proteins.

This deficiency is especially toxic to neurons during fetal development and consequently alters the development of key brain structures such as the pons and cerebellum.

PCH2B is inherited in an autosomal recessive pattern, meaning an individual must receive a mutated copy of the gene from each of their parents to develop the disease.
587.symptoms: The symptoms of PCH2B are evident from birth or the first few months of life. The most characteristic clinical finding is progressive microcephaly, where the baby's head does not grow at the expected rate. It is accompanied by global developmental delay, with an almost complete absence of motor or cognitive milestone acquisition. Extrapyramidal movement disorders are common, such as involuntary and jerky movements (chorea) or abnormal postures (dystonia), along with swallowing problems, spasticity, and frequent seizures.
587.prevention: Currently, there is no cure for HCH2B, so management focuses on symptomatic treatment and supportive care to improve quality of life. A multidisciplinary team addresses the different complications: antiepileptic drugs are used to control seizures, and other medications are used to manage spasticity and movement disorders.
587.technical_description:
The TSEN complex is composed of four different subunits: two catalytic subunits encoded by TSEN2 and TSEN34, and two structural subunits encoded by TSEN15 and TSEN54.

The ClinVar database currently collects fewer than 50 pathogenic or possibly pathogenic variants in the TSEN2 gene that have been linked to PCH2B, among which are missense variants and those affecting splicing sites (c.960+1delGTAAG).
587.technical_citations: Hayashi Y, Hamada K, Rethanavelu K, et al. Biallelic TSEN2 variants causing pontocerebellar hypoplasia type 2. J Hum Genet. 2025 Aug 27. Namavar Y, Barth PG, Kasher PR, et al. Clinical, neuroradiological and genetic findings in pontocerebellar hypoplasia. Brain. 2011 Jan.134(Pt 1):143-56. van Dijk T, Baas F. TSEN54 Pontocerebellar Hypoplasia. 2009 Sep 8 [Updated 2020 May 28]. In: Adam MP, Feldman J, Mirzaa GM, et al., editors. GeneReviews® [Internet]. Seattle (WA): University of Washington, Seattle. 1993-2025.

589.name: Pontocerebellar Hypoplasia Type 1A (VRK1 gene)
589.description: Pontocerebellar hypoplasia type 1A is a severe neurodegenerative disorder, of autosomal recessive inheritance, caused by mutations in the VRK1 gene. The condition is characterized by the underdevelopment (hypoplasia) of the brainstem pons and cerebellum, combined with the loss of motor neurons in the spinal cord.
589.overview:
The VRK1 gene provides instructions for making a serine-threonine kinase protein, an enzyme that plays a critical role in basic cellular processes, including cell division, DNA damage response, and Cajal body assembly. This enzyme is vital for the proper development and survival of neurons. When mutations in both copies of the VRK1 gene impair its function, the proliferation and maintenance of specific neuronal populations are severely affected during fetal development.

This deterioration particularly affects the cerebellum, pons, and anterior horn cells of the spinal cord (motor neurons). The result is a combination of a structural brain malformation (pontocerebellar hypoplasia) and a condition resembling the most severe form of spinal muscular atrophy (SMA). PCH1A is an extremely rare disorder inherited in an autosomal recessive pattern, meaning that an affected individual must receive a mutated gene from both parents.
589.symptoms: PCH1A symptoms are present at birth and are profound. They include generalized and severe muscle weakness (hypotonia), leading to an inability to move, breathe, or feed effectively. Other common features include congenital joint contractures (arthrogryposis), microcephaly, and, often, seizures.
589.prevention: There is no cure for PCH1A. Medical management is entirely supportive, palliative, and focused on symptom control. This includes respiratory and nutritional support. Medications can be used to control seizures. A multidisciplinary palliative care team is essential to support the family and coordinate care.
589.technical_description:
Currently, there are four genes associated with PCH1 (PCH1A-D). Of the 4 PCH1 genes, the EXOSC3 gene is typically altered in PCH1 patients. Pathogenic variants of the VRK1 gene that cause PCH1A have been associated with various neurodevelopmental and neuromuscular disorders, such as microcephaly, and motor neuron disorders. Pathogenic variants of VRK1 follow an autosomal recessive mode of inheritance.

The c.1160G>A (p.Arg387His, psychomotor development) variant which replaces an arginine with a histidine is one of the most studied and affects a highly conserved residue believed to be involved in nucleosome binding. The variant has been observed in homozygosis and in compound heterozygosis in patients with PCH1A and in patients with phenotypes similar to spinal muscular atrophy, it has also been identified in individuals affected by adult-onset distal motor neuropathy.
589.technical_citations:
Lazo PA, Morejón-García P. VRK1 variants at the cross road of Cajal body neuropathogenic mechanisms in distal neuropathies and motor neuron diseases. Neurobiol Dis. 2023 Jul.183:106172.

 Škarica M, Acsadi G, Živković SA. Pontocerebellar Hypoplasia Type 1 and Associated Neuronopathies. Genes (Basel). 2025 May 15.16(5):585.

 van Dijk T, Baas F, Barth PG, Poll-The BT. What's new in pontocerebellar hypoplasia? An update on genes and subtypes. Orphanet J Rare Dis. 2018 Jun 15.13(1):92.

 Živković SA, Nowak RJ, DiCapua D. CMT2 and distal hereditary motor neuropathy associated with VRK1 variants: Case series. Neuromuscul Disord. 2025 Feb.47:105254.

591.name: Neuronal Ceroid Lipofuscinosis Type 2 (TPP1 gene)
591.description: Neuronal ceroid lipofuscinosis type 2 (CLN2) is a rare, inherited neurodegenerative disorder caused by mutations in the TPP1 gene. This genetic defect leads to a deficiency of the enzyme tripeptidyl peptidase 1, resulting in the harmful accumulation of waste products within cells, particularly neurons.
591.overview:
The TPP1 gene expresses an enzyme called tripeptidyl peptidase 1 (TPP1), which is crucial for the normal function of lysosomes, our cells' recycling centers. This enzyme helps break down specific proteins and peptides. In individuals with neuronal ceroid lipofuscinosis type 2 (CLN2), having two copies of a mutation in the TPP1 gene causes a severe deficiency in the activity of this enzyme.

As a consequence, waste materials known as ceroid lipofuscins accumulate to toxic levels within lysosomes, particularly in the nerve cells of the brain. This relentless accumulation causes progressive neuronal damage and death, leading to the symptoms of the disease. CLN2 is an autosomal recessive disorder with an estimated incidence of 0.2 to 1 in every 100,000 live births worldwide.
591.symptoms: CLN2 disease typically manifests in late infancy, between 2 and 4 years of age. Early signs often include delayed language development and the onset of seizures, particularly myoclonic jerks and atonic seizures. As the disease progresses rapidly, children experience loss of motor skills (ataxia), cognitive decline leading to dementia, and vision loss culminating in blindness. Without treatment, survival beyond adolescence is rare. An atypical phenotype has also been described in which the onset of clinical manifestations is later and the progression is slow.
591.prevention: Management of CLN2 focuses on treating the underlying enzyme deficiency and controlling symptoms. The treatment of choice is enzyme replacement therapy (ERT) with cerliponase alfa (Brineura®), which is administered directly into the brain ventricles to replace the missing TPP1 enzyme and slow motor and speech deterioration. Supportive care is also essential, including anti-seizure medications to control epilepsy, along with physical, occupational, and speech therapies to maximize function and quality of life.
591.technical_description:
Neuronal ceroid lipofuscinosis type 2 (NCL2) is a rare neurodegenerative disease caused by alterations in the TPP1 gene. This gene expresses the enzyme tripeptidyl peptidase 1, responsible for waste recycling within cell centers known as lysosomes. A failure in this enzyme leads to a toxic accumulation of material in neurons, triggering the disease. Genetically, NCL2 is dominated by two main variants worldwide, c.509-1G>C and c.622C>T (p.Arg208Ter), found in 69% to 89% of patients, often in compound heterozygosity (inheriting one copy of a different variant from each parent). Although most variants in TPP1 cause the classic late infantile form, certain missense mutations such as c.380G>A (p.Arg127Gln) and c.1340G>A (p.Arg447His) are associated with a later onset and a more prolonged course of the disease.

Furthermore, a founder effect is described in this disease, perfectly illustrated by the variant c.851G>T (p.Gly284Val). This mutation is responsible for 55% of cases in families from Canada and Newfoundland but is practically absent worldwide, suggesting an origin in a common founder ancestor from that region.
591.technical_citations:
Fietz M, AlSayed M, Burke D, et al. Diagnosis of neuronal ceroid lipofuscinosis type 2 (CLN2 disease): Expert recommendations for early detection and laboratory diagnosis. Mol Genet Metab. 2016 Sep.119(1-2):160-7.

  Malik K, Santucci K, Sremba L, et al. Neuronal Ceroid Lipofuscinoses Overview. 2001 Oct 10 [updated 2025 May 29]. In: Adam MP, Feldman J, Mirzaa GM, Pagon RA, Wallace SE, Amemiya A, editors. GeneReviews® [Internet]. Seattle (WA): University of Washington, Seattle. 1993–2025.

  Schulz A, Specchio N, de Los Reyes E, et al. Safety and efficacy of cerliponase alfa in children with neuronal ceroid lipofuscinosis type 2 (CLN2 disease): an open-label extension study. Lancet Neurol. 2024 Jan.23(1):60-70.

592.name: Neuronal Ceroid Lipofuscinosis Type 8 (CLN8 gene)
592.description: Neuronal ceroid lipofuscinosis type 8 (NCL8) is a rare, hereditary neurodegenerative disorder, caused by mutations in the CLN8 gene, which lead to the accumulation of toxic material in cells. This pathology primarily affects the nervous system, resulting in progressive epilepsy, cognitive decline, and motor difficulties.
592.overview:
The CLN8 gene expresses a protein essential for cellular transport between the endoplasmic reticulum and the Golgi apparatus. Pathogenic variants (mutations) in the CLN8 gene alter this process, causing the toxic accumulation of waste products (called lipopigments) within the lysosomes of cells, especially in neurons. This progressive accumulation leads to damage and death of nerve cells, driving the neurodegeneration observed in neuronal ceroid lipofuscinosis type 8.

NCL8 is an extremely rare disease worldwide. However, its incidence is significantly higher in certain populations due to founder effects. The best-known examples are found in Finland, where it presents as a milder form known as northern epilepsy, and in some families in Turkey, where a more severe variant of the disease is observed. It is noteworthy that in recent years new cases of NCL8 have been described, expanding the ethnic diversity of the disease.
592.symptoms: The disease presents with two main clinical forms. A late and severe classic childhood form, which generally begins between 2 and 7 years of age, characterized by vision loss, ataxia, developmental regression, and difficult-to-control epileptic seizures. A milder form, Nordic epilepsy, has its onset between 5 and 10 years of age, with prominent generalized seizures and a slower and more gradual cognitive and motor deterioration over several decades.
592.prevention: There is currently no cure for LCN8. Management is supportive and symptomatic, focusing on improving the patient's quality of life. Antiepileptic drugs are relevant for seizure control, although they may lose efficacy as the disease progresses.
592.technical_description:
Neuronal ceroid lipofuscinosis type 8 (NCL8) is a very rare inherited neurodegenerative disease, caused by the accumulation of a waste material called lipofuscin in nerve cells, leading to their progressive deterioration. The genetic origin of this condition lies in the CLN8 gene, which provides instructions for making an endoplasmic reticulum protein essential for the proper transport of lipids and other molecules. When there are mutations in the CLN8 gene, the protein does not function correctly, disrupting this transport and causing the toxic accumulation that damages the nervous system.

A characteristic aspect of NCL8 is the presence of founder effect variants, which explain the higher frequency of certain mutations in families from specific populations. For example, the c.70C>G (p.Arg24Gly) variant is common in the Finnish population and is associated with a milder form of the disease, known as slow-progressing Northern epilepsy. In contrast, the c.677T>C (p.Leu226Pro) variant is more frequently found in families of Turkish origin and is related to the classical and more severe form of NCL8, characterized by early onset and rapid neurological deterioration. It should be noted that, due to technical limitations, our study did not include the analysis of the Turkish variant c.677T>C.
592.technical_citations:
Alkhars FZ, Bo Ali AY, Almohanna MA, et al. Neuronal ceroid lipofuscinoses type 8: Expanding genotype/phenotype diversity-first report from Saudi Arabia. Neurosciences (Riyadh). 2020 Jan.25(1):65-69.

  Malik K, Santucci K, Sremba L, et al. Neuronal Ceroid Lipofuscinoses Overview. 2001 Oct 10 [updated 2025 May 29]. In: Adam MP, Feldman J, Mirzaa GM, Pagon RA, Wallace SE, Amemiya A, editors. GeneReviews® [Internet]. Seattle (WA): University of Washington, Seattle. 1993–2025.

  Ranta S, Lehesjoki AE. Northern epilepsy, a new member of the NCL family. Neurol Sci. 2000.21(3 Suppl):S43-7.

  Sahin Y, Güngör O, Gormez Z, et al. Exome sequencing identifies a novel homozygous CLN8 mutation in a Turkish family with Northern epilepsy. Acta Neurol Belg. 2017 Mar.117(1):159-167.

593.name: Neuronal Ceroid Lipofuscinosis Type 10 (CTSD gene)
593.description: Neuronal ceroid lipofuscinosis type 10 (NCL10) is a rare, hereditary neurodegenerative disorder, caused by mutations in the CTSD gene that lead to a deficiency of the enzyme cathepsin D. This defect causes a toxic accumulation of waste products in nerve cells, resulting in severe and rapidly progressive brain damage from birth.
593.overview:
Neuronal ceroid lipofuscinosis type 10 (NCL10) is a neurodegenerative disorder caused by mutations in the CTSD gene. This gene encodes cathepsin D, an essential enzyme that operates in lysosomes, responsible for cellular recycling. The primary role of cathepsin D is to break down proteins and other molecules so that their components can be reused. In individuals with NCL10, pathogenic variants in both copies of the CTSD gene result in a severely deficient or completely non-functional cathepsin D enzyme.

This enzymatic failure leads to the toxic accumulation of waste materials, known as ceroid lipofuscins, primarily within neurons of the brain and other tissues. The progressive accumulation of these substances causes widespread neuronal death, leading to severe brain damage. NCL10 is inherited in an autosomal recessive pattern and is an extremely rare condition with a prevalence of less than one case per million individuals.
593.symptoms: LCN10 is one of the most severe forms of LCN, typically presenting as a congenital form with onset at birth or in the early infant period. Affected infants often show symptoms from the first day, including intractable seizures difficult to control with medication, microcephaly (abnormally small head), and severe respiratory distress. Affected infants usually experience premature death, generally within the first weeks or months of life. Other phenotypes with variable onset, between early childhood and adolescence, characterized by movement disorders, seizures, vision loss, cognitive impairment, and speech problems, also exist. In late-onset phenotypes, life expectancy is also shortened.
593.prevention: Currently, there is no cure or therapy that modifies the course of the disease for LCN10. Medical management is entirely palliative and symptomatic, with the goal of maximizing the quality of life of the affected infant. This supportive care mainly includes the use of multiple antiepileptic drugs to control seizures, although they are often refractory to treatment.
593.technical_description:
Neuronal ceroid lipofuscinosis type 10 (NCL10) is a rare inherited neurodegenerative disease that primarily affects the nervous system. The gene responsible for this condition is CTSD, which contains the instructions for making the enzyme cathepsin D. This enzyme is essential for the cellular recycling system, as it is responsible for breaking down and eliminating waste proteins within neurons. A failure in this cleaning process causes a toxic accumulation of waste materials, leading to the death of nerve cells and the development of the disease.

The severity and age of onset of the disease are directly related to the amount of enzymatic function that is preserved. Complete loss of cathepsin D enzyme activity leads to the most severe form, congenital NCL, with a fatal outcome in early childhood. On the other hand, partial loss of CTSD enzyme activity results in NCL10 disease forms with a later onset, either in infancy, youth, or adulthood, and generally slower progression.
593.technical_citations:
CLN10 disease. MedlinePlus. National Library of medicine (NIH) [October 2016].

  Malik K, Santucci K, Sremba L, et al. Neuronal Ceroid Lipofuscinoses Overview. 2001 Oct 10 [updated 2025 May 29]. In: Adam MP, Feldman J, Mirzaa GM, Pagon RA, Wallace SE, Amemiya A, editors. GeneReviews® [Internet]. Seattle (WA): University of Washington, Seattle. 1993–2025.

594.name: Neuronal Ceroid Lipofuscinosis Type 11 (GRN gene)
594.description: Neuronal ceroid lipofuscinosis type 11 (NCL11) is a rare, hereditary neurodegenerative disorder caused by having two non-functional copies of the GRN gene. This genetic defect leads to a complete absence of the progranulin protein, resulting in progressive vision loss, seizures, and cognitive decline that typically begins in late childhood.
594.overview:
Neuronal ceroid lipofuscinosis type 11 (NCL11) is a severe and progressive neurodegenerative disease that belongs to a group of lysosomal storage disorders. It is caused by mutations in the GRN gene, which expresses the progranulin protein. This protein is essential for the proper functioning of lysosomes and for neuronal survival. NCL11 occurs when an individual inherits two mutated copies of the GRN gene, one from each parent, leading to a complete lack of functional progranulin. This inheritance pattern is known as autosomal recessive.

The absence of progranulin impairs lysosomal function, causing waste materials, specifically ceroid lipofuscins, to accumulate within neurons of the brain and retina. This accumulation is toxic and leads to the progressive death of these cells. NCL11 is a rare disease, with only a small number of cases reported worldwide. Importantly, inheriting a single mutated copy of GRN does not cause NCL11, but it is a significant risk factor for an adult-onset condition called frontotemporal dementia (FTD).
594.symptoms: The first signs of LCN11 usually appear from late childhood to the second decade. The disease presents both cases of rapid progressive and slow progressive courses. Symptoms include progressive vision loss that can eventually lead to blindness. This is followed by the development of seizures, cognitive impairment affecting memory and learning, difficulties with coordination and movement (ataxia), and behavioral changes.
594.prevention: Currently, there is no cure for LCN11. Management is supportive and focuses on alleviating symptoms to improve quality of life. Treatment strategies include the use of antiepileptic drugs to control seizures and specialized educational programs to address cognitive impairment. Physical, occupational, and speech therapies are crucial for managing motor and communication difficulties.
594.technical_description:
Neuronal ceroid lipofuscinosis type 11 (NCL11) is a hereditary neurodegenerative disease, severe in nature and with childhood onset. It is classified as a lysosomal storage disease, as the cell's recycling center does not function correctly, leading to a toxic accumulation of cellular waste, especially in neurons. The genetic cause of this pathology lies in the GRN gene, which encodes the progranulin protein. This protein is fundamental for nerve cell survival, inflammation regulation, and, critically, for the proper functioning of lysosomes. The complete absence of functional progranulin disrupts these vital processes, leading to the neurological deterioration characteristic of NCL11.

The disease follows an autosomal recessive inheritance pattern, meaning an individual must inherit two defective copies of the GRN gene, one from each parent, to manifest it. An example of a pathogenic variant causing NCL11 is c.768_769dup (p.Gln257fs) in exon 8 of the GRN gene. This mutation consists of a small duplication in the DNA sequence that causes a frameshift. As a consequence, the cellular machinery creates an incomplete and non-functional protein.
594.technical_citations:
Huin V, Barbier M, Bottani A, et al. Homozygous GRN mutations: new phenotypes and new insights into pathological and molecular mechanisms. Brain. 2020 Jan 1.143(1):303-319.

  Malik K, Santucci K, Sremba L, et al. Neuronal Ceroid Lipofuscinoses Overview. 2001 Oct 10 [updated 2025 May 29]. In: Adam MP, Feldman J, Mirzaa GM, Pagon RA, Wallace SE, Amemiya A, editors. GeneReviews® [Internet]. Seattle (WA): University of Washington, Seattle. 1993–2025.

  Nóbrega PR, Paiva ARB, Amorim Junior AD, et al. Further description of the phenotypic spectrum of neuronal ceroid lipofuscinosis type 11. Genet Med. 2025 Jan.27(1):101291.

595.name: Neuronal Ceroid Lipofuscinosis Type 13 (CTSF gene)
595.description: Neuronal ceroid lipofuscinosis type 13 (NCL13) is a rare adult-onset neurodegenerative disorder caused by pathogenic variants in the CTSF gene. The resulting deficiency of the cathepsin F enzyme leads to a toxic accumulation of waste products in neurons, causing progressive cognitive, psychiatric, and motor impairment.
595.overview:
The CTSF gene provides instructions for making an enzyme called cathepsin F. This enzyme is found in lysosomes, which are specialized compartments within cells that act as recycling centers. Cathepsin F plays a crucial role in breaking down and recycling various proteins, helping to maintain cellular health. When an individual inherits two mutated copies of the CTSF gene, the production of functional cathepsin F is severely reduced or eliminated.

This deficiency disrupts normal lysosomal function, leading to the accumulation of waste materials, specifically lipopigments (lipofuscin), within neurons and other cells. The buildup of these toxic materials causes progressive damage and death of nerve cells, resulting in the neurodegenerative symptoms of LCN13. This condition presents with an adult Kufs disease type B phenotype and is inherited in an autosomal recessive pattern.
595.symptoms: LCN13 is characterized by an onset in adulthood, generally between 20 and 30 years of age. Clinical manifestations include psychomotor deterioration, progressive cognitive decline, dementia, and behavioral alterations. Cases of progressive myoclonic epilepsy and seizures, related to the Kufs type A phenotype, are also described.
595.prevention: Currently, there is no cure or disease-modifying therapy for LCN13. The management strategy is symptomatic and supportive, aiming to maximize the patient's quality of life. Treatment may include anticonvulsant drugs to control seizures, medications to manage psychiatric symptoms and movement disorders, and comprehensive palliative care.
595.technical_description:
Neuronal ceroid lipofuscinosis type 13 (NCL13) is a very rare neurodegenerative disease that manifests in adulthood, usually with symptoms resembling frontotemporal dementia and motor problems. The disease is caused by mutations in the CTSF gene, which encodes the cathepsin F enzyme. This lysosomal protease participates in the degradation and recycling of proteins within lysosomes, considered the cellular recycling centers. When mutations occur in the CTSF gene, enzyme function is compromised, leading to a pathological accumulation of waste material in neurons and, consequently, to progressive degeneration of nervous tissue.

NCL13 is inherited in an autosomal recessive manner, meaning that to suffer from it, it is necessary to inherit two copies of the mutated gene, one copy from each parent. Several pathogenic variants in the CTSF gene have been identified, such as the variant c.962A>G (p.Gln321Arg) in exon 7 or indels like c.993_1004delinsTGCCTACT (p.Lys331fs), which alter the structure and function of the protein.
595.technical_citations:
Ghayal NB, Roemer SF, Tipton PW, et al. Clinical Heterogeneity of Neuronal Ceroid Lipofuscinosis Type 13: A Case Report and Systematic Review of Literature. Neurol Genet. 2024 Dec 23.11(1):e200227.

  Malik K, Santucci K, Sremba L, et al. Neuronal Ceroid Lipofuscinoses Overview. 2001 Oct 10 [updated 2025 May 29]. In: Adam MP, Feldman J, Mirzaa GM, Pagon RA, Wallace SE, Amemiya A, editors. GeneReviews® [Internet]. Seattle (WA): University of Washington, Seattle. 1993–2025.

601.name: Mucolipidosis III Gamma (GNPTG gene)
601.description: Mucolipidosis type III gamma is a rare inherited lysosomal storage disorder caused by pathogenic variants in the GNPTG gene. These genetic changes alter the cell's ability to direct specific enzymes to the lysosome, leading to a harmful accumulation of certain molecules and causing progressive multisystemic problems, primarily affecting skeletal, joint, and connective tissues.
601.overview:
The GNPTG gene provides instructions for making the gamma subunit of an essential enzyme complex called GlcNAc-1-phosphotransferase. This enzyme works by adding a specific tag (mannose-6-phosphate) to newly created enzymes to direct them to lysosomes. Lysosomes are the recycling centers of the cell, responsible for breaking down waste materials. In mucolipidosis III gamma, a defective gamma subunit impairs this tagging process. Consequently, lysosomal enzymes are not delivered correctly and are secreted outside the cell, while waste products such as mucopolysaccharides and glycolipids accumulate within lysosomes, causing cellular damage.

This condition is inherited in an autosomal recessive pattern, meaning an individual must inherit two mutated copies of the GNPTG gene, one from each parent. The exact prevalence of mucolipidosis type III gamma is unknown, given that it is considered an ultra-rare disease.
601.symptoms: The onset of symptoms occurs in early childhood, initially manifesting as progressive stiffness in the finger joints, which can begin at 18 months of age. This condition progresses slowly, affecting other joints such as the shoulders and hips, resulting in a "claw hand" deformity, short stature, and carpal tunnel syndrome. Chronic pain, especially in the lower extremities, is common and significantly affects quality of life. While mild thickening of facial features and mild cardiac involvement may develop, intellectual development is normal in the vast majority of individuals.
601.prevention: Currently, there is no cure for mucolipidosis type III gamma; therefore, management focuses on alleviating symptoms and improving quality of life. A multidisciplinary approach including physical and occupational therapy is essential to maximize mobility and joint function. Orthopedic surgery may be necessary to address skeletal complications such as carpal tunnel syndrome or hip dysplasia. Regular follow-up with a cardiologist is also recommended, as heart valve problems can develop over time.
601.technical_description:
Mucolipidosis type III gamma is a hereditary disorder caused by mutations in the GNPTG gene. This gene expresses a key enzyme that tags other proteins for delivery to the lysosome, the cell's recycling center. When the gene fails, this tagging process is interrupted, leading to an accumulation of waste in cells that primarily damages bones and joints. To develop the disease, it is necessary to inherit one copy of the mutated gene from each parent (autosomal recessive inheritance).

Regarding genetic variants, although no strict correlation between disease severity and the type of pathogenic variant in GNPTG has been described to date, it has been observed that variants predicting a complete loss of protein function are associated with a severe phenotype. This is the case for recurrent variants such as c.499dup (p.Leu167Profs). In addition to these, there are other variants of interest described in different studies and populations, such as c.196C>T, which has been linked to a milder phenotype, and c.316G>A (p.Gly106Ser), which consists of a substitution within a conserved domain.
601.technical_citations:
Raas-Rothschild A, Spiegel R. Mucolipidosis III Gamma. 2010 Jan 28 [updated 2019 Nov 21]. In: Adam MP, Feldman J, Mirzaa GM, Pagon RA, Wallace SE, Amemiya A, editors. GeneReviews® [Internet]. Seattle (WA): University of Washington, Seattle. 1993–2025.

  van Meel E, Kornfeld S. Mucolipidosis III GNPTG Missense Mutations Cause Misfolding of the γ Subunit of GlcNAc-1-Phosphotransferase. Hum Mutat. 2016 Jul.37(7):623-6.

  Alegra T, Sperb-Ludwig F, Guarany NR, et al. Clinical Characterization of Mucolipidoses II and III: A Multicenter Study. J Pediatr Genet. 2019 Dec.8(4):198-204.

609.name: Multiple Endocrine Neoplasia Type 1
609.description: Multiple endocrine neoplasia type 1 (MEN1) is a hereditary disorder caused by mutations in the tumor suppressor gene MEN1, which leads to the development of tumors, mainly in the endocrine glands. It is an autosomal dominant condition that requires continuous surveillance to manage tumor growth.
609.overview:
Multiple endocrine neoplasia type 1 (MEN1) is a hereditary condition that predisposes individuals to develop tumors in various endocrine glands. It is caused by pathogenic variants (mutations) in the MEN1 gene, which functions as a tumor suppressor gene. This gene encodes the protein menin, which acts as a tumor suppressor by controlling cell division and proliferation, preventing uncontrolled cell growth. When a mutation occurs in the MEN1 gene, this control is lost, significantly increasing the risk of developing tumors. The primarily affected glands are the parathyroid, anterior pituitary, and pancreas, often referred to as the "3 Ps".

The condition has an estimated prevalence of 3 to 20 cases per 100,000 individuals worldwide. Although most individuals with MEN1 inherit a mutated gene from a parent, approximately 10% of cases occur spontaneously (de novo mutation) without a family history. Lifelong monitoring is essential for individuals with a mutation in MEN1 to detect tumors at an early and treatable stage.
609.symptoms: The clinical signs of MEN1 vary notably, even among family members, and usually manifest between the ages of 20 and 40. The most common and earliest manifestation is hyperparathyroidism, which causes elevated blood calcium levels, potentially leading to kidney stones and bone fragility. Tumors in the pituitary gland, such as prolactinoma, and gastroenteropancreatic neuroendocrine tumors are also characteristic. Less frequently, carcinoid tumors and tumors in the adrenal glands may develop.
609.prevention: The management of MEN1 focuses on proactive surveillance and symptomatic treatment. Periodic biochemical tests and imaging studies (e.g., MRI, CT scans) are crucial for early tumor detection. The primary treatment for most MEN1-associated tumors is surgical removal. Medications are also used to control the effects of hormone overproduction, such as somatostatin analogues.
609.technical_description:
Multiple endocrine neoplasia type 1 (MEN1) is an autosomal dominant genetic syndrome that predisposes to the development of tumors in various endocrine glands, including the parathyroid, pituitary, and pancreas. This condition is caused by pathogenic variants in the MEN1 gene, which encodes the menin protein, a key tumor suppressor in the regulation of cell growth. When a mutation prevents menin from functioning correctly, cell growth control is lost and the risk of tumor formation increases.

This condition is inherited in 90% of cases; the remaining 10% is due to de novo mutations. More than 1,200 different mutations have been identified distributed throughout this gene, with most being variants that result in an incomplete and non-functional protein, such as frameshift, nonsense, and deletion mutations. Although there is generally no clear correlation between the specific mutation and the type of tumors the patient will develop, an exception has been reported with the variant c.1378C>T (p.Arg460Ter), known as the "Burin variant." Individuals carrying this specific mutation show a characteristic clinical pattern, with a higher prevalence of prolactinomas than average and a lower prevalence of gastrinomas compared to other patients with MEN1.
609.technical_citations:
Giusti F, Marini F, Brandi ML. Multiple Endocrine Neoplasia Type 1. 2005 Aug 31 [updated 2022 Mar 10]. In: Adam MP, Feldman J, Mirzaa GM, Pagon RA, Wallace SE, Amemiya A, editors. GeneReviews® [Internet]. Seattle (WA): University of Washington, Seattle. 1993–2025.

  Kamilaris CDC, Stratakis CA. Multiple Endocrine Neoplasia Type 1 (MEN1): An Update and the Significance of Early Genetic and Clinical Diagnosis. Front Endocrinol (Lausanne). 2019 Jun 11.10:339.

  Manoharan J, Albers MB, Rinke A, et al. Multiple Endocrine Neoplasia Type 1. Dtsch Arztebl Int. 2024 Aug 9.121(16):527-533.

 Multiple Endocrine Neoplasia Type 1 National Institute of Diabetes and Digestive and Kidney Diseases [July 2020].

610.name: Multiple Endocrine Neoplasia Type 4
610.description: Multiple endocrine neoplasia type 4 (MEN4) is an extremely rare hereditary cancer syndrome caused by pathogenic variants in the tumor suppressor gene CDKN1B, leading to an increased risk of developing tumors in various endocrine glands. Its clinical presentation is very similar to that of the more common MEN1 syndrome.
610.overview:
Multiple endocrine neoplasia type 4 (MEN4) is a rare inherited disorder that predisposes individuals to developing tumors in their endocrine glands. The condition is caused by pathogenic variants (mutations) in the CDKN1B gene. This gene provides instructions for making a protein called p27Kip1 (commonly known as p27), which acts as a tumor suppressor. The p27 protein plays a crucial role in regulating the cell cycle, preventing cells from growing and dividing too quickly or in an uncontrolled manner.

When a mutation occurs in one copy of the CDKN1B gene, the production or function of the p27 protein is affected. This loss of control over cell division significantly increases the risk of tumor formation in hormone-producing glands. MEN4 is inherited in an autosomal dominant pattern, meaning that only one mutated copy of the gene is needed to increase risk. Due to its extreme rarity, with only a small number of families reported worldwide, the exact incidence is unknown.
610.symptoms: The clinical features of NEM4 largely overlap with those of NEM1 syndrome, although they differ in clinical severity and age of symptom onset. The most common manifestations are parathyroid gland tumors (leading to primary hyperparathyroidism), pituitary adenomas, and neuroendocrine tumors of the gastro-entero-pancreatic tract. Less commonly, individuals may develop other tumors such as adrenal neoplasms, papillary thyroid carcinoma, and renal cell carcinoma. The age of onset is variable, but generally occurs in adulthood. The mean age of onset of the first endocrine tumor is estimated to be 43.5 years.
610.prevention: The management of NEM4 focuses on proactive surveillance for the early detection and treatment of associated tumors. This involves lifelong regular follow-up, including biochemical testing (e.g., blood tests for calcium, parathyroid hormone, and prolactin) and periodic imaging studies (e.g., MRI or CT scans). Treatment is not directed at the genetic variant itself but is tailored to the specific type, size, and location of the tumor found, more commonly involving surgical removal, medication, or, occasionally, radiation therapy.
610.technical_description:
Multiple endocrine neoplasia type 4 (MEN4) is a rare hereditary disease that predisposes to the development of tumors, mainly in endocrine glands such as the parathyroid and pituitary glands. This pathology is caused by pathogenic variants in the CDKN1B gene, which acts as a tumor suppressor. The CDKN1B gene encodes the p27 protein, an inhibitor of cyclin-dependent kinases. Its main function is to regulate the cell cycle, stopping the progression from phase G1 to phase S, preventing uncontrolled cell division and tumor formation. Mutations in CDKN1B that alter the function of p27 increase the risk of uncontrolled cell proliferation.

Pathogenic variants have been identified in non-coding regions, such as in the 5' UTR region. Furthermore, it has been observed that individuals with "indel" (insertions/deletions) variants have a higher incidence of primary hyperparathyroidism. Likewise, those with variants in codons 94-96 have an increased risk of developing tumors in the parathyroid and pituitary glands. It is important to note that the penetrance of the disease is low and increases with age, meaning that not all individuals carrying variants will develop symptoms.
610.technical_citations:
Ahmed FW, Majeed MS, Kirresh O. Multiple Endocrine Neoplasias Type 4. 2023 Oct 18. In: StatPearls [Internet]. Treasure Island (FL): StatPearls Publishing. 2025 Jan–.

  Alrezk R, Hannah-Shmouni F, Stratakis CA. MEN4 and CDKN1B mutations: the latest of the MEN syndromes. Endocr Relat Cancer. 2017 Oct.24(10):T195-T208.

  Brock P, Kirschner L. Multiple Endocrine Neoplasia Type 4. 2023 Sep 21. In: Adam MP, Feldman J, Mirzaa GM, Pagon RA, Wallace SE, Amemiya A, editors. GeneReviews® [Internet]. Seattle (WA): University of Washington, Seattle. 1993–2025.
