611.name: Neurofibromatosis Type 2
611.description: Neurofibromatosis type 2 (NF2) is a genetic disorder caused by mutations in the tumor suppressor gene NF2, which leads to the growth of noncancerous tumors in the nervous system. The condition is characterized by bilateral vestibular schwannomas, causing progressive hearing loss and balance problems.
611.overview:
The NF2 gene, located on chromosome 22, provides instructions for making a protein called merlin (or schwannomin). Merlin acts as a tumor suppressor, playing a critical role in controlling cell growth, division, and movement. Pathogenic variants in the NF2 gene disrupt the production or function of merlin, leading to the uncontrolled growth of certain nervous system cells, particularly Schwann cells, which form insulating layers around nerves. This results in the development of multiple tumors.

Neurofibromatosis type 2 is a rare condition with an estimated incidence of 1 in 25,000 to 40,000 individuals worldwide. Approximately half of the cases are inherited from a parent in an autosomal dominant pattern, while the other half are due to new, spontaneous (de novo) mutations in the NF2 gene in individuals with no family history of the disorder.
611.symptoms: The hallmark of NF2 is the development of bilateral vestibular schwannomas, tumors on the nerves responsible for hearing and balance. Symptoms typically appear in late adolescence or early twenties and include progressive hearing loss, tinnitus (ringing in the ears), and balance problems. Other nervous system tumors such as meningiomas, gliomas, and ependymomas, as well as cataracts and skin tumors, may also occur.
611.prevention: NF2 management requires a multidisciplinary team and focuses on regular MRI surveillance to monitor tumor growth. Treatment options are tailored to the individual and may include surgery to remove tumors or stereotactic radiosurgery to halt their growth. For hearing loss, auditory brainstem implants may be an option. The drug bevacizumab has shown efficacy in reducing tumors and improving hearing in some patients.
611.technical_description:
Neurofibromatosis type 2 (NF2) is a genetic condition that predisposes to the development of tumors in the nervous system, the most characteristic being bilateral vestibular schwannoma, which affects the nerves of hearing and balance. This disease is caused by variants in the NF2 gene, which contains the instructions for making the merlin protein, a tumor suppressor that regulates cell growth. When this gene does not function correctly, cells can multiply uncontrollably, leading to tumors.

There is a correlation between the type of genetic variant (genotype) and the severity of the disease (phenotype). Truncating variants, such as nonsense and frameshift types, are associated with a severe phenotype, which implies an earlier onset and a greater number of tumors. Conversely, missense variants and large deletions usually cause a mild phenotype. Splice site variants are more variable and can be associated with both mild and severe forms, often depending on their location in the gene. In general, the position of the variant is also important, as those in the second half of the gene (3' end) are associated with a lower risk of developing meningiomas.
611.technical_citations:
Ardern-Holmes S, Fisher G, North K. Neurofibromatosis Type 2. J Child Neurol. 2017 Jan.32(1):9-22.

  Halliday D, Parry A, Evans DG. Neurofibromatosis type 2 and related disorders. Curr Opin Oncol. 2019 Nov.31(6):562-567.

  Neurofibromatosis Type 2. St. Jude Children’s Research Hospital.

  Screnci M, Puechmaille M, Berton Q, et al. Bevacizumab for Vestibular Schwannomas in Neurofibromatosis Type 2: A Systematic Review of Tumor Control and Hearing Preservation. J Clin Med. 2024 Dec 9.13(23):7488.

  Tiwari R, Singh AK. Neurofibromatosis Type 2. 2022 Oct 3. In: StatPearls [Internet]. Treasure Island (FL): StatPearls Publishing. 2025 Jan–.

616.name: Polymerase Proofreading–Associated Polyposis (POLD1 and POLE genes)
616.description: Polymerase Proofreading-Associated Polyposis (PPAP) is an inherited cancer predisposition syndrome caused by mutations in the POLE or POLD1 genes. These genes are crucial for error correction during DNA replication, and their failure leads to a massive accumulation of mutations, drastically increasing the risk of colorectal polyps and various types of cancer.
616.overview:
The POLE and POLD1 genes encode key subunits of DNA polymerases, the enzymes responsible for duplicating our genetic material each time a cell divides. An essential function of these polymerases is "proofreading," a self-correction mechanism that identifies and repairs copying errors in real-time. Germline (inherited) mutations in specific domains of these genes override this correction capability. As a result, cells accumulate mutations at a rate up to 100 times higher than normal (an "ultramutation" phenotype), which accelerates the transformation of healthy cells into cancerous ones.

PPAP is a rare condition, and its exact incidence is not yet well-established, as it was first described in 2013. It is estimated that it could account for a small fraction of cases of familial polyposis and colorectal cancer that are not due to more common genes such as APC or the Lynch syndrome genes. Inheritance is autosomal dominant, meaning a single mutated copy of the gene is sufficient to increase risk. It affects all populations equally.
616.symptoms: The clinical presentation is variable. Carriers of mutations in POLE or POLD1 typically develop between 10 and 100 adenomatous polyps in the colon and rectum (attenuated polyposis) during adulthood, although some may have very few or no polyps. The main symptom is an elevated risk of developing colorectal cancer at a relatively early age (often before 50 years of age). In addition, there is an increased risk of other tumors, such as endometrial, ovarian, brain (glioma), and duodenal cancer, configuring a broad spectrum of tumor predisposition.
616.prevention: Management is based on intensive surveillance for early detection and prevention of cancer. High-quality colonoscopies are recommended from 25-30 years of age, or earlier if there is a family history, repeating every 1-2 years depending on the findings. Surveillance for endometrial cancer in women (from 30-35 years of age) and consideration of screening for other associated tumors are also advised. There is no specific pharmacological treatment, so the main strategy is endoscopic polypectomy and, in cases of severe polyposis or cancer, surgery (colectomy).
616.technical_description:
PPAP associated with the POLE and POLD1 genes follows an autosomal dominant inheritance pattern.

The POLE gene encodes for the epsilon subunit of DNA polymerase. Individuals carrying pathogenic variants in this gene have a 40-60% risk of developing colorectal cancer, and a significantly elevated risk of endometrial cancer in women. Mutations in this gene are almost exclusively of the missense type and are usually clustered in the exonuclease domain. The most common and studied variant is c.1270C>G (p.Leu424Val, rs483352909) which causes the production of a non-functional protein.

The POLD1 gene encodes for the delta subunit of DNA polymerase. The presence of pathogenic variants, especially in the exonuclease domain, significantly increases the risk of developing colorectal cancer, endometrial cancer, and possibly brain and breast tumors. The variant c.1433G>A (p.Ser478Asn, rs397514632) is the most frequent and responsible for the majority of cases. It is the one that is preferentially sought in genetic diagnosis.
616.technical_citations:
Palles C, Martin L, Domingo E, et al. The clinical features of polymerase proof-reading associated polyposis (PPAP) and recommendations for patient management. Fam Cancer. 2022 Apr.21(2):197-209.

  Church JM. Polymerase proofreading-associated polyposis: a new, dominantly inherited syndrome of hereditary colorectal cancer predisposition. Dis Colon Rectum. 2014 Mar.57(3):396-7.

  Polymerase proofreading-related polyposis. Orphanet. ORPHA447877 [June 2024].

618.name: Variegate Porphyria (PPOX gene)
618.description: Porphyria variegata is an inherited metabolic disorder caused by mutations in the PPOX gene, which encodes a key enzyme in the production of heme. This enzymatic deficiency leads to the accumulation of precursor molecules called porphyrins, resulting in a unique combination of acute neurological symptoms and photosensitivity skin lesions.
618.overview:
The PPOX gene provides instructions for making the enzyme protoporphyrinogen oxidase, the seventh of eight steps in the heme synthesis pathway, an essential component of hemoglobin and other vital hemoproteins. A mutation in one of the two copies of the PPOX gene reduces the activity of this enzyme to approximately 50%, leading to the accumulation of protoporphyrinogen IX and other precursors in the body. This accumulation is directly responsible for the pathology of the disease, which manifests with a characteristic mixed clinical presentation.

Variegate porphyria is one of the acute porphyrias and has an estimated global prevalence of 1 in 100,000 people, although its distribution is very uneven. The disease is particularly common in South Africa, especially among the white population of Dutch descent (Afrikaners), where the prevalence reaches 1 in 300 individuals due to a specific founder mutation (R59W) inherited from a 17th-century settler. It affects men and women equally, although women tend to present with acute symptoms more frequently due to hormonal factors.
618.symptoms: The disease is characterized by a dual presentation. Skin symptoms consist of extreme fragility of the skin in sun-exposed areas (hands and face), with blister formation, erosions, and subsequent scarring with hyperpigmentation and hypertrichosis (excess hair). On the other hand, patients may suffer acute neurovisceral attacks, often triggered by drugs, alcohol, fasting, or hormonal changes. These attacks are characterized by intense abdominal pain, nausea, vomiting, tachycardia, hypertension, and neurological symptoms such as muscle weakness, anxiety, confusion, and seizures. The onset of symptoms usually occurs after puberty.
618.prevention: Management focuses on two aspects: the prevention and treatment of acute attacks. Prevention involves strictly avoiding known trigger factors, especially a long list of porphyrinogenic drugs. Skin lesions are managed with rigorous photoprotection and wound care. Acute attacks constitute a medical emergency and are treated with the intravenous administration of human hemin, which acts by replenishing the heme deficit and slowing the overproduction of toxic porphyrins, along with a high carbohydrate intake.
618.technical_description:
Porphyria variegata is a disease with an autosomal dominant inheritance pattern, although, like other porphyrias, it has incomplete penetrance. It is estimated that between 40-50% of carriers will develop symptoms during their lifetime, although this is variable as it depends on exposure to triggering factors.

The most frequent and studied variant worldwide is p.Arg59Trp (c.175C>T, rs121918324). It is a missense type mutation, responsible for the high prevalence of porphyria variegata in the South African population of European origin (Afrikaner). This is a case of founder effect and, today, this variant accounts for more than 95% of PV cases in South Africa.

Outside of South Africa, PV presents greater allelic heterogeneity. The definitive confirmation of the diagnosis and the identification of asymptomatic carriers is carried out by sequencing the PPOX gene. This allows the identification of the responsible pathogenic variant, which is crucial for genetic counseling and for warning at-risk family members on how to avoid triggers for acute attacks.
618.technical_citations:
Singal AK, Anderson KE. Variegate Porphyria. 2013 Feb 14 [Updated 2019 Dec 12]. In: Adam MP, Feldman J, Mirzaa GM, et al., editors. GeneReviews® [Internet].

  Variegate Porphyria. American Porphyria Foundation (APF).

  Variegate porphyria. Orphanet. ORPHA79473 [June 2014].

619.name: Hereditary Coproporphyria (CPOX gene)
619.description: Hereditary coproporphyria is a rare metabolic disorder, with autosomal dominant inheritance, caused by variants in the CPOX gene that lead to a deficiency in the enzyme coproporphyrinogen oxidase. Although many carriers of the genetic variant never develop symptoms, the disease can manifest as acute neurovisceral attacks triggered by certain factors.
619.overview:
Hereditary coproporphyria (HCP) is one of the acute hepatic porphyrias, a group of metabolic diseases caused by defects in the heme synthesis pathway. It is inherited in an autosomal dominant manner due to variants in the CPOX gene, which encodes the enzyme coproporphyrinogen oxidase. A deficiency in this enzyme leads to the accumulation of porphyrin precursors, mainly delta-aminolevulinic acid (ALA) and porphobilinogen (PBG), which are neurotoxic.

A key feature of HCP is its incomplete penetrance, meaning that most individuals who inherit the genetic variant (~80%) remain asymptomatic throughout their lives. Symptoms only appear when the heme pathway is overloaded by specific triggers such as certain drugs, alcohol, or hormonal changes. The disease affects both sexes equally, although women may be at higher risk for attacks due to hormonal fluctuations. A prevalence of approximately 1 in 100,000 individuals in Europe is estimated.
619.symptoms: CPH can manifest with two types of presentations. The most severe are acute neurovisceral attacks, which usually appear after puberty. These episodes are characterized by intense and diffuse abdominal pain, nausea lasting at least 48 hours, and hyponatremia (low blood sodium levels), constipation, and diarrhea. Neurologically and psychiatrically, they can cause anxiety, confusion, hallucinations, muscle weakness that can progress to paralysis, and, in severe cases, seizures. A subgroup of patients (~30%) may also develop skin symptoms, with blisters, fragility, and erosions on sun-exposed skin.
619.prevention:
The management of AIP focuses on the treatment of the acute attack and prevention. An acute attack is a medical emergency that requires immediate hospitalization. The treatment of choice is the administration of intravenous hemin (or heme arginate), which acts by negative feedback to slow the production of toxic precursors. It is accompanied by supportive management with safe analgesics, hydration, and a high carbohydrate intake. Individuals with more than four attacks per year are at higher risk of developing chronic neurological manifestations. Givosiran, an interfering RNA (iRNA) that acts directly against ALAS1, is effective in preventing acute attacks in these individuals and was approved in the United States in 2019.

Prevention is fundamental and consists of the strict avoidance of all known triggering factors, including oral contraceptives and progestogens in women, and certain medications, such as barbiturates and phenytoin, alcohol, fasting, and low-calorie diets.
619.technical_description:
The CPOX gene (coproporphyrinogen oxidase) is composed of seven exons in which pathogenic variants have been identified in people with CPH. These include nonsense and amino acid-changing variants, small deletions, insertions, indels, splice variants, and large deletions.

The c.1210A>G (p.Lys404Glu, K404E, rs121917868) variant, when present in homozygosis, produces a neonatal hemolytic anemia known as “harderoporphyria” in which a distinct intermediate metabolite is produced than that produced in CPH and which can cause jaundice, anemia, photosensitivity, and, if levels are very high, porphyric attacks. This mutation is part of the catalytic site of the CPOX protein, specifically in a highly conserved region of the protein.
619.technical_citations:
Wang B, Bissell DM. Hereditary Coproporphyria. 2012 Dec 13 [Updated 2022 May 19]. In: Adam MP, Feldman J, Mirzaa GM, et al., editors. GeneReviews® [Internet]. Seattle (WA): University of Washington, Seattle. 1993-2025.

 Wang B, Bonkovsky HL, Lim JK, Balwani M. AGA Clinical Practice Update on Diagnosis and Management of Acute Hepatic Porphyrias: Expert Review. Gastroenterology. 2023 Mar.164(3):484-491.

 Wagner MEU, Frost M, Frystyk J. Acute hepatic porphyria in Denmark. a retrospective study. Orphanet J Rare Dis. 2025 Feb 28.20(1):93.

 Whatley SD, Mason NG, Woolf JR, et al. Diagnostic strategies for autosomal dominant acute porphyrias: retrospective analysis of 467 unrelated patients referred for mutational analysis of the HMBS, CPOX, or PPOX gene. Clin Chem. 2009 Jul.55(7):1406-14.

620.name: Familial Porphyria Cutanea Tarda (UROD gene)
620.description: Familial Cutaneous Porphyria Tarda (PCT) is a metabolic disorder caused by a hereditary deficiency in the enzyme Uroporphyrinogen Decarboxylase (UROD), encoded by the UROD gene. This deficiency, combined with other susceptibility factors such as elevated iron, leads to the accumulation of porphyrins in the skin, causing chronic cutaneous photosensitivity with blistering and fragility.
620.overview:
The UROD gene provides instructions for making the enzyme uroporphyrinogen decarboxylase, the fifth step in the heme synthesis pathway. In familial PCT (also known as Type II), an inherited mutation in one of the two copies of the UROD gene reduces the activity of this enzyme by approximately 50% in all body cells. However, this genetic deficiency alone is usually not sufficient to cause disease. Clinical manifestation requires the presence of acquired susceptibility factors—primarily hepatic iron overload—that further inhibit the already deficient enzyme activity in the liver, leading to massive overproduction and accumulation of uroporphyrins.

PCT is the most common porphyria worldwide, with an estimated prevalence of 1 in 10,000 people. The familial form accounts for about 20% of all PCT cases. The disease affects all populations, but its clinical expression depends heavily on the presence of triggering factors such as alcohol consumption, hepatitis C infections, estrogens, and hereditary hemochromatosis.
620.symptoms: The symptoms are exclusively cutaneous and usually appear in adulthood, typically between 40 and 60 years of age. The main characteristic is chronic photosensitivity in sun-exposed areas (back of hands, forearms, face). This manifests with extremely fragile skin, blister formation (vesicles and bullae) with minimal trauma, slowly healing erosions, and development of milia cysts. Over time, pigmentary changes (hyperpigmentation) and hypertrichosis (excessive hair growth, especially on the temples) may appear.
620.prevention: The goal of treatment is to reduce hepatic iron overload and decrease porphyrin levels. First-line treatment consists of periodic phlebotomies (controlled blood draws) to remove excess iron from the body. An alternative, especially in patients with anemia or intolerance to phlebotomy, is low-dose hydroxychloroquine or chloroquine, which help mobilize and excrete excess porphyrins. It is also essential to avoid triggering factors such as alcohol and treat underlying infections.
620.technical_description:
Familial PCT follows an autosomal dominant inheritance pattern, although it presents with low clinical penetrance. It is estimated that only 10-20% of carriers of a mutation in the UROD gene will develop the disease during their lifetime.

Familial PCT is characterized by great allelic heterogeneity, with mutations of different types having been described. Among them are missense variants, such as p.Pro62Leu, nonsense variants, such as p.Gln116Ter, variants affecting the splicing site, such as c.21-1G>C, and small deletions or insertions, such as p.Ala254fs. 

The majority of PCT cases are triggered by external factors in carriers. Therefore, confirmation of the presence of a pathogenic variant is fundamental for genetic counseling and for identifying carrier relatives who could benefit from avoiding these triggering factors.
620.technical_citations:
Rudnick S, Phillips J, Bonkovsky H. Familial Porphyria Cutanea Tarda. 2013 Jun 6 [Updated 2022 Jun 9]. In: Adam MP, Feldman J, Mirzaa GM, et al., editors. GeneReviews® [Internet].

  Porphyria Cutanea Tarda. American Porphyria Foundation (APF).

  Porphyria cutanea tarda. Orphanet. ORPHA101330 [March 2024].

624.name: Retinoblastoma
624.description: Retinoblastoma is a childhood eye cancer caused by mutations in the RB1 gene, a tumor suppressor crucial for cell cycle control. The loss of function of both copies of this gene in developing retinal cells triggers uncontrolled cell growth, leading to the tumor.
624.overview:
The RB1 gene (Retinoblastoma 1) was the first tumor suppressor gene discovered, and its function is crucial for regulating cell growth and division. The protein it encodes, pRB, acts as a "brake" on the cell cycle, preventing cells from dividing uncontrollably. Retinoblastoma arises when both copies of the RB1 gene are inactivated in an immature retinal cell (retinoblast), following Knudson's "two-hit hypothesis." This can occur hereditarily (a mutated allele is inherited, and the other is lost in the retina) or sporadically (both mutations occur de novo in the same retinal cell).

Retinoblastoma is the most common malignant intraocular tumor in childhood, with an incidence of approximately 1 in 15,000 to 20,000 live births. It affects children of all ethnicities and around the world equally, with no sex predilection. Approximately 40% of cases are hereditary, often affecting both eyes (bilateral), while 60% are sporadic and typically unilateral.
624.symptoms: The disease manifests almost exclusively in young children, generally before 5 years of age, with a peak diagnosis around 1-2 years. The most common and distinctive clinical sign is leukocoria, a white pupillary reflex or "cat's eye" that is often observed in flash photographs. Other symptoms include strabismus (eye deviation), eye pain, redness, poor vision, or enlargement of the eyeball.
624.prevention: The goal of treatment is to eradicate the tumor, preserve as much vision as possible, and save the child's life. Management is multidisciplinary and depends on the size, location, and whether it is unilateral or bilateral. Options include chemotherapy (systemic, intra-arterial, or intravitreal) to reduce tumor size, followed by focal therapies such as cryotherapy or laser thermotherapy. In cases or those with a risk of dissemination, enucleation (eye removal) or radiotherapy may be necessary.
624.technical_description:
40% of retinoblastoma cases are hereditary. It is inherited in an autosomal dominant pattern and, in most cases, affected individuals inherit a defective copy of the RB1 gene from one of their parents, and the second copy undergoes spontaneous mutation in the retinal cells, causing the disease.

To date, more than 1000 different variants have been described. The most frequent are nonsense mutations, which introduce a stop signal, leading to an incomplete protein. There are also small insertions or deletions, splicing mutations, or large deletions.
624.technical_citations:
Lohmann DR, Gallie BL. Retinoblastoma. 2000 Jul 18 [Updated 2023 Sep 21]. In: Adam MP, Feldman J, Mirzaa GM, et al., editors. GeneReviews® [Internet].

  Byroju VV, Nadukkandy AS, Cordani M, Kumar LD. Retinoblastoma: present scenario and future challenges. Cell Commun Signal. 2023 Sep 4.21(1):226.

  Retinoblastoma. Orphanet. ORPHA790 [June 2014].

625.name: Autosomal Dominant Retinitis Pigmentosa
625.description: Retinitis pigmentosa is a hereditary eye disease that causes progressive vision loss due to pathogenic variants in genes crucial for retinal function, such as RHO, SNRNP200, and SAG.
625.overview:
Autosomal Dominant Retinitis Pigmentosa is a group of genetic disorders that cause progressive death of the photoreceptor cells of the retina (rods and cones). Being an "autosomal dominant" condition, a single copy of the mutated gene, inherited from one of the parents, is sufficient to cause the disease. It affects approximately 1 in 4,000 people worldwide, without a specific predilection for ethnicity or geography, and accounts for between 20-25% of all retinitis pigmentosa cases.

The RHO, SNRNP200, and SAG genes are fundamental for vision. The RHO gene, the most common cause of ADRP (responsible for ~25% of cases), produces rhodopsin, a protein essential for light absorption and vision in low-light conditions. Mutations in SNRNP200 alter the RNA splicing process, a vital cellular mechanism for building proteins correctly. For its part, the SAG gene produces arrestin, a protein that works in conjunction with rhodopsin to regulate the response to light. Alteration in any of these genes disrupts the function and survival of photoreceptors, leading to their gradual degeneration.
625.symptoms: The first symptoms usually manifest in childhood or adolescence, beginning with a marked difficulty in seeing in the dark or in low-light environments (nyctalopia). As the disease progresses, there is a gradual loss of peripheral vision, resulting in a "tunnel" visual field. Some individuals may also experience flashes of light (photopsia) and increased sensitivity to bright light (photophobia), culminating in legal blindness in adulthood.
625.prevention: Currently, there is no definitive cure for most forms of Retinitis Pigmentosa. Management focuses on slowing the progression of the disease, treating complications, and helping the patient adapt to vision loss. This includes the use of UV-filtered sunglasses to protect the retina from light damage and regular ophthalmological monitoring. In some cases, supplementation with vitamin A palmitate, under strict medical supervision, may help slow degeneration. Emerging therapies such as gene therapy are a very active and promising area of research for the future.
625.technical_description:
Retinitis pigmentosa (RP) refers to a group of hereditary disorders in which abnormalities of the photoreceptors (cones and rods) of the retina cause progressive vision loss. 

From a genetic point of view, the cause is very heterogeneous, with autosomal (recessive and dominant), X-linked, and unknown causes. Cases associated with genes with autosomal dominant inheritance account for up to 30% of cases of non-syndromic retinitis pigmentosa. The RHO, SNRNP200, and SAG genes are involved in very different cellular processes, but are equally vital for the survival of the retina's photoreceptors (rods and cones). 

The most frequent gene associated with the dominant form is RHO, which encodes rhodopsin. The most common variant in this gene, especially in people of European origin, is p.Pro23His (rs104893768) which causes misfolding of the protein. The p.Pro347Leu (rs29001566) variant is also frequent among those affected. 

The SNRNP200 gene encodes an essential protein of the spliceosome. Mutations in this gene affect this process, to which the retina is particularly sensitive. One of the most studied variants is p.Arg681His (rs527236113). 

The SAG gene is essential in the deactivation of the phototransduction cascade. It is the least common cause of dominant retinitis pigmentosa and for which fewer variants have been described.
625.technical_citations:

626.name: X-linked Retinitis Pigmentosa
626.description: X-linked retinitis pigmentosa is an inherited eye disorder caused primarily by mutations in the RPGR and RP2 genes, which are essential for the survival of the retina's photoreceptor cells. This condition causes a progressive vision loss that severely affects almost exclusively men, typically beginning in childhood with night blindness.
626.overview:
X-linked retinitis pigmentosa (XLRP) is one of the most severe forms of retinitis pigmentosa. The RPGR and RP2 genes are primarily responsible, causing approximately 70-90% and 10-20% of cases, respectively. The OFD1 gene is a much rarer cause. These genes encode proteins crucial for the function of the connecting cilium, a vital structure for molecular transport in the retina's photoreceptor cells (rods and cones). Mutations in these genes disrupt this function, leading to progressive cell death and retinal degeneration.

The global incidence of retinitis pigmentosa is approximately 1 in 4,000 people, and the X-linked form accounts for 10% to 15% of all cases. As it is X-linked inheritance, it primarily affects males, who manifest the disease severely. Carrier females of the mutation are usually asymptomatic or may present mild symptoms, such as patchy vision or later retinal degeneration. The disease is found in all populations worldwide without a specific ethnic predilection.
626.symptoms: The most characteristic initial symptom is night blindness (nyctalopia), which usually appears in the first decade of life. Subsequently, there is a gradual loss of peripheral visual field, leading to "tunnel vision". Central visual acuity and color perception are usually preserved until stages of the disease. The progression is severe, and many affected males reach total blindness between 30 and 40 years of age.
626.prevention: Currently there is no cure, and management focuses on patient support and ophthalmological follow-up. This includes the use of low vision aids, filters to reduce glare, and guidance for daily living. Vitamin A palmitate supplementation may be considered to modestly slow progression, although it must be under strict medical supervision. Specific gene therapies to replace the defective RPGR gene are in stages of clinical trials and represent the greatest hope for future treatment.
626.technical_description:
Retinitis pigmentosa (RP) refers to a group of hereditary disorders in which abnormalities of the photoreceptors (cones and rods) in the retina cause progressive vision loss. 

From a genetic point of view, the cause is very heterogeneous, with autosomal (recessive and dominant), X-linked, and unknown causes. X-linked retinitis pigmentosa cases account for between 5%-15% of the total and are mainly associated with the *RPGR* and *RP2* genes. 

The *RPGR* gene encodes the X-linked retinitis pigmentosa GTPase regulator, which is essential for the proper functioning of the connecting cilia of the retina's photoreceptor cells. Most pathogenic variants identified in this gene are loss-of-function types, especially because they cause frameshift mutations, such as c.2236_2237del, or *nonsense* mutations that result in a truncated protein.
626.technical_citations:
Fahim AT, Daiger SP, Weleber RG. Nonsyndromic Retinitis Pigmentosa Overview. 2000 Aug 4 [Updated 2023 Apr 6]. In: Adam MP, Feldman J, Mirzaa GM, et al., editors. GeneReviews® [Internet]. 

  O'Neal TB, Tripathy K, Luther EE. Retinitis Pigmentosa. [Updated 2024 Feb 12]. In: StatPearls [Internet] 

  Retinitis pigmentosa. Orphanet. ORPHA791 [June 2014].

 Tsang SH, Sharma T. X-linked Retinitis Pigmentosa. Adv Exp Med Biol. 2018.1085:31-35.

627.name: Retinitis Pigmentosa with or without Skeletal Abnormalities
627.description: Retinitis pigmentosa with or without skeletal anomalies is a condition caused by pathogenic variants of the CWC27 gene, characterized by the combination of the progressive vision loss typical of retinitis pigmentosa and various skeletal anomalies, especially short stature and abnormally short fingers and toes or brachydactyly.
627.overview:
The CWC27 gene provides instructions for making a protein that is an essential component of the spliceosome. The spliceosome is a complex molecular machine within our cells that processes messenger RNA (mRNA) in a critical step of gene expression called splicing. The process of splicing plays a fundamental role in generating the protein diversity needed to establish the complex structures and functions found in all eukaryotes. When mutations in both copies of the CWC27 gene alter its function, the efficiency and accuracy of the spliceosome are affected.

This defect in splicing is particularly detrimental to highly specialized cells that depend on the correct expression of many complex genes, such as photoreceptor cells in the retina and developing bone cells (osteoblasts).

The condition is inherited in an autosomal recessive pattern, meaning an individual must inherit a mutated gene from both parents to be affected.
627.symptoms: The symptoms are divided into two main categories. Ocular symptoms are those of typical retinitis pigmentosa, with onset of night blindness (nyctalopia) in childhood or adolescence, followed by a gradual but progressive loss of the peripheral visual field. Skeletal abnormalities are also present from an early age and commonly include disproportionate short stature and brachydactyly.
627.prevention: There is no cure for this condition; therefore, management focuses on monitoring symptoms and providing supportive care. This involves regular follow-up with an ophthalmologist to monitor the progression of retinitis pigmentosa. An orthopedic evaluation is recommended to manage any significant skeletal issues, such as scoliosis. Genetic counseling is an important resource for affected individuals and their families to understand the inheritance and implications of the disorder.
627.technical_description:
Retinitis pigmentosa associated with the CWC27 gene is a hereditary disease that causes progressive vision loss and, in many cases, presents along with other manifestations such as short stature, finger abnormalities (brachydactyly), and other neurological or craniofacial defects. The affected gene, CWC27, plays a crucial role in a process of genetic material editing called splicing. This function is indispensable for genetic instructions to be read correctly and functional proteins to be produced, being especially important for the survival of retinal cells. When the CWC27 gene has mutations, this editing process fails, leading to retinal degeneration and, depending on the mutation, to problems in other body systems.

A characteristic of variants in this gene is genotype-phenotype correlation, meaning that the location of the mutation in the gene directly influences the severity of the disease. A trend has been observed: variants that truncate or cut the protein at its initial part (N-terminal end) are usually associated with the most severe and multisystemic phenotypes. In contrast, variants that affect the final part of the protein (C-terminal end), such as the c.1002dup (p.Val335fs) mutation observed in some families, tend to cause a milder phenotype, often restricted to a later-onset retinal degeneration. This suggests that the initial region of the protein is critical for its main function, while the final region might be less essential.
627.technical_citations:
Xu M, Xie YA, Abouzeid H, et al. Mutations in the Spliceosome Component CWC27 Cause Retinal Degeneration with or without Additional Developmental Anomalies. Am J Hum Genet. 2017 Apr 6.100(4):592-604. doi: 10.1016/j.ajhg.2017.02.008. 

 Yassin SH, Henderson R, Lenberg J, et al. Further delineation of the CWC27-associated spliceosomeopathy: Case report and review of the literature. Am J Med Genet A. 2023 May.191(5):1378-1383.

628.name: Recessive Retinopathy (RPE65 gene)
628.description: Retinopathy associated with mutations in the RPE65 gene is a severe hereditary dystrophy of the retina that causes severe vision loss from childhood. Mutations in this gene prevent the proper functioning of the visual cycle, a process essential for light detection.
628.overview:
The RPE65 gene provides instructions for making an essential protein in the retinal pigment epithelium (RPE), a layer of cells that nourishes photoreceptors (the cones and rods). This protein, called all-trans-retinal isomerase, is a key enzyme in the "visual cycle," a biochemical process that recycles retinal, a form of vitamin A, allowing photoreceptors to "reset" and continuously detect light. When both copies of the RPE65 gene are mutated, this enzyme does not function correctly, disrupting the cycle and leading to the accumulation of toxic byproducts. This causes progressive photoreceptor death, resulting in vision loss.

RPE65 retinopathies are a subgroup of retinal dystrophies and primarily present as Leber congenital amaurosis (LCA) or early-onset retinitis pigmentosa (RP). The combined incidence of these conditions is approximately 2-3 per 100,000 births, and mutations in RPE65 account for a significant percentage of these cases. The condition affects all populations worldwide, with no clear ethnic predilection.
628.symptoms: Symptoms usually manifest from birth or during the first months of life. Affected individuals present with very severe visual impairment, nystagmus (rapid, involuntary eye movements), and a greatly reduced or absent pupillary light response. As the disease progresses, there is a progressive loss of peripheral visual field (tunnel vision) and night blindness (nyctalopia).
628.prevention: Management has historically focused on supportive therapies, such as the use of low vision aids and the adaptation of the educational and social environment. However, the field has been revolutionized by the approval of the first in vivo gene therapy for a hereditary disease, voretigene neparvovec (Luxturna®). This treatment consists of a subretinal injection that introduces a functional copy of the RPE65 gene into retinal cells, partially restoring visual function and halting disease progression.
628.technical_description:
RPE65-related retinopathy is inherited in an autosomal recessive manner. The gene codes for a fundamental protein in the maintenance of the retina's photoreceptors. To date, more than 200 variants associated with the disease have been identified, a large part of them with founder effect. 

In the European population, the c.11+5G>A variant, one of the most studied affecting splicing, the p.Tyr368His, a founder mutation from the Netherlands of the missense type, and the c.292_311del, a deletion more frequent in the Iberian Peninsula, stand out.
628.technical_citations:
Chao DL, Burr A, Pennesi M. RPE65-Related Leber Congenital Amaurosis / Early-Onset Severe Retinal Dystrophy. 2019 Nov 14. In: Adam MP, Feldman J, Mirzaa GM, et al., editors. GeneReviews® [Internet].

  Kumaran N, Georgiu M, Bainbridge JW, et al. Retinal Structure in RPE65-Associated Retinal Dystrophy. Invest Ophthalmol Vis Sci. 2020 Apr 29.61(4):47

668.name: Hereditary Paraganglioma-Pheochromocytoma Syndrome
668.description: Hereditary paraganglioma-pheochromocytoma syndrome is a genetic condition that predisposes to the development of neuroendocrine tumors, whose location and malignancy are influenced by the specific associated gene. Mutations in genes such as SDHB are associated with an increased risk of malignant tumors, while others, such as those in SDHD, SDHAF2 and MAX must be inherited paternally to manifest, since they are maternally imprinted genes.
668.overview:
Hereditary paraganglioma-pheochromocytoma syndrome is caused by mutations in a set of genes, including SDHB, SDHC, SDHD, SDHAF2, MAX, and TMEM127. These genes play a crucial role in the cellular energy cycle and oxygen sensing. Mutations in the SDHx (succinate dehydrogenase) gene family lead to an accumulation of succinate, which promotes tumor formation under conditions of apparent low oxygen. The MAX and TMEM127 genes are also involved in cellular proliferation pathways, and their alteration contributes to tumor development.

The global incidence of this syndrome is low, affecting approximately 1 in every 300,000 people per year, regardless of ethnicity, although the prevalence of specific mutations can vary. The affected gene largely determines the type and location of tumors. For instance, mutations in SDHB are frequently associated with extra-adrenal paragangliomas with a high potential for malignancy. On the other hand, mutations in SDHD and SDHAF2 typically cause head and neck paragangliomas and exhibit a phenomenon of maternal imprinting, where the disease only manifests if the mutation is inherited from the father.
668.symptoms: Symptoms depend on the tumor's location and whether it produces an excess of hormones like catecholamines. Secretory tumors can cause high blood pressure (sustained or in crises), intense headaches, excessive sweating, palpitations, and paleness. Tumors in the head and neck can cause hearing problems, hoarseness, or difficulty swallowing. The age of onset is variable, but it tends to be earlier in hereditary forms, often being diagnosed in childhood or in young adults.
668.prevention: The main management is surgical removal of tumors, whenever possible. Before surgery, pharmacological control with alpha-adrenergic blockers is crucial to stabilize blood pressure and prevent complications. In cases of metastatic disease or unresectable tumors, options include targeted therapies, chemotherapy, or radiotherapy with radioactive isotopes such as 131I-MIBG. Lifelong periodic follow-up with imaging tests and biochemical analyses is essential to detect recurrences or new tumors.
668.technical_description:
Hereditary paraganglioma-pheochromocytoma syndrome is caused by the presence of mutations in the MAX, SDHA, SDHAF2, SDHB, SDHC, SDHD, or TMEM127 genes, which act in cellular pathways that regulate the response to oxidative stress, apoptosis, and cell proliferation. They are inherited in an autosomal dominant pattern. The SDHD, SDHAF2, and possibly MAX genes have a peculiarity, in that they have demonstrated an effect depending on the parental origin, such that they only cause the disease when inherited paternally. Therefore, an individual who inherits a mutation in these genes from their father would be at risk of developing the disease, while someone who inherits it from their mother would not be (although exceptions have been found).

Of all the causal genes, the most frequent are SDHB (responsible for 50-55% of cases) and the SDHD gene (responsible for 20-25% of cases). The syndrome is characterized by a high rate of founder variants that are clustered in certain regions, such as p.Asp92Tyr (rs80338845), the best-known variant located in the SDHD gene.

Else T, Greenberg S, Fishbein L. Hereditary Paraganglioma-Pheochromocytoma Syndromes. 2008 May 21 [Updated 2023 Sep 21]. In: Adam MP, Feldman J, Mirzaa GM, et al., editors. GeneReviews® [Internet].

  Yang QD, Young WF. Pheochromocytoma and Paraganglioma. Endocrine Surgery Comprehensive Board Exam Guide (pp. 525-550). Springer International Publishing

  Hereditary pheochromocytoma-paraganglioma. Orphanet. ORPHA29072 [June 2025].

669.name: Peutz-Jeghers Syndrome
669.description: Peutz-Jeghers Syndrome (PJS) is a hereditary disorder caused by mutations in the tumor suppressor gene STK11, characterized by the appearance of benign gastrointestinal polyps and distinctive dark pigmentation on the skin and mucous membranes. Despite the benign nature of the polyps, this condition, with autosomal dominant inheritance, confers a very high risk of developing various types of cancer throughout life.
669.overview:
Peutz-Jeghers Syndrome is a genetic condition that predisposes to the development of polyps and cancer. The main cause lies in mutations in the STK11 gene (also known as LKB1), a fundamental tumor suppressor gene. The STK11 protein acts as a "master enzyme" that regulates metabolism, growth, and cell polarity, functioning as an essential brake on uncontrolled proliferation. A mutation that inactivates one of the two copies of the STK11 gene (autosomal dominant inheritance) is sufficient to release this brake. This alteration leads to the formation of characteristic hamartomatous polyps in the gastrointestinal tract and, more importantly, drastically increases susceptibility to malignant transformation in multiple tissues.

The incidence of PJS is rare, affecting approximately 1 in 50,000 to 200,000 people, without distinction of sex or ethnicity. The STK11 gene is primarily responsible for the disease. Genetic diagnosis is of vital importance because the risk of cancer is the most serious aspect of the syndrome. Individuals with PJS have a lifetime cumulative risk for any type of cancer that can reach 90%, including colorectal, stomach, small intestine, pancreatic, lung, breast, and reproductive organ (ovarian, testicular) cancers. Therefore, genetic confirmation is crucial to initiate an intensive and multidisciplinary oncological surveillance program from an early age.
669.symptoms: The characteristic signs of the syndrome usually appear in two stages. First, during early childhood, melanocytic macules (dark, freckle-like spots) develop around the mouth, on the lips, nostrils, and sometimes on hands and feet. These spots may fade with age. Second, during late childhood or adolescence, intestinal polyps can cause complications such as recurrent colicky abdominal pain, gastrointestinal bleeding leading to anemia, and an increased risk of intussusception (a medical emergency in which one part of the intestine slides into another).
669.prevention: SPJ management is proactive and focuses on surveillance to reduce the risk of polyp complications and, fundamentally, for early cancer detection. Management includes regular upper gastrointestinal endoscopies, colonoscopies, and capsule videoendoscopies (every 2-3 years) from late childhood to remove large polyps and prevent intussusception and bleeding. Furthermore, a strict screening program for multiple cancers is established from young adulthood, including surveillance of the breast, pancreas, ovaries, testes, and lungs, according to clinical guidelines.
669.technical_description:
PJS is caused by mutations in the STK11 gene and follows an autosomal dominant inheritance pattern, although in 25-50% of cases it occurs due to de novo mutations. STK11 is a tumor suppressor gene and encodes a protein that acts on the AMPK/mTOR signaling pathway.

PJS is characterized by great allelic heterogeneity, and pathogenic variants have been described throughout the entire gene. The described mutations are loss-of-function, resulting in an aberrant or absent protein. Large deletions and duplications are the most common type of mutation, accounting for up to 30% of all pathogenic variants. These variants are not detectable by conventional sequencing and require specific techniques.

Nonsense mutations, which introduce a premature stop codon, and frameshift mutations occur in 30-40% of cases. Both result in a shortened and non-functional protein.

Missense mutations account for approximately 20% of cases and usually occur in critical regions of the protein, such as the kinase domain, which is essential for its function.

670.name: Juvenile Polyposis Syndrome (BMPR1A gene)
670.description: Juvenile Polyposis Syndrome (JPS) is a hereditary disorder caused by mutations in genes such as BMPR1A, which lead to the growth of multiple benign polyps (hamartomas) in the gastrointestinal tract. Although the polyps are not cancerous, this condition, with autosomal dominant inheritance, significantly increases the risk of developing colorectal and gastric cancer throughout life.
670.overview:
Juvenile Polyposis Syndrome is a genetic condition that predisposes to gastrointestinal cancer. Its cause lies in mutations in genes that act as tumor suppressors, with the BMPR1A gene being one of the main ones involved. This gene encodes a receptor (bone morphogenetic protein receptor type IA) which is a key piece in a cellular signaling pathway (TGF-β) responsible for controlling cell growth, differentiation, and programmed death in the mucosa of the digestive tract. A mutation causing loss of function in one of the two copies of the BMPR1A gene (autosomal dominant inheritance) is sufficient to alter this control pathway, allowing cells to grow uncontrollably and form the characteristic juvenile polyps.

The incidence of JPS is rare, estimated between 1 in 100,000 and 1 in 160,000 people, and it affects all ethnic and demographic groups equally. Mutations in the BMPR1A and SMAD4 genes explain the majority of cases. Although the polyps are histologically benign, the cumulative risk of developing colorectal cancer for a person with JPS can exceed 50%, and there is also an increased risk of gastric cancer. For this reason, genetic diagnosis is crucial for initiating an intensive endoscopic surveillance program and for identifying at-risk family members who would benefit from this follow-up.
670.symptoms: Symptoms usually appear during childhood or adolescence, although diagnosis can be made at any age. The most common manifestation is rectal bleeding, visible in the stool, which can lead to chronic iron deficiency anemia. Other frequent symptoms include abdominal pain, diarrhea, constipation, and, on rare occasions, prolapse of a polyp through the anus. It is important to note that many individuals may not present symptoms until they undergo a screening endoscopy for family history.
670.prevention: The management of FAP focuses entirely on surveillance and cancer prevention, as there are no drugs to prevent polyp formation. The standard protocol includes regular colonoscopies and upper endoscopies (annually or biennially), starting in early adolescence (12-15 years old) or earlier if symptoms are present. During these procedures, all detected polyps are removed (polypectomy). If the number of polyps is unmanageable or if cancer is detected, surgery may be necessary to remove part or all of the colon (colectomy) or stomach (gastrectomy).
670.technical_description:
PJCR is genetically heterogeneous, being mainly caused by mutations in two genes: BMPR1A and SMAD4.

PJCR associated with BMPR1A follows an autosomal dominant inheritance pattern. This gene encodes for the Bone Morphogenetic Protein Receptor, Type IA, a membrane receptor that is a key piece in the Transforming Growth Factor beta (TGF-β) signaling pathway. This pathway is fundamental for controlling cellular processes such as growth, differentiation, and programmed cell death (apoptosis).

The presence of mutations in the BMPR1A gene is responsible for 40-50% of PJCR cases with an identified genetic cause. To date, pathogenic loss-of-function variants have been described throughout the gene. Large deletions and duplications are responsible for 20% of cases and cannot be detected by conventional sequencing, requiring specific methods for their study. Nonsense and frameshift variants are also frequent, and result in the production of a shortened and non-functional protein. Missense mutations and small deletions/insertions have also been described, although they are less frequent than the former.
670.technical_citations:
Larsen Haidle J, MacFarland SP, Howe JR. Juvenile Polyposis Syndrome. 2003 May 13 [Updated 2022 Feb 3]. In: Adam MP, Feldman J, Mirzaa GM, et al., editors. GeneReviews® [Internet].

679.name: Von Hippel-Lindau Syndrome
679.description: Von Hippel-Lindau syndrome (VHL) is an inherited disease caused by mutations in the tumor suppressor gene VHL, which predisposes to the development of various benign and malignant neoplasms in different organs. This condition requires multidisciplinary and proactive medical monitoring to detect and treat lesions early, significantly improving prognosis and quality of life.
679.overview:
Von Hippel-Lindau syndrome is an inherited genetic disorder that drastically increases the risk of developing a variety of tumors. The responsible gene, VHL, is a tumor suppressor gene; its normal function is to regulate cell growth and division, preventing tumor formation. Specifically, the VHL protein helps to degrade other proteins, such as hypoxia-inducible factors (HIFs), which accumulate when oxygen levels are low. Mutations in the VHL gene prevent the cell from correctly detecting oxygen levels, leading to a constant accumulation of HIF. This triggers a cascade of signals that promote the formation of new blood vessels (angiogenesis) and uncontrolled cell growth, resulting in the tumors characteristic of the disease.

The incidence of VHL syndrome is estimated at approximately 1 in 36,000 live births. It affects men and women equally, and is found in populations worldwide. The vast majority (around 80%) of affected individuals inherit the mutation from one of their parents, following an autosomal dominant inheritance pattern. This means that having just one copy of the altered VHL gene is sufficient to develop the condition, and an affected parent has a 50% chance of passing it on to each of their offspring. The remaining 20% of cases are "de novo," meaning the mutation occurs for the first time in the individual with no family history.
679.symptoms: The symptoms of VHL syndrome vary enormously depending on the type and location of the tumors, and the age of onset can range from infancy to adulthood, with a median diagnosis in the third decade of life. The most frequent tumors are hemangioblastomas in the brain, spinal cord, and retina. Also common are renal cell carcinomas (risk of kidney cancer), pheochromocytomas (adrenal gland tumors), pancreatic neuroendocrine tumors, and endolymphatic sac tumors in the inner ear, which can cause hearing loss.
679.prevention: VHL management focuses on active surveillance and early tumor treatment to prevent complications. It requires a multidisciplinary team and regular screening programs (MRI scans, ultrasounds, blood and urine tests, and ophthalmological reviews). Treatment has traditionally been surgical resection or radiotherapy of tumors. Recently, Belzutifan, a specific HIF-2α inhibitor, has been approved as the first systemic pharmacological treatment for certain VHL-associated tumors (renal cell carcinoma, CNS hemangioblastomas, and pancreatic neuroendocrine tumors) that do not require immediate surgery.
679.technical_description:
The cause of von Hippel-Lindau disease is the presence of mutations in the VHL gene, a tumor suppressor gene located on the short arm of chromosome 3. The disease is inherited in an autosomal dominant pattern. Penetrance is very high, causing symptoms to appear in the vast majority of carriers before the age of 65. In 80% of cases, the mutated gene is inherited from one of the parents, with a family history of the disease, while the remaining 20% is caused by the appearance of de novo mutations.

Most cases of VHL syndrome are caused by the presence of point mutations or small deletions/insertions. Among these, missense mutations, in which one amino acid is substituted for another (as in the case of p.Arg167Gln, rs5030821), and nonsense mutations, in which an incomplete protein is produced (as in p.Lys159Ter, rs1575932011), are notable.

A significant proportion of cases, between 20-40%, are caused by large deletions, which eliminate one or more exons, or even the entire gene.
679.technical_citations:
van Leeuwaarde RS, Ahmad S, van Nesselrooij B, et al. Von Hippel-Lindau Syndrome. 2000 May 17 [Updated 2025 May 1]. In: Adam MP, Feldman J, Mirzaa GM, et al., editors. GeneReviews® [Internet].

  Von Hippel-Lindau Disease. National Cancer Institute (NIH) [23 Oct 2024].

688.name: Congenital Myasthenic Syndrome (CHRNB1 gene)
688.description: Congenital myasthenic syndrome (CMS) is a rare genetic disorder that disrupts communication between nerves and muscles, causing muscle weakness. The condition is inherited and its symptoms, which typically appear in infancy, can be managed with specific drug treatments and supportive therapies.
688.overview:
Congenital myasthenic syndrome (CMS) is a group of rare inherited disorders that affect the neuromuscular junction, the point of chemical communication between nerve cells and muscle fibers. Mutations in the CHRNB1 gene are one of the causes of postsynaptic CMS. This gene contains the instructions for producing the beta subunit of the acetylcholine receptor (AChR), a crucial protein in the muscle cell membrane. When a nerve signal reaches the muscle, it releases acetylcholine, which binds to these receptors to trigger muscle contraction. Pathogenic variants in CHRNB1 can lead to a deficiency of receptors or alter their function, resulting in ineffective neuromuscular transmission and, consequently, muscle weakness and fatigability.

The global incidence of congenital myasthenic syndromes is estimated at 1-5 per 500,000 people, classifying them as rare diseases. No clear predisposition by sex or ethnicity has been described, affecting populations worldwide. Prevalence may be higher in specific communities due to founder effects, where a particular mutation is transmitted more frequently in an isolated or highly inbred population. The inheritance of mutations in CHRNB1 is typically autosomal recessive, meaning an individual must inherit two copies of the altered gene, one from each parent, to develop the disease. There are reported cases of autosomal dominant inheritance in which affected individuals
688.symptoms: Symptoms of CMS associated with CHRNB1 usually manifest from birth to early childhood. The main characteristic is muscle weakness that worsens with exertion and improves with rest (fatigability). The most common signs include drooping of the eyelids (ptosis), double vision (diplopia) due to involvement of the eye muscles, difficulty sucking, swallowing, and speaking (bulbar weakness), and generalized weakness in the limbs. In the most severe cases, respiratory failure requiring ventilatory support may occur.
688.prevention: Management of the disease is personalized according to the underlying genetic abnormality. In the case of CMS due to mutations in CHRNB1 that cause receptor deficiency, treatment typically includes drugs that increase the amount of acetylcholine at the neuromuscular junction, such as acetylcholinesterase inhibitors (e.g., pyridostigmine). Sometimes, other medications such as 3,4-diaminopyridine or adrenergic agonists like salbutamol are used to improve muscle function. Accurate genetic diagnosis is crucial, as some treatments beneficial for one type of CMS can be detrimental in others. Management is complemented by physical therapy, occupational therapy, speech therapy, and respiratory support if necessary.
688.technical_description:
Congenital myasthenic syndromes are a heterogeneous group of genetic diseases. In this condition, neuromuscular communication is affected, and several genes have been identified. The subtypes are divided into presynaptic, synaptic, and postsynaptic, depending on the affected gene.

The gene CHRNB1 encodes the beta subunit of the acetylcholine receptor and causes postsynaptic myasthenic syndrome. Mutations in this gene alter the structure or function of the acetylcholine receptor located on the muscle cell membranes, hindering the transmission of the nerve impulse.

To date, fewer than 30 variants in the CHRNB gene have been described as causing the pathology, most of them being loss-of-function variants. The most common are genetic variants that alter the reading frame, such as c.605dup (rs1447564693), nonsense mutations that introduce a premature stop codon, such as p.Tyr357Ter (p.Tyr357Ter), splicing mutations, and also large deletions, such as exon 8 deletion. It is important to note that founder effect mutations have been identified whose frequency may vary between populations.

  da Silva ISF, Sopa I, Gomes D, et al. From skin lesions to tyrosinemia type II diagnosis. J Inherit Metab Dis. 2024 Sep.47(5):1107-1108.

693.name: Hereditary Hemorrhagic Telangiectasia
693.description: Hereditary Hemorrhagic Telangiectasia (HHT) is a genetic disorder primarily caused by mutations in the ENG and ACVRL1 genes, which alter the normal development of blood vessels. This condition, with autosomal dominant inheritance, causes the formation of fragile vessels that can bleed spontaneously, affecting the skin and vital organs.
693.overview:
Hereditary Hemorrhagic Telangiectasia, or Rendu-Osler-Weber syndrome, is a vascular condition caused by mutations in key genes for blood vessel integrity. The genes ENG and ACVRL1 encode crucial proteins in the TGF-β signaling pathway in endothelial cells (endoglin and ALK1, respectively). A defect in either of these genes disrupts proper capillary formation, leading to direct and fragile connections between arteries and veins known as telangiectasias (in the skin and mucous membranes) and arteriovenous malformations, or AVMs (in internal organs). As an autosomal dominant disease, inheriting just one mutated copy of one of these genes is sufficient to develop the condition.

The incidence of HHT is estimated at 1 in 5,000 to 10,000 people, affecting men and women of all ethnicities equally worldwide. Although the most visible symptoms, such as nosebleeds, may seem mild, the true risk of HHT lies in the presence of AVMs in vital organs such as the lungs, liver, and brain. These internal malformations can go unnoticed for years but carry a significant risk of serious complications such as pulmonary hemorrhage, heart failure, or stroke. Therefore, genetic identification is fundamental for establishing a monitoring and prevention plan.
693.symptoms: The most common and earliest symptom is recurrent nosebleeds (epistaxis), which usually begin in childhood and worsen over time. During adolescence or adulthood, the appearance of telangiectasias is common, which are small, bright red spots or vascular spiders on the lips, tongue, face, and fingers. The presence of AVMs in internal organs can be asymptomatic or cause serious problems, such as difficulty breathing or neurological symptoms (migraines, seizures, strokes), while gastrointestinal bleeding can cause chronic anemia.
693.prevention: Currently, there is no cure for HHT, so management focuses on preventing complications and treating symptoms. Periodic screening studies are crucial to detect AVMs in the lungs, brain, and liver, even in asymptomatic patients. Nosebleeds are managed with local measures (lubrication, cauterization) and medications (tranexamic acid), while anemia is treated with iron supplements. High-risk AVMs are treated through embolization procedures to prevent severe hemorrhages.
693.technical_description:
The cause of HHT is the presence of mutations in genes involved in the formation and development of blood vessels. Among these genes, the most important are ACVRL1, which encodes for activin receptor-like kinase 1 and is responsible for 52% of cases, and ENG, which encodes for endoglin and is present in 44% of affected individuals. The disease is inherited in an autosomal dominant pattern and the penetrance of these pathogenic variants is approximately 95%, making it very common to find a clear family history of affected individuals. 

It is a disease with great allelic heterogeneity for which numerous pathogenic variants have been described. In the case of the ACVRL1 gene, the most frequent are missense mutations in exons 5 to 10 that affect the serine/threonine domain, a fundamental part of the protein. In the ENG gene, the most frequent are nonsense, frameshift, or splicing mutations, which result in a truncated and non-functional protein.

694.name: Tyrosinemia Type II
694.description: Tyrosinemia type II, or Richner-Hanhart syndrome, is an inherited metabolic disorder caused by mutations in the TAT gene. Deficiency of the enzyme tyrosine aminotransferase leads to the accumulation of tyrosine, mainly affecting the eyes and skin, and occasionally intellectual development.
694.overview:
Tyrosinemia type II is a genetic disorder of autosomal recessive inheritance, meaning an individual must inherit two altered copies of the TAT gene, one from each parent, to develop the pathology. The TAT gene encodes the enzyme tyrosine aminotransferase. This enzyme is the first and crucial step in the degradation pathway of the amino acid tyrosine, a fundamental component of the proteins we obtain from our diet. When this enzyme is deficient, tyrosine cannot be metabolized correctly and accumulates to toxic levels in the blood and tissues.

This accumulation is the direct cause of the disease's symptoms. The condition is rare, with an estimated incidence of less than 1 in every 250,000 people worldwide. Although it can affect individuals of any ethnic background, a higher frequency of cases has been observed in populations of Arab and Mediterranean descent, where consanguineous unions may be more common.
694.symptoms: The main symptoms of Tyrosinemia type II are ocular and cutaneous, and they usually manifest at early ages. During the first year of life, the appearance of ocular symptoms such as excessive tearing, redness, pain, and marked sensitivity to light (photophobia) is common, due to the deposition of tyrosine crystals in the cornea (pseudodendritic keratitis). Cutaneous manifestations usually develop after the first year and consist of painful and thickened lesions on the palms of the hands and soles of the feet (palmoplantar hyperkeratosis). In approximately half of untreated cases, a variable degree of intellectual disability may be present.
694.prevention: The fundamental pillar of managing Tyrosinemia type II is strict lifelong dietary control. Treatment consists of a very low-tyrosine and phenylalanine (a precursor of tyrosine in the body) amino acid diet. This involves eliminating protein-rich foods such as meats, dairy products, and legumes, and substituting them with special metabolic formulas and low-protein foods. Early diagnosis and treatment are crucial, as dietary restriction has proven very effective in preventing or completely reversing ocular and cutaneous lesions, and can reduce the risk of neurological involvement. The use of oral retinoids may be considered to help manage severe skin lesions.
694.technical_description:
Tyrosinemia type II is characterized by the presence of corneal dystrophy, painful palmar hyperkeratosis, and variable intellectual disability. Individuals treated since infancy may be asymptomatic or only present mild ocular and skin manifestations. The diagnosis is established by identifying pathogenic variants in the TAT gene, which encodes tyrosine aminotransferase and significantly reduces its activity. It is a rare disease with fewer than 100 cases reported to date.

To date, more than 40 pathogenic variants associated with the pathology have been identified. The most common variant, especially in individuals from northern Italy, is p.Arg57Ter (rs118203914), which introduces a premature stop codon, generating an aberrant protein. a founder variant of northern Italian populations. Other described founder pathogenic variants include p.Cys151Tyr (rs1357257156) in Lebanon and p.Arg417Ter (rs118203916) in Palestine, among others. Consanguinity is frequent among the parents of affected individuals, which may contribute to a higher prevalence of this disorder among Arab populations.

695.name: Congenital disorder of glycosylation type 1c (ALG6 gene)
695.description: Congenital Disorder of Glycosylation type 1c (CDG-1c) is a rare genetic disease that arises from mutations in the ALG6 gene, affecting glycosylation, a vital process for the proper functioning of many proteins. This condition manifests with a wide range of symptoms that can impact multiple body systems.
695.overview: Congenital Disorder of Glycosylation type 1c (CDG-1c) is an autosomal recessive genetic disease, meaning an individual must inherit two altered copies of the ALG6 gene (one from each parent) to develop the condition. This disorder affects glycosylation, a fundamental biological process in which sugar chains (glycans) are added to proteins. The ALG6 gene encodes a glycosyltransferase, an essential enzyme that catalyzes the addition of the first glucose to the GlcNAc2Man9 oligosaccharide chain, a crucial step in the N-linked glycosylation pathway in the endoplasmic reticulum. When the ALG6 gene has mutations, this enzyme does not function correctly, resulting in defective protein glycosylation. Misfolded, poorly glycosylated proteins may not fold correctly, affecting their function and stability, leading to widespread dysfunction of multiple organs and systems. The incidence of CDG-Ic is extremely low, considered an ultra-rare disease. Although not associated with a specific ethnicity or geographic population, like many recessive diseases, its prevalence may be slightly higher in communities with consanguineous marriages.
695.symptoms: The symptoms of CDG-1c are variable and can manifest from infancy. The most common signs include psychomotor developmental delay, muscle hypotonia, seizures, and coagulation problems, which can lead to episodes of bleeding or thrombosis. Gastrointestinal problems may also occur, such as feeding difficulties and diarrhea, as well as liver dysfunction and abnormal stratus.
695.prevention: The management of CDG-1c is mainly supportive and symptomatic, as there is currently no specific curative treatment that can correct the alterations present in the ALG6 gene. The objective is to alleviate symptoms and improve the patient's quality of life. This may include physical and occupational therapy to improve motor development, anticonvulsant medications to control seizures, and nutritional support to address feeding problems. Coagulation complications often require monitoring and, in some cases, may be managed with plasma transfusions or clotting factors. There are no available pharmacological treatments that directly correct the glycosylation defect caused by mutations in ALG6.
695.technical_description:
Congenital disorder of glycosylation type 1c is a rare pathology for which, to date, fewer than 100 cases have been described, all with symptoms of hypotonia and developmental delay. 

It is caused by a defect in the enzyme alpha-1,3-glucosyltransferase, encoded by the ALG6 gene. This protein is essential for the N-glycosylation process. The cause is the presence of mutations in this gene in homozygosity or compound heterozygosity. To date, there are dozens of described variants that could be associated with the disease. The most common is the p.Ala333Val variant (rs121908443), which has been reported in patients, both in homozygosity and compound heterozygosity.

696.name: Congenital disorder of glycosylation type 1k (ALG1 gene)
696.description: Congenital Disorder of Glycosylation type 1k is a rare genetic disease caused by mutations in the ALG1 gene, which alters a crucial process for the function of many proteins. This condition is characterized by a wide range of symptoms affecting multiple body systems.
696.overview: Congenital Disorder of Glycosylation type 1k (CDG-Ik), also known as mannosyltransferase 1 deficiency, is a rare autosomal recessive genetic disorder that affects a fundamental biochemical process in our cells: protein glycosylation. The ALG1 gene (asparagine-linked glycosyltransferase 1) encodes the catalytic subunit of N-acetylglucosaminyltransferase I, an enzyme essential for the assembly of the oligosaccharide precursor Glc3Man9GlcNAc2-PP-Dol. This precursor is crucial for the correct glycosylation of a large number of proteins that are important for various cellular functions, including protein folding, trafficking, and stability. Mutations in the ALG1 gene lead to defective or insufficient production of this enzyme, resulting in abnormal protein glycosylation and, consequently, in the dysfunction of multiple organs and systems. The exact incidence of CDG-Ik is difficult to determine due to its rarity and diagnostic challenge, but it is estimated to be one of the less common forms of CDG. No specific population has been identified as disproportionately affected, although, as with most rare recessive diseases, there may be a higher prevalence in populations with high rates of consanguinity.
696.symptoms: The symptoms of CDG-Ik are highly variable and can appear from birth or in early childhood. The main symptoms include intellectual disability, hypotonia (low muscle tone), seizures, microcephaly, skeletal anomalies, facial dysmorphia, feeding problems and vomiting, as well as liver dysfunction and coagulation problems. Visual and hearing problems may also occur.
696.prevention: The management of CDG-Ik is mainly supportive and symptomatic, as there is currently no definitive cure. Treatments focus on symptom relief and improving the patient's quality of life. This may include physical and occupational therapy to address developmental delays and hypotonia, anticonvulsant medications to control seizures, and nutritional support for feeding problems. In some cases, blood or plasma transfusions may be administered to manage coagulopathies. There are no specific pharmacological treatments that directly address the underlying cause of the ALG1 deficiency.
696.technical_description:
Congenital disorder of glycosylation type 1k is caused by the presence of pathogenic variants in the ALG1 gene. This gene encodes a mannosyltransferase, an enzyme from the glycosyltransferase family that participates in the N-glycan biosynthesis process. It is a very rare disease with a prevalence of less than 1 in 1,000,000 and fewer than 100 described cases. 

To date, 13 pathogenic variants have been described associated with the disease, both in homozygosis and in compound heterozygosis. Of these, the most frequent is p.Ser258Leu (rs28939378), reported in multiple affected individuals and which introduces a non-conservative amino acid change that affects the protein's activity and, therefore, the glycosylation process.

700.name: Wilms Tumor 1 (WT1 gene)
700.description: Wilms tumor, also known as nephroblastoma, is the most common type of kidney cancer in childhood. Mutations in the WT1 gene, a tumor suppressor gene crucial for genitourinary development, are responsible for a portion of these tumors, often associated with genetic syndromes with other congenital anomalies.
700.overview:
Wilms tumor is an embryonic cancer that originates from immature kidney cells. The WT1 gene plays a fundamental role as a regulator of normal kidney and gonad development. When this tumor suppressor gene has mutations, it can lose its ability to control cell growth, leading to tumor formation. Although most Wilms tumors are sporadic (non-hereditary), approximately 10-20% are associated with pathogenic variants in predisposition genes, with WT1 being one of the most important.

Hereditary (germline) mutations in WT1 cause cancer predisposition syndromes, such as WAGR syndrome (Wilms tumor, aniridia, genitourinary anomalies, and intellectual disability), Denys-Drash syndrome, and Frasier syndrome. The global incidence of Wilms tumor is approximately 1 in 10,000 children under 15 years of age. It affects children worldwide, with no clear ethnic predilection, although some studies suggest a slightly higher incidence in children of African descent and lower in those of Asian descent.
700.symptoms: The most common sign of Wilms tumor is the presence of a palpable and generally painless abdominal mass or swelling. Other symptoms may include abdominal pain, fever, blood in the urine (hematuria), and high blood pressure (hypertension). The most frequent age of onset is between 3 and 4 years old, and the vast majority of cases are diagnosed before the age of 6. In syndromic cases associated with WT1, other clinical findings such as ocular or urogenital abnormalities may be present from birth.
700.prevention: The treatment of Wilms' tumor is usually very effective, with high cure rates. The therapeutic strategy is multimodal and is based on the combination of surgery (nephrectomy) to remove the affected kidney, chemotherapy to eliminate remaining cancer cells, and, in cases of disease or unfavorable histology, radiotherapy. The specific protocol is adapted to the stage of the tumor. The identification of a mutation in WT1 is key for the long-term management of associated syndromes and for family genetic counseling.
700.technical_description:
Although most cases of Wilms' tumor are sporadic, a percentage are associated with the presence of pathogenic variants in certain genes such as WT1. This gene encodes a protein that acts as a transcription factor and is essential in the development of the urogenital system. 

To date, approximately 20 associated variants have been identified, including missense mutations, nonsense mutations, splicing variants, and small deletions or insertions. It is inherited in an autosomal dominant manner. One of the most frequent variants is c.1447+5G>A (rs587776576), which affects gene splicing and is also related to other pathologies such as Denys-Drash syndrome, Frasier syndrome, Meacham syndrome, or nephrotic syndrome.
