705.name: X-linked Adrenoleukodystrophy
705.description: Adrenoleukodystrophy is a peroxisomal disorder that primarily affects the nervous system and adrenal glands. It is caused by mutations in the ABCD1 gene, which lead to the accumulation of very long-chain fatty acids. Its incidence is estimated at 1 in 17,000 births.
705.overview:
Adrenoleukodystrophy (ALD) encompasses a group of genetic disorders that primarily affect the nervous system and adrenal glands. Within this group, the most common and well-known form is X-linked adrenoleukodystrophy (X-ALD). This disease is caused by pathogenic variants in the ABCD1 gene, which produces the ALDP protein (ATP-binding cassette sub-family D member 1). ALDP is a transporter protein that plays a crucial role in carrying very long-chain fatty acids (VLCFAs) into peroxisomes.

Peroxisomes are cellular organelles that act as "recycling centers," responsible for breaking down and processing various substances, including VLCFAs. When there are mutations in the ABCD1 gene, the ALDP protein is not produced correctly or does not function as it should. This leads to VLCFAs not being able to be transported and metabolized efficiently, resulting in their accumulation in various tissues of the body. This accumulation is particularly damaging in the brain, spinal cord, and adrenal glands
705.symptoms:
Given its X-linked recessive inheritance pattern, ALD affects men more severely than women. Women who carry one copy of the mutated ABCD1 gene are usually asymptomatic. In contrast, men with one altered copy of the ABCD1 gene develop the disease.

X-ALD mainly presents with three clinical forms: primary adrenal insufficiency (PAI), adrenomyeloneuropathy (AMN), and a progressive cerebral form (cALD).

PAI is very common in males with X-ALD, affecting 80% of symptomatic male patients. Although it generally precedes the onset of neurological symptoms, in some cases it can be the only symptom present.AMN is the most common of the three clinical presentations, manifesting as a slow progressive spastic paraparesis and sensory ataxia that usually appears between the ages of 20 and 50. Some carrier women may also develop milder AMN, with a later onset and slower progression than in men with AMN.The inflammatory progressive cerebral form with demyelination of the white matter, with more severe symptoms, generally presents between 4 and 10 years of age.
705.prevention: The management of X-ALD aims to slow the progression of the disease and control symptoms. For childhood cerebral X-ALD, hematopoietic stem cell transplant and gene therapy for ALD can help slow the progression of demyelination if performed early, before significant neurological damage has occurred. Adrenal insufficiency is treated with hormone replacement therapy (corticosteroids). The use of Lorenzo's oil, a mixture of unsaturated fatty acids, in combination with a fat-restricted diet, is used in certain cases, under medical supervision and with MRI monitoring, to help normalize ABCD1 gene levels, but its efficacy is limited when applied after the onset of the disease. Symptomatic management includes physical therapy, occupational therapy, and medications for spasticity, pain, and seizures. Regular monitoring of neurological function and adrenal status is essential.
705.technical_description:
X-linked adrenoleukodystrophy (X-ALD) is characterized by remarkable variability in its symptoms, despite being caused by mutations in a single gene. This means that identical variants can lead to very different forms of the disease, from mild AMN to severe childhood cALD, even within the same family. This variability suggests the influence of "modifier genes" and possibly additional environmental or epigenetic factors that determine how X-ALD manifests in each individual. Although the existence of a major modifier gene is suspected, efforts to identify it have been unsuccessful so far.

More than 800 pathogenic variants and more than 150 possibly pathogenic variants have been found in the ABCD1 gene. Between 4 and 19% of cases are due to de novo mutations. Some examples of variants that have been detected most frequently among patients with ALD include: rs128624218 (c.796G>A), rs128624219 (c.1202G>A), rs398123102 (c.1553G>A), rs398123105 (c.1679C>G), rs150346282 (c.1825G>A), rs11146842 (c.1850G>A), rs387906494 (c.1415_1416del), and rs1569541203 (c.1978C>T).

706.name: Familial Thoracic Aortic Aneurysm and Dissection (ACTA2 gene)
706.description: Pathogenic variants in the ACTA2 gene are the cause of familial thoracic aortic aneurysm and dissection (FTAAD), an autosomal dominant disorder. This condition compromises the structural integrity of the aorta, predisposing to the formation of aneurysms and dissections. Given that these events represent a medical emergency with a high risk of mortality, proactive cardiovascular surveillance is required.
706.overview:
Pathogenic variants in the ACTA2 gene are a leading cause of familial thoracic aortic aneurysm and dissection (FTAAD). This gene encodes smooth muscle alpha-2 actin, a protein essential for the strength and contractility of blood vessels, particularly the aorta. Mutations impair this function, leading to progressive weakening of the aortic wall. This structural fragility predisposes the vessel to dilation (aneurysm) and tearing (dissection), which constitutes a life-threatening medical emergency.

According to family studies, 20% of patients with thoracic aortic aneurysm and dissection have a first-degree relative with the disease. In individuals with a first-degree family history, the disease typically has a more rapid course and an earlier age of onset compared to sporadic cases. FTAAD is inherited in an autosomal dominant manner, meaning that only one copy of a mutation in the ACTA2 gene is required to increase the risk of disease development.
706.symptoms: Individuals with a variant in ACTA2 can be asymptomatic for years. The main manifestation is an aneurysm of the aortic root or ascending thoracic aorta, which can lead to dissection, presenting as sudden, sharp chest or back pain. Other associated features may include livedo reticularis (a reticulated, mottled skin pattern), iris flocculi (small cysts on the pupillary margin), and an increased risk of early-onset coronary artery disease and stroke. The age of onset for aortic events is variable, often occurring between 30 and 60 years of age.
706.prevention: Management focuses on proactive surveillance and risk reduction. This includes regular imaging of the aorta (echocardiogram, CT scan, or MRI) to monitor its diameter. Medications such as beta-blockers or angiotensin II receptor blockers (e.g., losartan) are prescribed to reduce blood pressure and stress on the aortic wall. Lifestyle modifications, such as avoiding contact sports and isometric weightlifting, are recommended.
706.technical_description:
The ACTA2 gene provides instructions for making a protein called alpha-2 actin. This protein is part of the contractile machinery of smooth muscle cells. These are the specialized cells that line the walls of blood vessels, including the aorta, the largest artery in the body. The proper functioning of alpha-2 actin allows these cells to contract and relax, which helps regulate blood pressure and maintain the structural integrity and elasticity of blood vessel walls. In essence, the ACTA2 gene is vital for keeping your arteries strong and flexible.

Pathogenic variants (mutations) in the ACTA2 gene are the most common cause of inherited thoracic aortic aneurysms and dissections. These variants typically alter the structure or function of the alpha-2 actin protein, weakening the smooth muscle cells in the aortic wall. This weakness can cause the aorta to bulge outward (aneurysm) and makes it susceptible to tearing (dissection).

Some of the recurring pathogenic variants reported in two or more families include c.635G>A (p.Arg212Gln), c.353G>A (p.Arg118Gln), and c.445C>T (p.Arg149Cys), with the latter also being highlighted for its association with premature coronary artery disease. Furthermore, the c.772C>T (p.Arg258Cys) mutation has been linked to the early onset of ADAF.

707.name: Familial Thoracic Aortic Aneurysm and Dissection (MYH11 gene)
707.description: Pathogenic variants in the MYH11 gene compromise the structural integrity of aortic smooth muscle cells, leading to a high risk of aortic dilation (aneurysm) and tearing (dissection). Since these events represent a medical emergency with a high risk of mortality, proactive cardiovascular surveillance is required.
707.overview:
Pathogenic variants in the MYH11 gene are a leading cause of familial thoracic aortic aneurysm and dissection (FTAAD). This gene encodes the myosin-11 protein, a component of the heavy chain of myosin in muscle. Mutations in the MYH11 gene alter the structure and assembly of myosin filaments, affecting the strength and contractility of the smooth muscles that form the walls of the aorta and other arteries. This structural fragility predisposes the aorta to progressive dilation, known as an aneurysm, and to a potential tear in its inner layer, a life-threatening event called dissection. The condition is inherited in an autosomal dominant pattern, meaning that inheriting a single altered copy of the gene from a parent is sufficient to increase the risk of the disorder.

FTAAD caused by variants in MYH11 is considered a rare disorder. While an estimated 20% of all aortic aneurysms are familial cases, only a small portion of these cases are due to mutations in MYH11. A notable feature of MYH11-related disease is its frequent association with patent ductus arteriosus (PDA), a congenital heart defect.
707.symptoms: Many individuals with ADAF related to MYH11 are asymptomatic. When aortic dissection occurs, symptoms are typically sudden and severe, including characteristic pain, described as tearing or ripping in the chest, back, or abdomen. The age of onset of aortic dilation is variable, but it can begin in adolescence or early adulthood. In addition to aortic disease, associated findings may include patent ductus arteriosus (PDA) at birth, early-onset cataracts, and small cystic lesions in the colored part of the eye (iris flocculi).
707.prevention: Management focuses on proactive surveillance and risk reduction. This includes regularly obtaining images of the aorta (echocardiogram, CT scan, or MRI) to monitor its diameter. Medications such as beta-blockers or angiotensin II receptor blockers (e.g., losartan) are prescribed to reduce blood pressure and stress on the aortic wall. Lifestyle modifications are recommended, such as avoiding contact sports and isometric weightlifting.
707.technical_description:
Familial aortic aneurysm and dissection (FAAD) is a hereditary connective tissue disorder that predisposes individuals to progressive dilation (aneurysm) and tearing (dissection) of the thoracic aorta, with an elevated risk of rupture and premature death. This pathology is related to mutations in the MYH11 gene. This gene encodes the heavy chain of smooth muscle myosin, a protein essential for the structural integrity and contractile function of the smooth muscle cells that make up the aortic wall. A defect in this protein weakens the structure of the vessel, making it susceptible to hemodynamic changes and leading to degeneration of its wall.

The disease is inherited in an autosomal dominant pattern, implying a 50% risk of transmission to offspring. The pathogenic genetic variants described are concentrated in critical domains of the protein, such as the coiled-coil tail region, altering its function. These mainly include missense mutations, with the variant c.2135G>A (p.Arg712Gln) being an example. A distinctive clinical feature of high diagnostic value is the strong association of mutations in MYH11 with patent ductus arteriosus, a congenital heart defect.

708.name: Dilated Cardiomyopathy 1P (PLN gene)
708.description: Dilated cardiomyopathy 1P (DCM1P) is a heart condition caused by mutations in the PLN gene. These mutations affect a protein that regulates calcium, an element essential for cardiac contraction, which can lead to progressive weakening and heart failure.
708.overview: Dilated cardiomyopathy 1P (CMD1P) is a genetic disorder of the heart muscle that causes the heart chambers to enlarge and weaken, affecting its ability to pump blood efficiently. This condition is inherited in an autosomal dominant pattern, meaning that a single copy of an abnormal gene variant can cause the disease. The PLN gene encodes the phospholamban protein, which is a key regulator of the sarcoplasmic reticulum calcium pump (SERCA2a) function, vital for the contraction and relaxation of cardiac muscle cells. Mutations in PLN interfere with phospholamban's ability to properly regulate calcium handling. Dysfunction in calcium regulation reduces the force of cardiac contraction and the speed of relaxation, which, over time, leads to mechanical stress and the development of heart dilation. Although the incidence of CMD in general is approximately 1 in 2,500 people, mutations in PLN are a particularly important and prevalent cause in certain populations, such as those of Dutch origin.
708.symptoms: CMD1P symptoms usually manifest in adulthood, with a variable onset. The main signs are progressive heart failure, shortness of breath (dyspnea), fatigue, and, in some cases, electrical conduction problems in the heart that can lead to arrhythmias.
708.prevention: The management of dilated cardiomyopathy due to mutations in PLN focuses on treating the symptoms of heart failure and preventing arrhythmias. Pharmacological treatments include beta-blockers and diuretics. Due to the severe nature of the disease, implantation of an implantable cardioverter-defibrillator (ICD) may be necessary, and a heart transplant may be a therapeutic option.
708.technical_description:
Dilated cardiomyopathy 1P is caused by the presence of mutations in the PLN gene, which encodes the phospholamban protein, a crucial regulator of calcium homeostasis in cardiac muscle cells. These mutations result in a reduction in contractile strength, which, over time, leads to heart dilation and heart failure.

Mutations in PLN have been associated with a particularly severe and progressive phenotype that often leads to end-stage heart failure and sudden death. Various pathogenic variants have been identified, but p.Arg14del (c.37AGA[1], rs397516784) is the most prevalent and studied. It is a single amino acid deletion that has a severe effect on cardiac contraction. It is found at a high frequency in the Netherlands and Germany. This variant has also been linked to arrhythmogenic cardiomyopathy of the right ventricle.
708.technical_citations:
Eldemire R, Mestroni L, Taylor MRG. Genetics of Dilated Cardiomyopathy. Annu Rev Med. 2024 Jan 29.75:417-426.

709.name: Juvenile Polyposis Syndrome (SMAD4 gene)
709.description: Juvenile polyposis syndrome (JPS) is an inherited condition caused by mutations in the SMAD4 gene, which disrupts cell growth regulation and leads to the formation of hamartomatous polyps in the gastrointestinal tract. This alteration carries a high risk of developing cancer, especially colorectal and gastric.
709.overview:
Juvenile polyposis syndrome (JPS) is an inherited genetic disorder with an autosomal dominant inheritance pattern. Mutations in the SMAD4 gene are responsible for approximately 20-30% of cases. This gene is crucial in the TGF-β signaling pathway, a molecular cascade that controls vital cellular processes such as growth, proliferation, and programmed cell death. When SMAD4 is altered, this regulation is lost, which facilitates the uncontrolled formation of benign growths known as juvenile or hamartomatous polyps in the lining of the gastrointestinal tract, mainly in the colon, stomach, and small intestine.

Although these polyps are benign in nature, their presence significantly increases the risk of developing gastrointestinal cancer throughout life, with a cumulative probability of up to 50%. The incidence of JPS is estimated at 1 in 100,000 people, affecting men and women equally and without a clearly defined ethnic predilection. Individuals with mutations in SMAD4 may present with a more severe phenotype, with a higher number of gastric polyps, and often associate features of hereditary hemorrhagic telangiectasia (HHT) syndrome.
709.symptoms: The symptoms of juvenile polyposis syndrome usually manifest before 20 years of age, although the age of onset is variable. The most common signs include rectal bleeding, which can lead to iron deficiency anemia, abdominal pain, diarrhea, or constipation. In some cases, the polyps may prolapse or cause an intestinal obstruction.
709.prevention: The management of FAP focuses on surveillance and cancer prevention. Endoscopic surveillance (colonoscopy and upper endoscopy) is recommended to begin in adolescence or earlier if symptoms appear, repeating it every 1 to 3 years. During these procedures, all detected polyps are removed (polypectomy). In cases with an endoscopically unmanageable polyp burden or with high-grade dysplasia or cancer, surgery (colectomy or gastrectomy) may be necessary. There are no specific curative pharmacological treatments.
709.technical_description:
Juvenile polyposis syndrome is associated, mainly, with two genes: BMPR1A and SMAD4, each responsible for between 20-30% of cases. 

To date, more than 150 mutations have been described distributed throughout the entire gene; however, there are certain critical regions where mutations are more frequent. The MH2 domain is the most affected region, crucial for the interaction of the SMAD4 protein with other proteins in the cellular signaling cascade. The MH1 domain, another of the most affected, is responsible for the binding of the protein to DNA for its action as a transcriptional factor.

Among the pathogenic variants described, we find those of different natures. Missense mutations, in which one amino acid is substituted for another, are quite common and occur, especially, in the MH2 domain. Nonsense mutations, in which a premature stop codon is introduced, produce an incomplete and non-functional protein. Small insertions or deletions that alter the reading frame, producing an anomalous non-functional protein, have also been described. Furthermore, it is important to highlight that up to 30% of cases are produced by large deletions that include exons or even the entire gene.

Larsen Haidle J, MacFarland SP, Howe JR. Juvenile Polyposis Syndrome. 2003 May 13 [Updated 2022 Feb 3]. In: Adam MP, Feldman J, Mirzaa GM, et al., editors. GeneReviews® [Internet].

710.name: Long QT Syndrome Type 3
710.description: Long QT syndrome type 3 is a genetic heart rhythm disorder caused by mutations in the SCN5A gene, which encodes a defective sodium channel. This alteration, accounting for 5% to 10% of LQTS cases, increases the risk of lethal arrhythmias that typically occur during rest or sleep.
710.overview:
Long QT syndrome type 3 (LQTS3) is an inherited cardiac channelopathy that predisposes to life-threatening ventricular arrhythmias. The genetic cause lies in mutations in the SCN5A gene, which provides instructions for making the alpha subunit of the cardiac sodium channel (NaV1.5). Unlike types 1 and 2, LQTS3 mutations cause a "gain of function": sodium channels do not inactivate properly after the beat, allowing excessive and sustained entry of sodium into the cardiac cell. This excess ionic current prolongs the repolarization phase (the heart's electrical "reset"), which is observed as a prolonged QT interval on the electrocardiogram.

LQTS3 is the third most common form of the disease, accounting for 5% to 10% of all cases of long QT syndrome. Its exact incidence is difficult to determine, but it falls within the general prevalence of LQTS, estimated at 1 in 2,000 people. The condition affects individuals of all ethnic and geographic backgrounds equally, with no populations described as having a markedly higher prevalence.
710.symptoms: Cardiac events in LQT3 have a distinguishing characteristic: they tend to occur during periods of slow heart rate (bradycardia), such as rest or sleep, unlike other types of LQT that are exercise-induced. The main symptoms are syncope (fainting) or sudden cardiac arrest. Although symptoms can appear at any age, LQT3 events often present in adulthood, with a peak incidence between the third and fourth decades of life.
710.prevention: SQTL3 management focuses on preventing deadly arrhythmias. Unlike types 1 and 2, beta-blockers are not as effective and are not considered first-line monotherapy. Pharmacological management is mainly based on sodium channel blockers, such as mexiletine or ranolazine, which specifically correct the channel defect. An implantable cardioverter-defibrillator (ICD) is often recommended for high-risk patients, as it can immediately detect and treat a lethal arrhythmia.
710.technical_description:
The SCN5A gene is associated with long QT syndrome type 3. The presence of mutations in this gene is found in 10% of those affected by the disease. This gene codes for the main subunit of the cardiac sodium channel, which briefly opens at the beginning of each heartbeat to allow massive entry of sodium ions. The presence of certain genetic variants causes a defect in the inactivation mechanism, causing the channels to remain open longer. Therefore, LQT3 originates from gain-of-function mutations. 

It is important to note that this gene shows pleiotropy, as the presence of loss-of-function mutations causes another hereditary arrhythmia: Brugada syndrome. 

Most mutations that cause LQT3 are located in critical regions for channel inactivation, such as the region connecting domains III and IV and the c-terminal region. One of the best-known mutations is p.Gln1506_Pro1508del (ΔKPQ,rs397514251). This is a deletion of three amino acids whose discovery was essential for understanding the mechanism of the pathology. Other frequent mutations are p.Glu1783Lys (rs137854601), located in the C-terminal domain, and p.Arg1623Gln (rs137854600), located in the region connecting domains III and IV.

711.name: Long QT Syndrome Types 1 and 2
711.description: Long QT syndrome (LQTS) types 1 and 2 are the most common forms of this hereditary cardiac channelopathy, caused by mutations in the KCNQ1 and KCNH2 genes, respectively. These genetic alterations affect the heart's potassium channels, delaying the electrical recharging of cardiac cells and predisposing to dangerous ventricular arrhythmias, often triggered by exercise or sudden emotions.
711.overview:
Long QT syndrome is a heart rhythm disorder characterized by a delay in ventricular repolarization, visible as a prolonged QT interval on the electrocardiogram. Types 1 (LQT1) and 2 (LQT2) are the most prevalent, together accounting for about 75-80% of all cases. LQT1 is caused by loss-of-function mutations in the KCNQ1 gene, which encodes the alpha subunit of the IKs potassium channel. This channel is crucial for shortening the cardiac action potential during exercise and adrenergic stimulation. LQT2 is due to mutations in the KCNH2 gene, which encodes the alpha subunit of the IKr potassium channel, responsible for the final phase of repolarization at rest. In both cases, the genetic defect prevents potassium from leaving cardiac cells at the appropriate speed, prolonging electrical recharging and creating a vulnerable substrate for arrhythmias.

The combined incidence of LQTS is estimated at approximately 1 in 2,000 people. These conditions affect all populations globally without distinction of ethnicity or sex, although women tend to have a slightly higher risk of cardiac events, especially in LQT2. Inheritance is autosomal dominant, meaning an affected parent has a 50% chance of transmitting the condition to their offspring.
711.symptoms:
Symptoms include syncope (fainting), seizures (due to lack of oxygen to the brain during an arrhythmia), or sudden cardiac arrest. The age of onset is variable, from infancy to adulthood, although many events occur in adolescence. Specific triggers are characteristic:

LQT1: Cardiac events typically occur during physical exercise, especially swimming, or emotional stress.

LQT2: Triggers are usually sudden auditory stimuli (such as an alarm clock or a ringing phone), emotional stress, or the postpartum period in women.
711.prevention: The mainstay of treatment is lifestyle modification, avoiding known triggers and certain QT-prolonging medications. First-line pharmacologic therapy for both types are beta-blockers (nadolol or propranolol), which reduce the heart's response to adrenaline and have been shown to be very effective in preventing events, especially in LQT1. If the risk remains high or the patient has survived cardiac arrest, an implantable cardioverter-defibrillator (ICD) is implanted. In refractory cases, left cardiac sympathetic denervation may be considered.
711.technical_description:
The cause of long QT syndrome is the occurrence of pathogenic mutations in various genes. Of these, the most frequent are the KCNQ1 and KCNH2 genes, responsible for 35% and 30% of identified cases, respectively.

In the vast majority of cases, the pathogenic variants are loss-of-function, meaning either fewer channels are produced, or those that are produced do not function correctly. Founder variants have been described in both genes, being more frequent in certain regions, such as p.Gly589Asp (rs120074190), particularly prevalent in Finland.

Specifically, in the KCNQ1 gene, there are hot spots, such as the transmembrane region, where mutations are somewhat more common and more severe. In this regard, the variant p.Arg518Ter (rs17215500), which introduces a premature stop codon, has been reported in multiple unrelated families.

712.name: Long QT Syndrome Types 14, 15, and 16
712.description: Long QT syndrome (LQTS) types 14, 15, and 16 is an extremely rare and severe inherited cardiac channelopathy, caused by mutations in the CALM1, CALM2, or CALM3 genes. These mutations alter the function of calmodulin, a protein vital for calcium regulation in heart cells, leading to life-threatening ventricular arrhythmias from a very young age.
712.overview:
Long QT syndrome (LQTS) is a disorder of the heart's electrical system that can cause rapid, chaotic arrhythmias (torsades de pointes), leading to syncope, seizures, or sudden death. Types 14, 15, and 16 are particularly severe and very early-onset forms, known collectively as calmodulinopathies. They are caused by mutations in the CALM1, CALM2, or CALM3 genes. These three genes are almost identical and produce the same protein: calmodulin. Calmodulin is a fundamental calcium sensor in the body, regulating the activity of multiple targets, including the ion channels that control cardiac action potential. Mutations in any of these genes alter calmodulin's ability to manage the flow of calcium, sodium, and potassium ions, severely destabilizing heart rhythm.

These forms of LQTS are exceptionally rare, representing a very small fraction of all LQTS cases, with only a few dozen families described in the global medical literature. The inheritance pattern is autosomal dominant, meaning that a single copy of the altered gene is sufficient to cause the disease. Most reported cases are de novo, meaning the mutation appears for the first time in the individual with no family history. Due to their extreme rarity, no predisposition by sex or specific population has been identified.
712.symptoms: The symptoms of calpa-thiopathies are notably severe and manifest at a very early age, often in the neonatal period or in the first months of life. The most dramatic clinical presentation includes recurrent sudden cardiac arrests, unexplained syncope (fainting), and seizures caused by lack of oxygen to the brain during an arrhythmia. The electrocardiogram (ECG) shows a very marked prolongation of the QT interval (generally >600 ms), along with other abnormalities such as catecholaminergic polymorphic ventricular tachycardia (CPVT) or idiopathic ventricular fibrillation.
712.prevention: Management is complex and requires an aggressive approach due to the high risk of sudden death. First-line treatment usually includes beta-blockers (such as propranolol or nadolol) in high doses to reduce the cardiac response to adrenaline. Frequently, this is not sufficient, and other antiarrhythmic drugs such as mexiletine or flecainide are added. Most patients require the implantation of an implantable cardioverter-defibrillator (ICD) to treat life-threatening arrhythmias. In refractory cases, left cardiac sympathetic denervation may be considered.
712.technical_description:
Long QT syndrome is a cardiac disorder that can be caused by various genes, with KCNQ1 being the most frequent. However, there are other minor ones that can cause severe forms of the disease. 

Specifically, types 14, 15, and 16 are caused by the presence of mutations in the CALM1 (chromosome 14), CALM2 (chromosome 2), and CALM3 (chromosome 19) genes. These three genes produce an identical protein, calmodulin. Calmodulin is one of the most important and evolutionarily conserved proteins in eukaryotic cells, its main function being to act as a sensor and transducer of intracellular Ca2+ signals. The presence of three genes on different chromosomes ensures its continuous production and balance. The occurrence of a mutation in any of these three genes alters the balance, potentially causing electrical destabilization of the heart. 

Despite being a hereditary disease, most cases of calmodulinopathies are caused by de novo mutations. The genetic basis at the molecular level is also very peculiar, as almost all described mutations are missense, where one amino acid is substituted for a different one. The biological effect of these mutations is complex, with cases of loss of function and others of gain, where calmodulin binds with too much affinity for calcium. In both cases, the normal heart rhythm is altered. 

Mutations in the CALM1, CALM2, and CALM3 genes are also related to catecholaminergic polymorphic ventricular tachycardia, a pathology that causes arrhythmias or abnormal heart rhythms, especially during exercise or in moments of stress.

roffen AJ, Bikker H, Christiaans I. Long QT Syndrome Overview. 2003 Feb 20 [Updated 2024 Mar 21]. In: Adam MP, Feldman J, Mirzaa GM, et al., editors. GeneReviews® [Internet].

713.name: Catecholaminergic Polymorphic Ventricular Tachycardia (CASQ2 gene)
713.description: Catecholaminergic Polymorphic Ventricular Tachycardia (CPVT) associated with the CASQ2 gene is an inherited arrhythmic disorder caused by a deficiency in the protein that stores calcium in heart cells. This condition, of autosomal recessive inheritance, causes potentially life-threatening arrhythmias during physical or emotional stress, with a high risk of sudden death in young people.
713.overview:
CPVT is an electrical heart disease that manifests in individuals with a normal heart structure. Mutations in the CASQ2 gene are responsible for one of the recessive forms of this pathology. This gene contains the instructions for producing Calsequestrin 2, the main protein responsible for safely binding and storing calcium within the sarcoplasmic reticulum (the heart's calcium reservoir). When both copies of the CASQ2 gene are mutated, a defective or absent protein is produced, drastically reducing the cell's ability to store calcium. This generates overload and instability which, under adrenergic stress (exercise, emotions), causes calcium leakage through the RyR2 channel, triggering severe arrhythmias.

With a global incidence for CPVT of approximately 1 in 10,000 people, mutations in CASQ2 account for between 5% and 10% of cases. As an autosomal recessive disease, a person must inherit a defective copy of the gene from each parent to manifest the disease. The condition affects all populations, although some founder mutations have been described in specific groups. Due to the severity of the symptoms and the risk of sudden death as the first manifestation, genetic diagnosis is key to confirming the cause, establishing the inheritance pattern, and guiding the study of family members.
713.symptoms: Symptoms are typically triggered by exercise or emotional stress and usually appear in childhood, often at an earlier age than other forms of CPVT. Manifestations include syncope (fainting), dizziness, and effort-induced palpitations. In some cases, lack of cerebral perfusion during an arrhythmia can lead to seizures, resulting in a misdiagnosis of epilepsy. Sudden cardiac arrest can be, in a significant percentage of cases, the first and only symptom of the disease if it has not been previously diagnosed.
713.prevention: Treatment is fundamental to prevent cardiac events and is based on strict limitation of competitive physical activity and intense exercise. The first-line pharmacological therapy is beta-blockers (such as nadolol) at the highest dose the patient tolerates. The antiarrhythmic drug flecainide is often added for greater control. In patients who have survived cardiac arrest or who continue to experience syncope despite treatment, the implantation of an Implantable Cardioverter-Defibrillator (ICD) is indicated. Left cardiac sympathetic denervation is a surgical option for refractory cases.
713.technical_description:
CPVT is characterized by the appearance of syncope during intense physical exercise or acute emotion. The cause is the presence of mutations in genes involved in the regulation of calcium concentrations within cardiac muscle cells. 

Among the genes associated with the pathology, CASQ2 is responsible for one of the recessive forms of the condition. This gene encodes calsequestrin 2, a cardiac muscle protein that plays a crucial role in the storage and transport of calcium ions. 

Pathogenic variants described to date cause an unstable, absent, or non-functional protein to be generated. They are responsible for 5% of CPVT cases. Most affected families carry their own exclusive variants, but there are some of special interest that are somewhat more frequent. One of the most studied is p.Arg33Gln (rs749547712), reported in various populations, but more frequent in people of Finnish origin, suggesting a possible founder effect. For a correct diagnosis, complete sequencing of the gene is essential, as most patients will carry rare or unique variants in their family, the pathogenicity of which must be carefully evaluated.

Napolitano C, Mazzanti A, Bloise R, et al. Catecholaminergic Polymorphic Ventricular Tachycardia. 2004 Oct 14 [Updated 2022 Jun 23]. In: Adam MP, Feldman J, Mirzaa GM, et al., editors. GeneReviews® [Internet].

714.name: Catecholaminergic Polymorphic Ventricular Tachycardia (RYR2 gene)
714.description: Catecholaminergic Polymorphic Ventricular Tachycardia (CPVT) is a hereditary cardiac channelopathy primarily caused by variants in the RYR2 gene. This condition causes dangerous ventricular arrhythmias in response to physical or emotional stress, with a significant risk of sudden death in young people.
714.overview:
The RYR2 gene contains instructions for making the cardiac ryanodine receptor, a calcium channel crucial for heart contraction. Pathogenic variants in this gene cause uncontrolled calcium release within heart cells during times of adrenergic stimulation (exercise or intense emotions), which destabilizes the heart's electrical activity and can trigger life-threatening ventricular arrhythmias. This form of the disease, known as CPVT type 1, is inherited in an autosomal dominant manner, meaning that inheriting just one copy of the altered gene is sufficient to develop the condition.

CPVT is a rare disease, with an estimated incidence of 1 in 10,000 people. Variants in RYR2 are the most common cause, accounting for approximately 60% of all cases. It affects men and women of all ethnic and geographic groups worldwide equally, and it accounts for one of the most significant causes of sudden cardiac death in children and young adults who do not have visible structural heart disease.
714.symptoms: Symptoms of ARVD typically appear in childhood or adolescence, typically between the ages of 7 and 12. The most common manifestation is syncope (loss of consciousness) which occurs during physical exercise or in response to acute emotional stress. Occasionally, these episodes can be confused with epileptic seizures. Without treatment, up to 30-50% of affected individuals will suffer cardiac arrest before the age of 40.
714.prevention: The main objective of treatment is to prevent fatal arrhythmias. The first line of defense consists of restricting competitive physical activity and the use of beta-blocker drugs, with nadolol being the drug of choice. If symptoms persist, flecainide can be added. In high-risk patients or those who have survived cardiac arrest, the implantation of an implantable cardioverter-defibrillator (ICD) and/or left cardiac sympathetic denervation is considered.
714.technical_description:
Catecholaminergic Polymorphic Ventricular Tachycardia is characterized by the onset of syncope during intense physical exercise or acute emotion. The cause is the presence of mutations in genes involved in the regulation of calcium concentrations inside cardiac muscle cells.

In most cases (60-70%), the disease is due to the presence of pathogenic variants in the RYR2 gene, which encodes the ryanodine receptor 2, crucial for calcium release in cardiac cells. To date, more than 150 mutations have been described, inherited in an autosomal dominant manner, with an average penetrance of 83%, making it common to find a family history of cardiac syncope in affected individuals. Although not entirely clear, the frequency of pathogenic *de novo* mutations is estimated to be around 30%. These pathogenic variants cause abnormal channel activation (especially after catecholamine release), increasing cytosolic calcium concentration and creating an electrically unstable substrate.

The vast majority of variants are missense type, resulting in the substitution of one amino acid for another. Examples include p.Arg4497Cys (rs121918600), affecting the C-terminal region of the protein, or p.Arg176Gln (rs794728708), one of the most studied variants in the N-terminal region.

715.name: Catecholaminergic Polymorphic Ventricular Tachycardia (TRDN gene)
715.description: Catecholaminergic Polymorphic Ventricular Tachycardia (CPVT) is an inherited cardiac channelopathy in which mutations in the TRDN gene cause abnormal calcium handling in heart cells. This condition, with autosomal recessive inheritance, causes severe ventricular arrhythmias during physical or emotional stress, leading to a high risk of syncope and sudden death.
715.overview:
ARVC is a heart rhythm disorder that manifests in individuals with a structurally normal heart. Mutations in the TRDN gene, which encodes the triadin protein, are one of its genetic causes. Triadin is essential for stabilizing the sarcoplasmic reticulum calcium release complex in cardiac myocytes. When both copies of the TRDN gene are mutated (autosomal recessive inheritance), this complex becomes unstable, causing calcium "leaks" during catecholamine (such as adrenaline) stimulation. This uncontrolled release of calcium generates electrical instability that triggers the dangerous ventricular arrhythmias that define the disease.

The global incidence of ARVC is estimated at 1 in 10,000 people, being a major cause of sudden death in young people. The form associated with the TRDN gene is considerably rarer than that caused by the RYR2 gene (the most common and dominant inheritance). The disease affects all populations, regardless of ethnicity or sex. Since the heart appears normal on routine scans such as echocardiography, and resting electrocardiogram is usually normal, the risk can go unnoticed. Therefore, genetic testing is a crucial tool for confirming the diagnosis, guiding treatment, and studying at-risk family members.
715.symptoms: The symptoms are characteristically triggered by physical exertion or acute emotional stress and usually appear during childhood or adolescence (between 7 and 12 years of age). The most common manifestations include syncope (loss of consciousness) and intense dizziness. On occasion, lack of oxygen to the brain during an arrhythmia can cause convulsive crises, which may lead to a misdiagnosis of epilepsy. Tragically, the first manifestation of the disease can be sudden cardiac arrest.
715.prevention: The mainstay of treatment is the prevention of arrhythmias. This includes strict restriction of competitive sports and intense physical exercise. The first-line pharmacological treatment is beta-blockers (preferably non-selective ones like nadolol) at the maximum tolerated dose. Flecainide is frequently added if beta-blockers are insufficient. In high-risk patients—those who have survived cardiac arrest or continue to experience syncope—the implantation of an implantable cardioverter-defibrillator (ICD) is recommended to treat potentially life-threatening arrhythmias.
715.technical_description:
Catecholaminergic Polymorphic Ventricular Tachycardia is characterized by the occurrence of syncope during intense exercise or acute emotion. Various genes with autosomal dominant inheritance have been associated, such as RYR2 (most cases), CALM1, CALM2, CALM3, and KCNJ2 (not analyzed here) and others, such as TRDN, which follows an autosomal recessive inheritance pattern. 

To date, more than 40 variants in the TRDN gene associated with the disease have been described, with most being reading frame or splicing alterations, which generate a truncated non-functional protein. Most variants have been described in both homozygosis and compound heterozygosis. One of the most cited mutations is p.Asp18fs (rs768049331) in which 4 nucleotides are deleted, altering the reading frame and introducing a premature stop codon.

Napolitano C, Mazzanti A, Bloise R, et al. Catecholaminergic Polymorphic Ventricular Tachycardia. 2004 Oct 14 [Updated 2022 Jun 23]. In: Adam MP, Feldman J, Mirzaa GM, et al., editors. GeneReviews® [Internet].

716.name: Cerebrotendinous Xanthomatosis
716.description: Cerebrotendinous xanthomatosis (CTX) is an inherited genetic disorder of lipid metabolism, caused by mutations in the CYP27A1 gene. This alteration leads to the accumulation of a substance called cholestanol in multiple body tissues, resulting in a wide range of neurological and non-neurological symptoms that progress over time.
716.overview:
Cerebrotendinous Xanthomatosis is a lipid storage disease that is inherited in an autosomal recessive manner. The pathology originates from pathogenic variants in the CYP27A1 gene, which encodes the mitochondrial enzyme sterol 27-hydroxylase. The deficiency of this enzyme is crucial in the synthesis of bile acids, which disrupts normal cholesterol metabolism and leads to excessive production and accumulation of cholestanol in the lens, central nervous system, and tendons, causing progressive damage.

Although it is a rare disease, its estimated prevalence varies considerably between populations, with figures ranging from 1 in 36,000 to 1 in 460,000 people worldwide. A higher frequency has been observed in specific populations, such as the Jewish community of Moroccan origin. However, cases have been described in people of very diverse ethnic backgrounds, suggesting that it is an underdiagnosed pathology globally due to its clinical heterogeneity and its overlap with other more common conditions.
716.symptoms: The symptoms of XCT vary widely and their onset is progressive. The first manifestations are usually chronic diarrhea of unknown cause during childhood and the development of bilateral cataracts before the age of 20. During adolescence and the second decade of life, the appearance of xanthomas (fat deposits) on the tendons, especially on the Achilles tendon, is characteristic. In adulthood, progressive neurological deterioration develops, including ataxia (coordination problems), spasticity, cognitive decline, dementia, and psychiatric disorders.
716.prevention: The mainstay of treatment for XCT is chenodeoxycholic acid (CDCA) replacement therapy. This lifelong treatment helps normalize bile acid production, reduces blood and tissue cholestanol levels, and can halt disease progression and even reverse some symptoms. Early diagnosis and treatment initiation are critical to prevent irreversible neurological damage, which responds very poorly once established. Management may also include cataract surgery and symptomatic treatments for neurological complications.
716.technical_description:
Cerebrotendinous xanthomatosis is caused by the presence of mutations in the CYP27A1 gene, which encodes the enzyme sterol 27-hydroxylase, involved in cholesterol metabolism by forming chenodeoxycholic acid.

To date, more than 100 pathogenic variants of different natures have been described in the CYP27A1 gene, present in affected individuals in both homozygosis and compound heterozygosis. The majority of described mutations are located in exons 6-8 of the gene and, of all those identified, 22% are nonsense variants, 20% affect splicing, and 18% are small insertions or deletions. One of the most common variants is p.Arg395Ser (rs121908096), which introduces a non-conservative amino acid change that affects the protein's structure and function.
